title: Solid State and Materials Chemistry I
description: The aim of this course is to introduce the chemistry and physics of extended solids focusing on bonding, characterization, and physical properties (electrical, magnetic, thermal, and optical properties).

title: Solid State and Materials Chemistry II
description: This course will focus on the fundamental physical properties of extended solids with a focus on their thermal, electrical, magnetic, and optical behaviors. Emphasis is placed on understanding the relationship between the structure of materials and their properties with applications in modern technology. The objective is to develop a comprehensive understanding of these properties and relate them to the atomic and electronic structures of materials.

title: Special Topics in Materials Chemistry
description: Topics in materials science chemistry that are not covered in other graduate chemistry courses. May be repeated for credit if topic is different.

title: Principles of Biochemistry
description: In addition to concurrent enrollment in the Medical Sciences M.S. degree program. Online biochemistry course for students in the Medical Sciences Master's degree program. Foundational principles of molecular structure and function are followed by in-depth study of biomolecules, enzymatic processes, and metabolic pathways.

title: Advanced Special Topics in Chemistry
description: Topics in chemistry that are not covered in other graduate chemistry courses. May be repeated for credit if topic is different.

title: Graduate Research
description: Required of all graduate students. For research credit prior to admission to candidacy for an advanced degree. Credit will be given for the amount of work done. May be repeated for credit through 45 hours.

title: Thesis
description: Credit for the amount of work done. In no case will fewer than six semester hours be accepted for a thesis. Required of all master's students.

title: Dissertation
description: Required of all doctoral candidates. In no case will fewer than twelve semester hours be accepted for a dissertation.

title: The Archaeology of Sicily and Southern Italy
description: Study of monuments and topographical archaeology of southern Italy and Sicily. Readings include primary sources and analyses of excavated material.

title: Special Topics in Greek and Roman Art and Archaeology
description: See ARTH 4368 for course information.

title: Greek and Roman Sport and Spectacle
description: See ARTH 4369 for course information.

title: Topics in Classical Literature
description: Various texts to be read (in translation) are selected to meet the needs of the student. With content changed, this course may be repeated up to a total of six semester hours.

title: Proseminar in Classics
description: Introduction to the history, tools and resources, and main methods of research of the discipline of classics. In addition, the course provides an overview of the major subdisciplines of classical studies such as ancient history, epigraphy, papyrology, archaeology, and numismatics.

title: Topics in Ancient History
description: Specific topics in the history of ancient Greece and Rome and related fields with attention to the methodologies of ancient historical inquiry.  May be taken five times, provided topics change.

title: Final Project
description: Supervised research for final project.

title: Thesis
description: Supervised research for master’s thesis.

title: Seminar in American Studies
description: Studies the theory and practice of American Studies, presents students with the opportunity to analyze written and visual texts, and requires a major paper. Through written work and oral presentations, the course gives students a broad perspective on the American culture.

title: Bibliography and Research Methods
description: Practical introduction to the nature of printing and transmission of written material; a guide to the use of the libraries for graduate-level research; approaches to purposes for graduate studies. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Literary Criticism: Seminar
description: Issues in critical theory from Plato to the present with particular attention given to current practice and trends in literary analysis. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Independent Study in Literature
description: Research or reading project undertaken by an individual student working under the direction of a professor. Project to concern literary topics beyond what is included in the defined seminars. Prospectus to be approved by the director of graduate studies in English. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Research Methods in Mass Communication
description: Intensive study of and practice in research methods used in the study of mass communication, including content analysis, survey research, experimental designs, historical and qualitative methods. Classic and current research in mass communication will be reviewed.

title: Foundations of the American Economy
description: Behavior of households and business firms in determining the allocation of scarce resources among competing needs in a free market economy for those with no or little previous training in economics. Basic economic analysis is introduced as an aid in understanding the problems of energy, consumerism, unemployment, inflation, and pollution, among others. The course is designed to meet the requirements of Texas state law mandating instruction in free enterprise and economics education.

title: Basic American Documents
description: The development of the American political, economic, and social system by reference to the basic documents which undergird that structure. Emphasis is placed on understanding how the system developed and how that development reflected the thoughts of the American public as reflected in those documents. Particular emphasis is placed on the Constitution, court interpretation, and landmark Federal legislation.

title: Theory of Mass Communication
description: To study the origins of, evidence for, and applications of various mass communication theories; to study the logic, problems and techniques of theory building; to study the societal implications of mass media research; to extend theoretical assumptions.

title: American Political Development
description: Study of the development and reform of political institutions and practices over the course of American history.

title: Human Growth and Development
description: Processes and stages of human growth and development: physical, social, emotional, and intellectual. The impact of social, political, and economic factors on individuals and families is explored.

title: Psychology of Learning, Cognition, and Affect
description: Philosophical and historical roots of theories of learning, cognition, and affect. Major constructs of current theories and their application in instructional, administrative, counseling, and a variety of organizational settings.

title: Research in Education
description: Historical, descriptive, and experimental inquiry. Emphasis on interpretation of research. Use of references and resources; the problem; expression of hypotheses; research design; organizing the review of literature; gathering data; statistical analysis of data; reporting and discussing findings; drawing conclusions. Writing style will be applied to the student's major field of study.

title: History of American Christianity
description: History of American Christianity from pre-Columbian Christian settlements to the present, with particular emphasis on major movements and problems such as Puritanism, religious liberty, revivalism, westward expansion, the rise and growth of denominations, and post-Civil War trends.  The course may be taken up to three times when content differs.

title: The American Founding
description: Study of the debates on the proper structure, institutional arrangements, and purposes of government during the Founding period focusing on the creation and ratification of the American constitution.

title: Seminar in Mass Communication
description: Research seminar in selected areas of mass communication. May be repeated when topic changes.

title: Seminar in United States History
description: May be taken five times provided topics change.

title: Seminar in Public History
description: Field of public history, with emphasis on practical applications of historical methodology and the work of historians outside academia.

title: Seminar in Oral History
description: Literature and methods of recent United States oral history, with emphasis on the philosophy behind the oral history movement and the personal involvement of the student in the gathering of oral memoirs.

title: Advanced Graduate Research and Writing
description: Seminar for first-year students focusing on historical research skills, independent learning, critical thinking, and effective paper presentations.

title: Contemporary American Literature
description: Poetry, fiction, or drama of a single author or a movement embracing several writers from 1940 to the present. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Early American Literature
description: Poetry or prose of a single author or of a movement or topic embracing several writers of eighteenth-century America. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Nineteenth Century American Literature
description: Poetry or prose of a single author or of a movement or topic embracing several writers of nineteenth-century America. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Modern American Literature
description: Poetry, fiction, or drama of a single author or a movement embracing several writers from 1900-1940. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Seminar in American Educational Thought
description: Understanding the historical, philosophical, and sociological antecedents of current views on education and educational administration is a vital link in the formulation of a philosophy of educational administration. Historical and contemporary works in the general areas of educational history, educational philosophy, sociology of education, and educational administration will be studied.

title: American Studies: Seminar
description: American studies, treating such subjects as literature, history, philosophy, psychology, theology, and education. The course focuses on examining texts as cultural documents. May be repeated one time for credit provided topic is different.

title: Independent Study in Mass Communication
description: See JOU 5V90 for course details.

title: Thesis
description: hrs.

title: Introduction to Bioinformatics and Systems Biology
description: A project-orientated approach to defining, understanding, and applying modern tools for genomic and systems biology analysis. Students will gain proficiency at sequence, microarray, and systems biology annotation by following a biological problem through each step of the analysis process.

title: Advanced Computational Biology
description: See CSI 5330 for course information.

title: Medical Speech-Language Pathology
description: Etiologies, characteristics, diagnosis, and treatment of disorders associated with medical speech pathology.

title: Advanced Clinical Audiology
description: Routine and advanced audiologic measures, including masking and evoked-potential tests. Practical clinical experiences.

title: Diagnostic Methods
description: A study of diagnostic methods used in speech and language pathology, including interviewing, taking case histories, testing, and counseling. Evaluation of the standardization, reliability, and validity of existing tests. Practical application is required.

title: Introduction to Aural Rehabilitation
description: Methods for rehabilitating persons with hearing impairment: evaluating communicative needs, amplification devices, auditory-visual training, and modes of communication for the deaf and hearing impaired.

title: Clinical Methods
description: Methods for treating individuals who have communication disorders. Observation of therapy conducted in the Baylor Speech, Hearing, and Language Clinic is required.

title: Special Problems in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: A conference course providing additional study in communication sciences and disorders. May be repeated once for credit.

title: Introduction to Aural Rehabilitation
description: Methods for rehabilitation persons with hearing impairment: evaluation communicative needs, amplification devices, auditory-visual training, and modes of communication for the deaf and hearing impaired.

title: Leveling - Observation
description: Observation of speech and language therapy, to identify methods for treating individuals who have communication disorders, in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Clinical Practicum in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Practicum in evaluation and treatment of individuals who have communication disorders. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Introduction to Clinical Practice
description: Preparation for clinical experiences in the field that will establish clinical website accounts and review professionalism and clinical writing expectations.

title: Clinical Practicum Placement 1
description: The first practicum placement in the master's program for the evaluation and treatment of individuals with communication disorders. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Clinical Practicum Placement 2
description: The second practicum to be taken in the master's program for the evaluation and treatment of individuals with communication disorders. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Clinical Practicum Placement 3
description: The third practicum to be taken in the master's program for the evaluation and treatment of individuals with communication disorders. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Non-Thesis Degree Completion
description: To fulfill requirements for non-thesis master's students who need to complete final degree requirements other than coursework during their last semester.  This may include such things as a comprehensive examination, oral examination, or foreign language requirement. Students are required to be registered during the semester they graduate.

title: Leveling - Clinical Methods
description: Methods for treating individuals who have communication disorders. Observation of speech and language therapy in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling - Anatomy and Physiology
description: A study of the anatomy and physiology of speech in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling - Speech Science
description: Basic sciences underlying speech and hearing: physics of sound, the decibel, instrumentation, speech production, speech perception, and audition in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Advanced Aural Rehab
description: Hearing aids, cochlear implants, vibrotactile devices, and therapy programs for hearing-impaired persons.

title: Leveling - Survey of Speech-Language Pathology and Audiology
description: An introduction to the nature and causes of speech, language, and hearing disorders and speech language pathology as an educational and clinical field in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling - Language Development
description: Linguistic theory and language development in normal children in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling - Introduction to Phonological Science
description: Introduction to the phonological rules and processes of American English and an examination of descriptive, physiological, and acoustic phonetics in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling-Structures and Functions in Communication and Swallowing
description: Anatomy and physiology of the subsystems that underlie speech and swallowing—neural bases, respiration, phonation, resonance, and articulation as well as speech science in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Leveling - Introduction to Clinical Audiology
description: Hearing sciences and approaches for evaluating hearing: anatomy and physiology of the ear, the decibel, ear pathology, pure-tone audiometry, speech audiometry, and acoustic-immittance audiometry in preparation for graduate coursework.

title: Aphasiology
description: Etiology, symptomatology, and treatment of aphasia and kindred disorders.

title: Fluency and Fluency Disorders
description: Nature, evaluation, treatment, and research findings in stuttering and related fluency disorders (e.g., cluttering).

title: Augmentative and Alternative Communication
description: This course focuses on assessment and intervention practices for individuals who use augmentative and alternative communication (AAC).

title: Voice Pathology and Resonance Disorders
description: Discussion of the etiologies and application of assessment and management principles to organic, functional, and neurological voice disorders and inducible laryngeal obstruction. Application of principles of speech science to the nature, assessment, and management of resonance disorders.

title: Motor Speech Disorders
description: Etiologies, symptoms, classifications, evaluative procedures, and treatments of developmental and adult motor speech disorders.

title: Cleft Lip and Palate
description: Etiologies, classifications, evaluation procedures, and management of communication disorders associated with cleft lip and palate and related orofacial dysmorphologies.

title: Research Methods in Graduate Study in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Research methods used to evaluate evidence-based practices in CSD.

title: EBP Evaluation and Interprofessional Practice in a Diverse Society
description: This course focuses on orienting students to the principles of evidence-based practice (EBP) and evaluations and directs students in utilizing components of the EBP process in making decisions regarding treatment and evaluation.

title: Neurology and Advanced Instrumentation
description: Study of the neuroanatomy and neurophysiology of the mechanisms associated with speech, language, and swallowing, and the instrumentation and latest technological advances used to study speech, language, and swallowing.

title: Adolescent Language and Learning Disabilities
description: A neuropsychological approach to the etiology, classification, diagnosis, and treatments of learning disabled children.

title: Speech Sound Disorders
description: Current research, assessment, and treatment of speech-sound disorders (SSD) including articulation and phonological disorders with functional and organic etiologies.

title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Dysphagia
description: Development of swallowing, etiologies, evaluative procedures, and management of swallowing disorders.

title: Cognitive Linguistic Communication Disorders
description: Neuropathology, symptomology, assessment, and treatment of cognitive linguistic communication disorders associated with right hemisphere damage, traumatic brain injury, and dementia.

title: Traumatic Brain Injury Seminar
description: Familiarizes students with research literature regarding the neuropathology, symptomatology, assessment and treatment of persons having traumatic brain injury.

title: Multicultural Issues in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Relates cultural background to normal development of speech and language. Topics include sound system acquisition, syntax, pragmatics, and professional issues and concerns.

title: Pediatric Language Disorders 2
description: This course is an advanced study of spoken and written language disorders in school-age children.

title: Instrumentation and Advanced Speech Science
description: Principles and techniques of electronics and new technology used in the diagnosis and treatment of pathologies of speech and swallowing, including videostrobolaryngoscopy, digital signal analyses, and flexible fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing.

title: Pediatric Language Disorders 1
description: This course is an advanced study of language disorders in children from birth to five years of age.

title: Speech and Language Neurology
description: Neuroanatomy and neurophysiology as applied to the evaluation of normal and pathological speech and language behaviors.

title: Advanced Medical Speech Pathology
description: Advanced medical diagnostic procedures and treatment techniques associated with speech pathology patients in an advanced medical setting.

title: Mentored Research Experience in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Advanced study and application of research methods in communication sciences and disorders.  Supervised by a faculty member in CSD.

title: Clinical Internship
description: Class meetings in conjunction with a supervised, full-time clinical placement at a therapy site commensurate with the student's graduation requirements and clinical hour competency needs. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Speech-Language Pathology Internship
description: Supervised off-campus experience in speech pathology. Intern placement will be related to students' specialized area of interest. Students must earn a grade of B or better to meet degree requirements.

title: Seminar in Audiology
description: hrs.

title: Problems in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Designed to give students opportunities for additional work in their area of concentration. May be repeated for a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Advanced Clinical Practicum in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Supervised practicum in audiology using speech audiometry; hearing aid selection OR Supervised practicum in speech-language pathology.

title: Seminar in Speech Pathology
description: Published research, theoretical and clinical, in speech and hearing and allied fields.

title: Thesis
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and/or oral defense of an approved master’s thesis.  At least three hours of CSD 5V99 are required for thesis.

title: Mentored Research in CSD I
description: Research apprenticeship in area of expertise in CSD.  Design, implementation, and dissemination of study outcomes.

title: Mentored Research in CSD II
description: In this course doctoral students work with a research mentor in a collaborative area of research. This course may not be repeated for credit.

title: Mentored Teaching in CSD I
description: In this course doctoral students develop knowledge and skills associated with teaching in higher education. Content includes principles of adult learning, course design, equity and inclusion, teaching philosophy, student engagement, and student assessment.

title: Mentored Teaching in CSD II
description: In this course, doctoral students apply the knowledge and skills associated with teaching in higher education that they developed in Mentored Teaching I. Each student works with a mentor to teach an undergraduate or graduate course. This course cannot be repeated for credit.

title: Proseminar in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: This course exposes students to current research and topics in the field. The proseminar provides an opportunity for both students and faculty to present reports of research projects that are in the initial stages of formulation, in progress, or completed.

title: Professional Writing for Doctoral Students
description: Development of technical and scientific writing skills.  Emphasis on critical review and writing of scientific papers.

title: Professional Writing for Doctoral Students II
description: Doctoral students research, revise, and write publications for professional journals. (2 credit hours).

title: Introduction to Doctoral Studies in CSD
description: This course includes critical thinking, problem-solving, and synthesis of topics to introduce students to doctoral studies. The history of science in CSD, Ph.D. shortages, IRB, issues in scientific conduct, mentoring models, preparation for study, and servant leadership as well as launching a successful academic career post-graduation are discussed.

title: Advanced Neuroscience of Speech, Language, Swallowing, and Hearing
description: This course is designed to provide a comprehensive introduction to the neuroscience of speech, language, swallowing, and hearing, incorporating current research findings with current perspectives on assessment and treatment of language disorders. It will cover speech, language, swallowing, and hearing mechanisms in the human brain as well as the various types of research methods used in human neuroscience, and their intended uses.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Communication Sciences and Disorders (CSD)
description: Advanced study of special topics associated with speech, language, hearing, and/or swallowing. The course may be taken three times when content differs.

title: MATLAB® Programming for Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences
description: The course introduces basics of MATLAB® programming and trains students on managing data, plotting data, and graphical user interfaces. The course anticipates and promotes increased reliance on quantification and automation in the field of Speech-Language Pathology and Audiology.

title: Single-Subject Design
description: This course provides a solid foundation in single-subject design (SSD). Specific designs discussed and analyzed include withdrawal, alternating treatments, multiple baseline, and changing criterion designs. By way of contrast, methodologies such as group designs and case studies are also discussed. Students learn to obtain, analyze, interpret, evaluate, and design experimental single-subject studies.

title: Graduate Research
description: For research credit prior to admission to candidacy for an advanced degree. Credit will be given for the amount of work done. May be repeated for credit through 45 hours.

title: Doctoral Independent Study in Advanced Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Focuses on advanced topics in speech, language, hearing, and/or swallowing.   Permission of instructor is required.  This course may be repeated when content differs for a maximum of 12 semester hours.

title: Doctoral Dissertation in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Doctoral students in Communication Sciences and Disorders gain approval of a written dissertation proposal and research, analyze, write, and defend their dissertation. Required (12 credits).

title: Archaeological Theory
description: Use of archaeological data in reconstruction of past human cultural systems, with an emphasis on the role of archaeological theory in the process of interpretation.

title: Anthropological Theory
description: Theoretical approaches to modern-day anthropology, with emphasis on political economy, Marxism, hermeneutics, ecology, and feminism.

title: Societies and Cultures of East Asia
description: Cultural traits and social structures of China, Korea, and Japan in the context of their development from the traditional to the modern. Special attention on Japanese society in comparison with American society.

title: Societies and Cultures of Africa
description: Current social issues and policies in the light of historical and cultural foundations of selected African countries.

title: Culture, Personality and Identity
description: A thorough investigation of the relationship between the individual and culture/society, with emphasis on the "culture and personality" school of contemporary humanistic social science.

title: Climate Anthropology
description: An introduction to the causes and effects of climate change as it relates to people and power, ethics and morals, environmental costs and justice, and cultural and spiritual survival.

title: Medical Anthropology
description: Biological and sociocultural aspects of human health, disease, development, aging, and health care. Especially emphasized are the developmental, holistic, and cross-cultural perspectives on disease and the life cycle.

title: Human Catastrophe and Cultural Response
description: Impact of major catastrophes on human society with emphasis on coping strategies and the utility of disaster theory to help in the recovery process. Issues include disaster, toxic disaster, famine, epidemic, war and natural oppression.

title: Epidemiology
description: Epidemiological concepts and skills pertinent to the understanding of diseases. Assessment of cultural, ecological, environmental, occupational, and behavioral factors.

title: Global Health Disparities
description: Principles of modern medical, biological, and psychological theory are applied to understand how economic and social inequities affect child development and health.

title: Child and Family Health in Global Perspective
description: Principles of modern medical practice and evolutionary biology are used to understand family relationships and how/why they affect child development and health in global context.

title: The Human Fossil Record
description: Seminar on the evolutionary history of humans. Emphasis on fossil evidence and primary texts.

title: Environmental Archaeology
description: Distributional patterns of archaeological sites within specific environments. Archaeological/environmental field work in Texas, with respect to recent conservation laws protecting nonrenewable archaeological resources.

title: Archaeology of the Mediterranean
description: Environmental and cultural factors that led to the rise and fall of civilizations in the Mediterranean region.

title: African Archaeology
description: A survey of the African archaeological record, from emergence of stone tool technology 2.6 million years ago to the rise of complex civilizations and the African Diaspora.

title: Geoarchaeology
description: See GEO 4348 for course information.

title: Futuristics
description: Biological and cultural forces that will likely shape humankind's future. Emphasis on trends in demography, globalization, science, and technology.

title: Archaeology of North America
description: An archaeological survey of human societies in the United States and Canada from their earliest appearance in the New World to the arrival of Europeans. One-third of the course will focus on historical archaeology.

title: Forensic Anthropology
description: Forensic anthropological techniques used in civil and criminal court cases, including analysis of skeletal material for sex, age, stature, and biological affinity.

title: Anthropology of Religion
description: Myth, ritual and religion in social and cultural anthropology. Emphasis on structural and functional analysis, including critiques of pertinent classical and contemporary works.

title: Ethnographic and Analytical Methods in Ethnomusicology
description: See MUS 4360 for course information.

title: Applied Anthropology
description: An introduction to applied anthropology where major research components are identified and specific fields such as medical, nutritional, environmental anthropology, and Third World development are discussed.

title: Primate Behavior
description: The complex social behavior of primates. Includes field trips. Graduate students produce a comprehensive research paper.

title: Seminar in Anthropology
description: Debate of current theoretical issues that reflect the continually changing nature of the discipline. Students will address all sides of a currently debated issue, drawing upon their studies in anthropology and related fields. Faculty participation.

title: Evolutionary Medicine
description: Application of evolutionary theory to medicine using insights from evolutionary theory (biology) and human evolutionary ecology (biological and cultural anthropology) to inform our understandings of human health, development, and disease.

title: Sex, Hormones, and Behavior: Human Reproductive Ecology
description: Recent developments in human reproductive biology, human reproductive ecology, and fertility analysis.  The major features of the human reproductive process are considered using a combination of demographic, physiological and evolutionary approaches.

title: One Health: Connecting Global Health and Conservation Medicine
description: Analysis of the collaborative efforts among physicians, public health professionals, veterinarians, and social scientists to understand infectious disease exchange at the interface of human, wildlife, and livestock populations, and the varying ecological and cultural contexts in which these disease spillovers take place.

title: Human Evolutionary Anatomy
description: Survey of regional and systemic human anatomy viewed from a comparative evolutionary perspective. Non-human primate and non-primate vertebrates will be used to illustrate the unique characteristics of human anatomical structures that have been honed by natural selection throughout our evolutionary history.

title: Field School in Archaeology
description: Field training in archaeological excavation, survey, artifact processing, and analysis of material culture.

title: Field School in Cultural Anthropology
description: Residence for five to six weeks in a selected area to observe and analyze social, economic, and environmental systems.

title: Field School in Biological Anthropology
description: Training in research techniques to gain an understanding of the methodology and its application in field research in various topics related to biological anthropology.

title: Research Methods in Cultural Anthropology
description: Lecture and field experience in the methods and techniques of social and cultural anthropology. May be repeated for a total of six semester hours with different topics.

title: Archaeological Research
description: Independent library and lab research focused on a current topic in archaeology. May be repeated for a total of six semester hours with different topics.

title: Special Topics in Anthropology
description: A reading-research project in selected areas of ethnology, archaeology, or physical anthropology. May be repeated for credit up to a total of six semester hours, provided topic is different.

title: Multicultural Societies
description: Multicultural societies will be examined with respect to cultural histories as well as modern problems. Special attention will be given to the cultural complexity of the continental United States.

title: Advanced World Food Problems
description: This course focuses on chronic deficiencies of certain key ingredients in the diet, their causal factors, the impacts these deficiencies have, and solutions to these problems in the contemporary world. The course explores the consequences of world hunger with particular attention to food and nutritional security as it influences health status, ability to work, education outcomes, economic security, and social connectedness.

title: Descriptive and Exploratory Methods in Anthropology
description: Modern approaches to descriptive, exploratory, and formative anthropological research, with foundational concepts underlying research design as well as core methodologies. Students develop a domestic research project to collect primary data and gain experience in ethnographic methods, including participant observation, mapping, interviewing, survey design, data management and analysis (indexing, coding, transcribing, and related methods).

title: Laboratory Methods in Anthropological Reserach
description: Experience conducting actual research in human evolutionary biology. Students collect data on living humans, perform laboratory analyses, statistical analyses, and manuscript preparation and presentation.  Students gain experience with scientific methodology, hypothesis generation and study design, human subjects committees, biosafety and bioethics, biological sample collection, biomarker assays, survey design, and statistical analyses.

title: Professional Skills and Grant Writing
description: Students learn how granting at the National Science Foundation and National Institutes of Health works, identify research and publication biases, recognize ethical issues in research, distinguish good science from bad attempts at it, identify potential granting opportunities, develop general writing and oral presentation skills, and develop peer reviewing skills.

title: Advanced Human Biological Variation
description: This course examines human biological variation, with a focus on human genetic and phenotypic diversity, adaptation, and health disparities in contemporary global populations. The overall framework for understanding human variation is evolutionary and biocultural. It draws from various scientific disciplines, including anthropology, evolutionary biology, genetics, physiology, nutrition, psychology, and global health.

title: Advanced Human Genetics: Evolution and Health
description: This course covers advanced topics in human genetic and genomic research. We focus on the application of genetic principles to human populations with an emphasis on population history, human evolution, and genes underlying human health and disease. Students are introduced to population genetic statistics, human genomics from an evolutionary standpoint, and standard forms of genome-wide data analysis.

title: Advanced Evolution of Human Behavior
description: An advanced examination of major evolutionary approaches to the study of human behavior, including human reproduction, parenting, cooperation, and warfare.

title: Advanced Climate Anthropology
description: The primary goals of this course are fourfold: 1) To introduce the student to concepts and principles regarding relationships between climate and human activities; 2) To identify and critically assess the impacts on humans of climate-related events and vice versa, of humans on climate; 3) To explore the consequences of climate change via a wide range of case studies over time and space; 4) To examine the future in terms of climate change projections, the law of evolutionary potential, and factors that influence human responses to climate change.

title: Advanced Medical Anthropology
description: Students are taught key concepts in Medical Anthropology to examine how health and wellbeing are socially and culturally constituted in contexts of cultural diversity. We bring key insights from anthropological cross-cultural comparisons to public health and medical practice.

title: Advanced Global Health Ethics
description: Study of social theory that informs historical transformations in the ethics of global public health, including the history of research and practice in international and population health. Topics include equality and equity, access and competition, homogeneity and diversity, legitimacy and power, responsiveness and exploitation, and moral reasoning and justice, among others.

title: Advanced Child and Family Health in Global Perspective
description: Principles of modern medical practice and evolutionary biology are used to understand family relationships and how/why they affect child development and health in global context. We begin with a brief overview of major issues in global child health practice. We then examine these issues from the perspective of developmental biology, psychology, and evolutionary medicine.

title: Advanced Global Health Policy
description: Critique of existing domestic and international policy goals that include efforts to improve global health. Special attention (via analyses of case-studies) is given to the ethical and legal principles pertaining to global health policies.

title: Advanced One Health: Connecting Global Health and Conservation Medicine
description: One Health represents the concept that the health of humans, wildlife, domestic animals, and ecosystems are all codependent upon one another.  The general framework is based on the concept of consilience, or the convergence of evidence, in this case the collaborative efforts of physicians, public health professionals, veterinarians, social scientists, and others.  This transdisciplinary science, sometimes referred to as “ecosystem health” (or EcoHealth), implies an inclusive study of the broad determinants and indicators of health across species and biomes, which is particularly relevant in the current Anthropocene epoch in which humans are modifying the global environment in complex and accelerated ways.  The application of this approach to the explicit management/maintenance of protected species and habitats, particularly in the face of global environmental change, and with an understanding of the role of biodiversity in maintaining ecosystem health, has been referred to as “conservation medicine.”  Whatever term people choose to use, it is clear that connections among all species are complex, and understanding the health outcomes of these connections will continue to be hindered by a traditional, compartmentalized approach to health-related research and practices.  Understanding infectious disease exchange at the interface of human, wildlife, and livestock populations, and the varying ecological and cultural contexts in which this takes place, represents a major challenge requiring committed collaborations that also transcend international borders.  To this end, students will be trained on the fundamental topics and methods within One Health, ultimately to understand the complexities involved in global disease prevention and control.

title: Special Problems in Anthropology
description: Advanced work in Anthropology on variable topics. Subject and hours of credit agreed upon by student and instructor prior to registration. May be taken more than once provided the content differs substantially from that of any prior offering of the course that the student has taken.

title: Research
description: Supervised research for doctoral students developing a dissertation proposal and studying for the preliminary examination required for advancement to candidacy. A student may repeat this course for credit with a maximum of twelve total hours.

title: Internship
description: Provides graduate students opportunity for internship work experience in research positions with consent of advisory committee.

title: Dissertation
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and defense of an approved doctoral dissertation project. Student must have been admitted to candidacy before registering for dissertation hours.

title: Data Communications
description: Fundamentals of computer networking including data transmission, communication software, protocols, simple networks and internetworking.

title: Numerical Analysis
description: Numerical evaluation of derivatives and integrals, solution of algebraic and differential equations, and approximation theory.

title: Numerical Linear Algebra
description: See MTH 4328 for course information.

title: Database Design I
description: Concepts for current relational database design and implementation, including SQL, ER diagrams, normalization, JDBC, XML and DBMS components. Semester project designing a relational database.

title: Introduction to Operating Systems
description: Operating system design and implementation. Topics include process control and synchronization, memory management, processor scheduling, file systems, and security. Course projects implement parts of an operating system.

title: Computer Graphics
description: Introduction to graphic representation and display of information and objects by computer. Topics include hardware display technology and algorithms for two-dimensional and three-dimensional graphics. A current graphic system model will be used for programming assignments.

title: Object-Oriented Development
description: Object-oriented analysis and design methods. Group software projects.

title: Introduction to Data Mining
description: Introduction to the concepts, techniques, and applications of data warehousing and data mining. Topics include design and implementation of data warehouse and OLAP operations; data mining concepts and methods such as association rule mining, pattern mining, classification, and clustering; applications of data mining techniques to complex types of data in various fields.

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Research presentations by the graduate faculty, outside speakers, and select advanced graduate students. Attendance at various functions is also required.

title: Non-Thesis Degree Completion
description: To fulfill requirements for non-thesis master's students who need to complete final degree requirements other than coursework during their last semester.  This may include such things as a comprehensive examination, oral examination, or foreign language requirement. Students are required to be registered during the semester they graduate.

title: Foundations of Algorithms
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to computer algorithms taken from diverse areas of application. The course concentrates on algorithms of fundamental importance and on analyzing the efficiency of these algorithms.

title: Foundations of Database
description: The course covers current relational database design concepts including ER diagrams, database access techniques such as SQL, database issues including performance and security, and web-database applications.

title: Foundations of Software Engineering
description: Fundamentals of software engineering; software development processes, requirements analysis, modular design, design patterns, software testing and evolution, configuration management, and implementation of software systems. A small project to illustrate and extend concepts from lectures.

title: Foundations of Data Communications
description: Introduction to the the fundamentals of computer networking, including communication issues/solutions at various layers, socket programming, and internet protocols.

title: Foundations of Operating Systems
description: Online only.  Operating system design and implementation. Topics include process control and synchronization, memory management, processor scheduling, file systems, and security. Course projects implement parts of an operating system.

title: Foundations of Mathematics for Computer Science
description: A survey of mathematical topics for computer scientists.  An introduction to differential and integral calculus, matrices, proof techniques, and statistics.

title: Artificial Intelligence Programming Essentials
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to AI programming, covering essential theories, techniques, and tools used in modern AI development. Students will explore key topics such as dimension reduction, manifold learning, high-dimensional data processing, ensemble learning, deep reinforcement learning, and AI hardware acceleration using GPU/TPU, CUDA, and FPGA. The course emphasizes hands-on programming, enabling students to apply AI techniques to real-world problems, enhance their computational skills, and prepare for careers in AI and data science. Through projects, exams, and interactive discussions, students will develop a strong foundation in AI programming and professional communication within the field.

title: Introduction to Computation Theory
description: Several models of computation (including finite automata, pushdown automata, and Turing machines) and their related languages. Topics include closure properties, regular languages, context-free languages, decidability and recognizability, and time and space complexity (including NP-completeness and randomized complexity).

title: Ethics of Artificial Intelligence and Responsible Artificial Intelligence
description: This course explores the ethical, societal, legal, and theological implications of artificial intelligence (AI). Students will engage critically with topics including algorithmic fairness, data privacy, model transparency, bias, accountability, and the societal impacts of automation. A distinctive element of this course is the integration of Christian ethical principles—including human dignity, justice, stewardship, compassion, and moral responsibility—to guide the development and deployment of AI systems that promote human flourishing. Through case studies, readings, reflections, and project-based inquiry, students will develop frameworks for responsible AI practice consistent with Baylor’s mission of educating leaders committed to service, integrity, and the common good.

title: Advanced Data Communications
description: Fundamental principles of computer networking, including data transmission, layering, protocols, simple networks, and internetworking. Projects include applied research in contemporary networking problems.

title: Software Engineering
description: Methods for developing and maintaining software systems; system software life cycle, requirements elicitation, specification and design methods, planning, maintenance, configuration management, documentation and coding standards, cost estimation, metrics and quality attributes; class project.

title: Introduction to Machine Learning
description: An introduction to foundational topics in machine learning, including supervised and unsupervised learning, linear and non-linear models, regression and classification, model assessment and regularization, and the algorithmic, mathematical, and statistical bases for learning from data.

title: Advanced Machine Learning
description: This course provides a comprehensive and in-depth exploration of advanced topics in machine learning, emphasizing probabilistic approaches, mathematical rigor, and practical applications. It covers topics such as parameter estimation, optimization techniques, graphical models, graph-based learning, reinforcement learning, and modern deep learning architectures. Through theoretical study, algorithmic development, and hands-on projects, students will gain the skills necessary to tackle complex challenges in modern machine learning.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: This course introduces the core principles and methods of artificial intelligence, including maximum likelihood estimation, numerical optimization, artificial neural networks, convolutional neural networks, deep reinforcement learning, graphical models, transformers, and large language models. Students will learn both the theoretical foundations and practical techniques for designing, implementing, and evaluating intelligent systems in real-world settings.

title: Advanced Computational Biology
description: Advanced course of computational methods for understanding biological systems. Topics include string matching, suffix tree analysis, sequence alignment, and other graph theoretic algorithms for gene mapping and sequencing, phylogenetic inference, and biological network analysis.

title: Advanced Database
description: A continuation of database system implementations to include object-oriented and knowledge-based systems. Additional topics covered are physical-data organization, database integrity, security, transaction management, and distributed database management.

title: Data Models
description: Conceptual and abstract parts of databases. Topics include commonly used data models (hierarchical, network, relational, semantic network and infological) and the use of data models for database design and operation.

title: Advanced Operating Systems
description: Advanced topics in operating systems including queuing models, performance measurement and evaluation, security and protection, and design issues involved in operating system design.

title: Advanced Computer Organization
description: Advanced topics in computer systems organization, including techniques used in large-scale computer systems, parallel and pipeline architectures, stack machines, and other non-von Neumann architectures.

title: Software Verification and Validation
description: Advanced topics in software engineering research, including techniques used in software verification and validation with a particular focus on software specification and testing.

title: Introduction to Human Computer Interaction
description: Introduction to Human Computer Interaction is a research seminar designed to explore the issues of design, organization, implementation, communication, training, and management which confront humans as users of computer environments.

title: Analytic Models
description: Computer modeling of a variety of systems. Topics include selections from: linear programming, network analysis, queuing theory, game theory, and statistical methods and models.

title: Parallel Systems
description: Description and evaluation of parallel hardware and software. Distributed-memory versus shared-memory. Design and implementation of parallel programs using parallel hardware and software.

title: Design Automation
description: This course is about automating the design of Very Large Scale Integrated circuits. The curriculum covers compiled and event driven simulation algorithms, differential simulation techniques, current literature in electronic simulation, channel routing algorithms, Lee routers, partitioning, current literature in placement and routing, synthesis algorithms, and current literature in logic and circuit synthesis.

title: Distributed Systems
description: Design and implementation of distributed systems with up-to-date software architecture and relevant development frameworks. Topics include inter-module communication, asynchronous processing, security, concurrency, parallelism, and an overview of contemporary enterprise technology and challenges.

title: Advanced Algorithms
description: Advanced data structures, algorithm design, and analysis. Topics include common data structures, algorithms, implementation, classes of algorithms, algorithm analysis, computational tradeoffs, and adaptation of familiar algorithms to new problems.

title: Data Visualization
description: An in-depth exploration of the techniques and algorithms for creating effective visualizations based on principles from graphic design, visual art, psychology, and cognitive sciences. Explores how to better understand data, present clear findings, and tell engaging data stories.

title: Advanced Object-Oriented Development
description: Object-oriented design and development with best practices in solving recurring engineering problems. Topics include core object-oriented concepts, such as composition, inheritance, polymorphism, and templates; an overview of design pattern-based problem solving and design practices; and advanced design patterns applicable for enterprise solution development.

title: Multimedia Systems
description: System support for end-to-end multimedia pipelines. Topics include sensing, representation, compression, packaging, streaming protocols, and emerging paradigms.

title: Advanced Software Engineering
description: Advanced topics in software engineering research, including techniques used in the modeling and analysis of complex systems.

title: Data Mining and Analysis
description: Advanced topics in Data Mining are presented. These include the pattern analysis of numerical, categorical, time, and textual data. The course focuses on algorithms for clustering and predictive modeling with special attention to extracting useful information from raw data, and methods for data validation.

title: Cloud Computing
description: Programming and data storage with cloud architectures. Topics include the Apache Hadoop Ecosystem and related programming frameworks.

title: Applied Data Science
description: This course surveys practical areas of data science using an applicationbased approach. Additionally, students are introduced to new content and coding paradigms for developing more intelligent data processing environments. Students participate in guided projects intended to replicate the integration of scalable computing, integration of very large passive and active high-speed data sets, and new analytic approaches.

title: Information Retrieval & Natural Language Processing
description: Introduce fundamental and advanced algorithms in Information Retrieval and Natural Language Algorithms. Topics include Language Modelling, Retrieval Algorithms and Evaluation, and Language Processing techniques such as tagging, parsing, and lexical semantics. Applications and research topics are also covered.

title: Cybersecurity Concepts
description: Introduction to concepts in cybersecurity, including cryptography; instruction detection/prevention; attacking/defending; cybersecurity tools; malware and reverse engineering; and defensive programming.

title: Advanced Cybersecurity Concepts
description: Advanced topics in cybersecurity, including threat modeling, policy, hardware systems, network/wireless/protocol security, cloud security, risk analysis/management/mitigation, and compliance.

title: Secure Systems, Software Architecture, Development, and Operations
Cybersecurity Analytics
description: Fundamentals of data analytics approaches and applications for cybersecurity; algorithms for analysis of structured and unstructured data; applications of machine learning to anomaly detection in software and system; exploration of automated detection techniques, various attacks, and post-compromise activities.

title: Foundations of Quantum Computing
description: This course introduces the mathematical, physical, and computational foundations of quantum computing. Topics include qubits, superposition, entanglement, quantum gates, measurement, quantum information, and foundational algorithms such as Deutsch–Jozsa, Grover’s, and Shor’s. Overview of quantum hardware platform will also be introduced. Students learn to program quantum computing problems using cloud-based platforms including IBM Quantum Lab and Qiskit. Ethical and societal implications of emerging quantum technologies are integrated throughout.

title: Quantum Artificial Intelligence
description: This course explores quantum-enhanced approaches to machine learning, optimization, and artificial intelligence (AI). Students learn how to design variational quantum circuits, implement quantum kernel methods, quantum classification and regression, train quantum neural networks, quantum reinforcement learning, and construct hybrid quantum–classical AI workflows. Hands-on labs use tools such as PennyLane, Qiskit Machine Learning, Cirq, and cloud-based quantum hardware. The course emphasizes both technical fluency and ethical responsibility, ensuring students understand the societal implications of emerging quantum AI capabilities.

title: Introduction to Large Language Models and Artificial General Intelligence
description: This course focuses on introducing state-of-the-art theory and techniques in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). It aims to foster and enhance students’ AI capabilities by covering both the deep theoretical foundations and the hands-on skills required to build and deploy LLM and AGI systems.

title: Advanced Topics in Human-Computer Interaction
description: This class investigates the “emerging” next generation of user interaction with a focus on the design and evolution of interaction techniques.  Variety of user interaction styles may include gesture, virtual reality, augmented reality, ubiquitous, tangible, lightweight, tacit, passive, affective, perceptual, context-aware, and multi-modal interfaces.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Fintech
description: This course focuses on hands-on state-of-art AI techniques in Fintech. It aims to foster and enhance students’ AI techniques and analytics skills in fintech.

title: Master's Research
description: Concentrated research for the purpose of determining whether the thesis or project option is most appropriate, and for the initial selection of a topic area.

title: Special Topics in Computer Science
description: May be repeated for credit, provided topic is not duplicated, for a maximum of eighteen semester hours total.

title: Internship Experience
description: Provides graduate students opportunity for internship work experience in computer science-related positions with consent of major advisor.

title: Thesis
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and oral defense of an approved master's thesis. At least three hours of CSI 5V99 are required.

title: Advanced Deep Learning
description: This course offers an in-depth exploration of advanced deep learning methodologies, focusing on state-of-the-art neural network architectures such as convolutional, recurrent, attention mechanisms, and auto-regressive generative models. Emphasis is placed on advanced optimization techniques, hyperparameter tuning, and neural architecture search. Students will engage in rigorous theoretical study and practical implementation, addressing applications in computer vision, natural language processing, bioinformatics, and cybersecurity. The course also critically examines the ethical and societal implications of AI technologies. The course integrates theoretical foundations with research projects, preparing students to address complex challenges in AI research and industry.

title: Research Issues in Distributed Computing
description: Seminar course on research issues in distributed computing. Issues include correctness, performance, security, fault tolerance, applications and algorithms. Students will read a collection of research papers on one or more topics and present information related to a topic.

title: Doctoral Prospectus Research
description: Supervised research for developing a dissertation prospectus.  Prepares students for the preliminary exam required for students to advance to candidacy. A student may repeat this course for credit with a maximum of eighteen total hours.  Registration for this course is sufficient for achieving full-time status.

title: Special Topics in Computer Science
description: Special topics in Computer Science.  This course may be taken up to 6 times, on a different topic each time, for up to 18 hours of credit.

title: Dissertation
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and oral defense of an approved doctoral dissertation topic.

title: Organizational Communication
description: Communication within the organization and its relationship to organizational structure, roles, leadership, and management orientations.

title: Communication Training and Development
description: Theory and practice of performing and supervising training activities in an organizational setting. Emphasis on the design, execution, and evaluation of communication training and development programs and strategies.

title: Leadership Communication
description: The intersection between leadership and communication, emphasizing the theory, research, and practice of leadership communication.

title: Advanced Small Group Communication
description: An experiential, travel-based course that invites students to examine complex social, cultural, and organizational issues through a communication-centered lens while engaging directly with global institutions and local communities.

title: Nonprofit Communication
description: Explores communication dynamics and issues in nonprofit work and organizations. Emphasis is placed on stakeholder messaging, engaging communities, and member socialization.

title: Advanced Interviewing
description: Application of survey and probing interview techniques with emphasis on career development.

title: Political Communication
description: The dynamic relationships between political and communication institutions; topics include political discourse, news and information, and portrayal of politics in popular entertainment.

title: Conflict Communication
description: The role of communication in managing conflict in interpersonal, group, organization, and community contexts.

title: Investigating Communication Contexts
description: An experiential, travel-based course that examines advanced and complex types of communication contexts involving relational and group facilitation strategies, systemic interviewing practices, and the development of a basic understanding of systemic inquiry as a communication management strategy.

title: Family Communication
description: Survey of communication issues related to theory and research regarding relationships within the modern family system.

title: Communication Consulting
description: Design and implement a communication assessment of for-profit or non-profit organizations.

title: Health Communication
description: Health communication theory and practice, including patient-provider communication, healthcare organizational communication, and health information technology.

title: Advanced Interpersonal Communication
description: Advanced survey of theory and research regarding communication and personal relationships.

title: Health Narratives
description: Examines pervasive health narratives, including best practices for the communication surrounding illness both from those living with the illness and those in support roles.

title: Communication and New Technology
description: The ways in which communication is facilitated by new technologies with an emphasis on various theories related to computer-mediated communication and new communication technology.

title: Culture, Health, and Communication
description: This course examines health as a communicative process situated within cultural, social, and structural contexts.

title: Contemporary Communication Issues
description: Seminar topics vary each semester. May be repeated once with change in topic.

title: Rhetoric of Women and Gender
description: Analyzes the historical fight for women’s rights and contemporary arguments about gender-based rights through the tools of rhetorical criticism.

title: Criticism of Contemporary Public Address
description: Significant public speeches in contemporary society, with emphasis on applying principles and methods of rhetorical criticism.

title: Corporate Advocacy and Public Policy
description: Influence of contemporary organizations on public attitudes and public policy through analysis of communication campaigns during both favorable and unfavorable conditions.

title: Public Discourse and Foreign Policy
description: An analytical approach to the discourse generated by United States foreign policy in the post-World War II era. Topics covered include the nature of public opinion and foreign policy, rhetorical and political constraints on foreign policy discourse, and in-depth analysis of the arguments for and against the conflict in Vietnam.

title: African American Communication
description: Rhetorical strategies of African Americans, focusing on the historically important documents of oratory, argumentation, homiletic, and narrative.

title: Rhetorics of Race
description: Exploration of ideas about communication, rhetoric, and race. Emphasis on rhetorical criticism as a methodological approach to public discourse and analysis of race as understood in contemporary American culture.

title: Rhetorical Theory
description: Selected theories of persuasion in Western culture from the Greco-Roman period to the present. Topics covered include the relationship of rhetoric and poetic, arguments for a behavioristic approach to rhetoric, and contemporary claims concerning rhetoric as a way of knowing.

title: Visual Rhetoric
description: Theories and methodologies pertaining to visual rhetoric.

title: American Rhetoric
description: Origin and development of rhetoric in American social movements, with emphasis on the characteristics of various types of communication situations and the discovery, analysis, and evaluation of common persuasive strategies.

title: Public Discourse and the Classic Liberal Tradition
description: Analysis of major speeches, pamphlets, and essays in England and America on politics and political change from the early seventeenth century through the American Revolution. Topics addressed include the birth of the public sphere, church and state relations, and natural rights.

title: Workshop in Directing the Speech Program
description: Intended primarily for directors of speech activities in high schools and colleges. May be repeated once for credit.

title: Non-Thesis Degree Completion
description: To fulfill requirements for non-thesis master's students who need to complete final degree requirements other than coursework during their last semester.  This may include such things as a comprehensive examination, oral examination, or foreign language requirement. Students are required to be registered during the semester they graduate.

title: Modern Communication Theory
description: An overview of criticism regarding continuing developments in communication theory.

title: Seminar in Interpersonal Communication
description: An in-depth analysis of interpersonal theory and research.

title: Seminar in Argumentation
description: Advanced theoretical work on the form and function of argumentation. This course explores field theory, examines the utility of argument diagram, and considers approaches to ordinary language argument.

title: Seminar in Rhetoric and and Political Persuasion
description: An analysis of the function of rhetorical discourse in contemporary society and politics.

title: Seminar in Small Group Communication
description: An analysis of small group communication theory and research with a focus on topics such as decision making, leadership, social influence, and interaction analysis.

title: Seminar in Organizational Communication
description: An analysis of organizational communication theory and research.

title: Seminar in Organizational Change and Communication
description: Organizational change is viewed from a communication perspective with special attention placed on the conversational architectures that create sensible and coherent change.

title: Seminar in Rhetoric and the Public Sphere
description: Analysis of major theoretical statements on the changing nature of the public sphere in western democracies and the related implications for the role of argumentation and rhetorical discourse in the formation of public policy.

title: Seminar in Family Communication
description: An advanced examination of scholarly theory, research, and quantitative/qualitative research methods used for academic investigation of topics and issues related to communication within the family.

title: Leadership and Persuasion
description: Explores the interwoven relationship between educational leadership and persuasive communication. By the end of the class students should be able to fashion compelling persuasive messages as well as interpret the attempts at persuasion by others.

title: Organizational Membership and Identification
description: Explores the relationship between communication and one's self-concept as it is defined and shaped by membership in workplaces, civic organizations, churches, clubs, and other social groupings. Discussion and analysis of the processes and practical consequences of organizational identification.

title: Communication and Organizing in Disruptions
description: Examines the theory, research, and communicative processes related to disruptive events in organizations.

title: Seminar in Organizational Rhetoric and Discourse
description: This seminar focuses on how rhetoric and discourse shape organizational life in terms of power, culture, change, identity, and crisis.

title: Rhetoric and Cultural Studies
description: Methods of rhetorical criticism influenced and intersected by cultural studies, beginning with early twentieth century and continuing into present day.

title: Seminar in Presidential Rhetoric
description: See PSC 5350 for course information.

title: Qualitative Research Methods in Communication
description: Methods of qualitative inquiry in the study of communication theories. Emphasis on application of theory and research methods in a variety of communication contexts, e.g., organizational communication, interpersonal communication, technology communication, communication and text.

title: Seminar in Methods of Rhetorical Criticism
description: Quantitative/critical methodology utilized in the analysis of public discourse.

title: Rhetorical Theory
description: Examines the lines of inquiry that guide rhetorical theorizing and lenses that inform the practice of rhetorical criticism from ancient to contemporary usages.

title: Quantitative Research Methods in Communication
description: Introduction to the approaches and procedures used in designing and analyzing communication research studies.

title: Internship in Communication
description: Provides graduate students opportunity for application of communication-related skills and knowledge under the supervision of a professional employer in a corporate organization.

title: Problems in Communication
description: Designed to give individual students opportunities for additional work in their area of concentration. May be repeated in a different semester for a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Seminar in Communication
description: Seminar topics vary each semester. One to three semester hours may be earned in a semester. May be repeated once with change in topic for a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Professional Paper in Communication
description: Satisfies the non-thesis option for the master of communication. Under the direction of a supervising professor, a student will select a problem or topic in communication and will write a substantial paper or produce a substantial project for submission to the faculty. Maximum three hours.

title: Praxis Practicum
description: At least 150 hours of applied learning in a communication-centered role/field. Final project that includes a written and verbal report and draws from scholarly literature, original research, and field experiences.

title: Thesis
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and oral defense of an approved master's thesis.  At least six hours of CSS 5V99 are required.

title: Business Cycles and Forecasting
description: Examines basic causes of fluctuation in business activity. Topics include an examination of exogenous and endogenous influences on Gross National Product and other measures of economic activity, and the relationship between cycles in Gross National Product and cycles in particular industries and companies. The course emphasizes methods and techniques currently in use by business forecasters. Experience in Microsoft Excel is recommended for this course. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Energy Economics
description: Analysis of energy markets, with a focus on static and dynamic efficiency. Oil, natural gas, coal, nuclear, and renewables (including wind, solar, hydro, geothermal, and biofuels). Regulation and deregulation in electricity and natural gas markets. Energy derivatives. Policy issues related to market externalities, including pollution and climate change. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: The Environment and Economic Analysis
description: Economic analysis in description, analysis, and policy formulation of environmental problems such as natural resource development, ecology, energy needs, noise, water, and air pollution. Economic tools used will include social welfare analysis, externalities, and benefit cost analysis.

title: Research Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of current research in economics. Course will be graded pass/fail.

title: Research Seminar
description: Presentation and discussion of current research in economics. Course will be graded pass/fail.

title: Key Global Economic and Strategic Issues
description: This course will enable the participant to see how economic analysis is applied to key global issues that affect management decisions at home and abroad. Questions related to globalization and interdependence among nations will be addressed.

title: Pricing and Economic Analysis
description: Use of economic analysis by managers to help firms acquire and sustain competitive advantage.

title: Economics of Strategic Interaction
description: Use of economic analysis to aid managers in obtaining favorable outcomes in situations involving strategic interaction between and within firms.

title: Managerial Macroeconomics
description: Use of macroeconomic analysis by managers in tactical and strategic planning with reference to long-term macroeconomic trends and short-term business cycle fluctuations; sources of disaggregated data; forecasting.

title: Non-Thesis Degree Completion
description: To fulfill requirements for non-thesis master's students who need to complete final degree requirements other than coursework during their last semester.  This may include such things as a comprehensive examination, oral examination, or foreign language requirement. Students are required to be registered during the semester they graduate.

title: Business Foundations - Economics
description: This course is required for MBA and MSIS students who do not have an undergraduate degree in business from an AACSB-accredited institution.  It provides students with a foundation in economics which is expected of all business graduate students, and helps them to understand the market environment in which businesses operate.

title: Macroeconomic Analysis in the Global Economy
description: This course analyzes national and global economic issues from a macroeconomic perspective.  Topics include economic aggregates, interest rates, the balance of payments and exchange rates, aggregate production, economic growth, unemployment, consumption, investment, macroeconomic dynamics, monetary policy, and fiscal policy.

title: Seminar in Behavioral and Experimental Economics
description: Application of the scientific method to economics using laboratory experimentation to test economic theories about individual and group behavior. Exploration of behavioral concepts to expand economic modeling beyond pure rational choice models.

title: Microeconomic Theory and Business Decisions
description: A seminar designed to cover aspects of micro theory that are relevant for decision making within the firm. Emphasis is placed on the decision-making process. Numerous problems, cases, and examples are used to illustrate the theory.

title: Contemporary Government and Business Relations
description: An examination of the many ways in which government and business interact. Topics covered include the historical development of government regulations of business; the current state of antitrust enforcement; issues involving multinational corporations; the basis for regulated markets and forces favoring deregulation; the impact of consumer protection policies and affirmative action policies on business; and the outlook for government and business relations in the future.

title: Game Theory
description: This course provides a technical treatment of the theory of games and strategic behavior with an emphasis on applications in economics and business.  This framework helps us to understand the nature of competition and cooperation.  We also study contractual arrangements that affect incentives under different information constraints.

title: The Economics of Government
description: Economic rationale and effect of various taxes, user pricing, and the role of government in the allocation of resources, stabilization of the economy, and redistribution of wealth.
