description: Selected topics in Statistics. May be repeated once with change of topic.

title: Practice in Statistics
description: Consulting, research, and teaching in statistics.

title: Topics in Statistics
description: Selected topics in statistics. May involve texts, current literature, or an applied data model analysis. This course may be repeated up to four times with change of topic.

title: Thesis
description: Supervised research for the master's thesis. A maximum of three semester hours to count for the degree.

title: Advanced Statistical Inference
description: Extends core concepts from mathematical statistics into a unified framework for advanced inference. Students will progress from classical normal-theory models to general and curved exponential families, eventually exploring non-exponential settings to understand how likelihood-based inference adapts to increasing model complexity. Key topics include large-sample behavior, information geometry, and higher-order approximations. The course addresses practical challenges such as handling nuisance parameters, bias-variance approximation, and the calibration of likelihood-based intervals and tests. These concepts are applied to normal and generalized linear models, demonstrating how rigorous theory informs practical analysis in complex data settings.

title: Bayesian Theory and Methods I
description: This course introduces the principles of Bayesian statistical inference, covering foundational concepts, decision theory, prior construction, and posterior computation. Students will explore Bayesian methods for point and interval estimation, contrasting them with frequentist approaches, along with other key inferential techniques. Practical aspects of Bayesian computation, including modern computational techniques and their implementation, are also introduced.

title: Semiparametric Regression Models
description: Semiparametric inference, with an emphasis on regression models applicable to a wider class of problems than can be addressed with parametric regression models. Topics include scatterplot smoothing, mixed models, additive models, interaction models, and generalized regression. Models are implemented using various statistical computing packages.

title: Bayesian Theory and Methods II
description: This course focuses on Bayesian model building, with an emphasis on Bayesian hierarchical models and their practical implementation. Topics include prior construction, computational challenges, and sample size determination. The course also covers assessing model performance through simulation techniques.

title: Functional Data Analysis
description: Introduction to the analysis of data that may be considered to be realizations from smooth functions. Visualization and data exploration, nonparametric smoothing, functional linear models, functional principle components analysis, analysis involving derivatives, registration, and nonlinear smoothing.

title: Statistical Bioinformatics
description: Critical evaluation of current statistical methodology used for the analysis of genomic and proteomic data.

title: Computational Statistics I
description: A comprehensive introduction to computing for statisticians.  Topics range from information technology and fundamentals of scientific computing to computing environments and workflows, statistical document preparation for reproducible research, and programming languages.  Students cannot receive credit for this and for STA 5373 .

title: Computational Statistics II
description: A continuation of STA 6374 with an emphasis on computational and applied mathematics, pseudo-random variate generation, and Monte Carlo methods.  Credit cannot be received for this course and for STA 5373 .

title: Modern Trends in Data Science Computing
description: A hands-on survey of practical data science technologies and tools used in industry. Topics vary and may include version control systems and collaborative software development; distributed computing; data storage and access; cloud computing; web technologies, applications, and dashboards; and workflow and pipelining tools.

title: Theory of Linear Models
description: Theory of general linear models including regression models, experimental design models, and variance component models. Least squares estimation. Gauss-Markov theorem and less than full rank hypotheses.

title: Advanced Multivariate Analysis
description: Multivariate normal and related distributions. Topics include generalizations of classical test statistics including Wilk's Lambda and Hotelling's T2, discriminant analysis, canonical variate analysis, and principal component analysis.

title: Analysis of Categorical Responses
description: Theory of generalized linear models including logistic, probit, and log linear models with special application to categorical and ordinal categorical data analysis.

title: Graduate Research
description: For research credit prior to admission to candidacy for an advanced degree. Credit will be given for the amount of work done. May be repeated for credit through 45 hours.

title: Dissertation
description: Supervised research for the doctoral dissertation. maximum of nine semester hours will count for the degree. A student may register for one to six semester hours in one semester.

title: Ugaritic Grammar and Lexicography
description: Fundamentals of the language of Ugarit with special attention to the relationship of Ugaritic with Hebrew grammar and lexicography.

title: History of Costume
description: A study of dress from Egyptian times to the twentieth century.

title: History of Decor in the Western World
description: Major historical movements and periods in architecture and decorative arts in the West, with special attention to how these trends relate to theatrical design.

title: Advanced Costume Design
description: Advanced principles and practices of costume design, with an emphasis on the design team/director collaboration.

title: Creative Dramatics
description: Education, theory, and philosophy that will cultivate the techniques of creative dramatics and develop the skills needed for human interaction in dramatic play. Lab required.

title: Advanced Directing I
description: Directing techniques for departures from realism, with an emphasis on postmodern theatre, musical theatre, and verse drama. Workshop required.

title: Advanced Directing II
description: Continuation of THEA 4365 . Workshop required.

title: Theater History III
description: Historical investigation of theatre practice, performance, and dramatic literature from the early twentieth century to the present.

title: The Theatre and Christianity
description: An exploration of biblical perspectives concerning creativity and the arts with a special emphasis on theatre and the performing arts.

title: Dramaturgy: Theory and Practice
description: Investigation of the functions and methods of the dramaturg such as choosing a season, audience enrichment, new play development, researching production history, and understanding dramatic structure and theory.

title: Advanced Studies in Contemporary Theatre and Drama
description: Topics related to leading contemporary playwrights, current issues of dramatic style, and emerging trends in theatrical practice. May be repeated once for credit with different content.

title: Performing Arts Management
description: Organizational structure, fundraising techniques, and board development for non-profit professional and commercial performing arts organizations. Includes contracting with professional unions, budgeting, and season planning. Technical crew lab included.

title: Advanced Stage Management
description: Role and responsibilities of the stage manager using the Regional Theatre or LORT Stage Manager model. Explores budgeting, seasonal planning and responsibilities in touring and non-theatrical situations.

title: Technical Direction for Theater
description: Four basic areas of technical direction: creating technical drawings, estimating a set, choosing materials used in the theater, and scheduling the building of sets for the theater.  This course may be repeated once with a different topic.

title: Introduction to Graduate Theatre Studies
description: A two week introductory intensive designed to prepare students for graduate level theatre research, analysis, and teaching.

title: Non-Thesis Degree Completion
description: To fulfill requirements for non-thesis master's students who need to complete final degree requirements other than coursework during their last semester.  This may include such things as a comprehensive examination, oral examination, or foreign language requirement. Students are required to be registered during the semester they graduate.

title: Contemporary Directing Styles
description: Analysis of contemporary directing styles.

title: History and Theory of Directing
description: An historical and theoretical study of the development of the director, with emphasis on the late nineteenth century to the present.

title: Play Analysis for Directors
description: Advanced study of several methodologies for analyzing dramatic structure and composition; approaches to the direct application of analysis to play production.

title: Contemporary Performance Theory
description: Development of twentieth-century performance theory.

title: Dramatic Theory and Criticism
description: Dramatic theory and criticism from Aristotle to the twentieth century.

title: Seminar in Classical Drama
description: An historical and theoretical study of selected classical masterworks in performance.

title: Directing Modern Plays
description: A study of theories and techniques used in directing selected European and American masterworks with emphasis on script analysis and interpretation, staging practices, and particular concept and style.

title: Directing Classical Plays
description: Directing theories and concepts of tragedy and comedy from the Greeks through the nineteenth century.

title: Production Design
description: Research, analysis, and practical experience in designing scenery, lighting, sound, costumes, and makeup for a realized production.

title: Seminar in Modern Drama
description: This seminar course offers advanced study of modern American and British and European drama specifically for graduate students of theatre.

title: Director's Workshop
description: Practical experience in all areas of theatre production for the public presentation of a full-length play.

title: Theatre Scholarship and Research Methods
description: Seminar study of practical issues in advanced theatre scholarship, research methods, application of theory, academic writing, and scholarly publication.

title: Seminar in Dramatic Production
description: Research and critical analysis of plays and their productions.

title: Independent Study
description: Guided study of pre-approved topic(s).

title: Dramaturgy
description: Application of directorial script analysis and dramaturgical tools in production planning, development of the production script, and rehearsal.

title: Collaborative Theater Process
description: An investigation through research and discussion of the elements of design, the relationship between the director and designers, and the process of unifying various elements of theatre production. Students will submit proposals for designs of both classic and modern plays and justify their ideas through literary and pictorial research.

title: Actor-Director Collaboration
description: Through scene work and acting exercises, directors explore the theories, common vocabularies, and basic skills and techniques needed to work with actors from differing backgrounds. Attention will also be given to auditioning, casting and rehearsal strategies as well as the major directorial performance styles of the twentieth century.

title: Playwriting
description: A study of the art and craft of playwriting, emphasizing analytic approaches to writing, developing a personal voice, narrative, characters, and point of view. Workshop required.

title: Thesis Production and Research
description: Master of Fine Arts students only. Research, design, and direction of the thesis production.

title: Thesis
description: Research, data analysis, writing, and oral defense of an approved master’s thesis.  At least six hours of THEA 5V99 are required.

title: Survey of Accounting
description: An introduction to accounting for non-business majors that follows the business cycle of a start-up company. Topics include researching a company, financing operations, planning and budgeting, and the basic accounting cycle.

title: Financial Accounting
description: An introduction to corporate financial statements, their preparation, and their use in decision making by persons outside the organization. The course focuses on analyzing business transactions to chart their effects on the results of operations, the cash flows, and the financial position of businesses organized for profit.

title: Managerial Accounting
description: An introduction to principles of managerial accounting. Emphasis is given to the development and use of accounting information to support managerial decision-making processes in manufacturing, service, and not-for-profit settings. Topics include managerial concepts and systems, various analyses for decision making, and planning and control.

title: Accounting Career Management Seminar
description: The course provides career exploration and development experiences designed for accounting majors in the first semester of the accounting core. It assists you in self-assessment, increasing personal marketability, choosing your career concentration focus, and developing various “soft skills” desired by hiring employers. The course offers unique professional development activities. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Financial Accounting and Reporting I
description: An examination of financial accounting considerations of business transactions. Emphasis is placed on understanding the accounting cycle, the conceptual framework underlying financial accounting, and recognition, measurement and reporting of receivables, inventories, property, plant and equipment, intangibles, liabilities, and stockholders' equity. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Financial Accounting and Reporting II
description: A continuation course to ACC 3301 , examining financial accounting considerations of business transactions. Emphasis is placed on understanding the recognition, measurement, and reporting of revenue, earnings per share, deferred taxes, pensions, and leases.

title: Accounting Information Systems
description: An in-depth study of the application of information systems knowledge to the accounting environment. Emphasis is upon developing students' abilities to understand the processing of accounting data (with an emphasis on the computer environment) and the controls that are necessary to assure accuracy and reliability of the data processed by the accounting system. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Introduction to Federal Taxation
description: An examination of the economic theory on which the United States taxation system is based and the legal principles surrounding income taxation.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: (Not open to accounting majors.) The first of two courses for non-accounting majors covering financial accounting concepts and practices applicable to business enterprises organized for profit. Topics include the accounting cycle, the conceptual framework underlying financial accounting, measurement and reporting issues relating to receivables, inventories, intangible assets, property and equipment, and an overview of the income statement and balance sheet. May not be taken after credit received for ACC 3301 . BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: (Not open to accounting majors). A continuation of ACC 3305 for non-accounting majors, focusing on current liabilities, long-term debt, investments, stockholders' equity, and the statement of cash flows. Coverage includes accounting issues related to pensions, leases, deferred income taxes, earnings per share, accounting changes, and error analysis. May not be taken after credit received for ACC 3302 .

title: Accounting Research and Communication
description: Use of primary sources of authoritative literature in resolving complex, professionally-oriented problems, primarily in financial accounting and taxation. Development of critical thinking and written and oral communication skills through applied research cases.

title: Advanced Managerial Accounting
description: Advanced study of managerial accounting and its use in both internal and external reporting, with an emphasis on managerial decision making. Course topics include various costing systems, cost-volume-profit analysis and the contribution approach to decision making, budgeting, and other managerial topics for planning and control.

title: Data and Analytics in Accounting
description: Students apply data and analytics skills to audit, tax, operations management, and other accounting issues, focusing on data visualizations and applied statistics. Students examine current developments in technology and analytics and relate them to the accounting profession.

title: Not-for-Profit and Governmental Accounting
description: (Not open to pre-business students). Examination of accounting, financial reporting, and budgeting for state and local governments, the federal government, and not-for-profit entities.

title: Auditing, Attestation and Assurance Services
description: A study of the processes conducted by independent, internal, and governmental accountants to audit, attest, and provide assurance services on information provided by management. Theoretical concepts of materiality, audit risk, and evidential matter are explored, along with the auditor's understanding of controls in a sophisticated technological environment. The application of these concepts in a way that develops critical thinking and communications skills is emphasized.

title: Advanced Financial Accounting Topics
description: A study of partnership accounting practices and the techniques followed to consolidate the financial statements of parent and subsidiary companies. Attention is also given to the distinctive accounting procedures associated with governmental and not-for-profit accounting.

title: Business and Professional Ethics for Accountants
description: Examination of moral and ethical issues within the accounting profession and the broader business environment. In addition to a broad study of ethical behavior and decision making, various professional codes of conduct within the accounting profession will be examined. Central to this examination will be the discussion of integrity, independence and objectivity, as well as accountants' legal liability.

title: Internship in Accounting
description: Supervised work, full-time, for a minimum of eight consecutive weeks with duties consistent with those of beginning accounting graduates.

title: Special Studies in Accounting
description: Individualized research in accounting. Student's proposals for special study project must be approved by the supervising faculty member. Offered on demand and by consent of the advisor for one to six semester hours. May be repeated several times under different topics.

title: Undergraduate Research in Accounting and Business Law
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken in multiple semesters for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Foundations for Social Justice
description: Examines theories and practices of social justice as related to oppressed groups in a multicultural society utilizing religious and nonreligious perspectives.  Addresses issues of power, inequality, and privilege, and the diverse experiences of oppressed groups in framing strategies to promote social justice.

title: Trauma, Loss, and Mourning
description: Specialized knowledge and skills for loss and grief and therapeutic interventions for the bereaved, including bereaved children. Preparation for work with loss of relationship and health across the lifespan. Emphasis on religious faith and grief, and the response of faith communities and religiously affiliated providers to suicide, divorce, loss of parental rights, and other disenfranchised grief.

title: Human Trafficking
description: This course is designed to help students gain a better understanding of contemporary human trafficking and modern day slavery. The roles that entities such as government, the media, faith-based organizations, organized crime, and culture play in this complex human rights and social (in)justice issue will also be explored.

title: Sociology of Aging
description: Impact of aging upon individuals and society, as well as the reactions of individuals and society to aging. Social gerontology is the principal focus of attention of the course.

title: Aging and Mental Health
description: Mental health needs and related problems of aging individuals with considerable discussion of approved mental health treatments for such persons.

title: Special Topics
description: Provides instruction in areas of social work knowledge, values, and/or skills that are not available in the standard social work baccalaureate or graduate curricula. Course may be repeated up to six times with different topic of study, not to exceed a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Introduction to Graduate Studies
description: This course introduces students to the Advanced Standing MSW program. Students will review skills, competencies, and ethics to prepare for advanced practice courses. Additionally, students will orient to MSW program policies, procedures, and resources to support academic success.

title: Introduction to Advanced Internship
description: Introduction to the advanced internship in a specialization

title: Cross Cultural Interactive Seminar IV
description: Through this course, students have the opportunity to synthesize learning from the explicit and implicit social work curriculum of the MSW program, identify major learning gleaned through the program, and prepare for reentry into their social work practice in international context.

title: Evaluation of Practice I
description: Selection and implementation of outcome-based evaluation of practice protocols to inform intervention decisions by advanced practice social workers.

title: Evaluation of Practice II
description: Selection and implementation of outcome-based program evaluation protocols to inform intervention decisions by advanced practice social workers.

title: Capstone
description: Integrative seminar to demonstrate readiness to practice social work at an advanced level in the student's area of specialization.

title: Advanced Practice Readiness I
description: This course prepares students for entrance into Baylor's Advanced standing MSW Program. Introduces the mission of the program with an emphasis on integrated faith and social work practice, the strengths perspective, and building communities. Overviews the professional foundation to include human behavior and the social environment and social work practice with individuals, families, groups, communities, and organizations.

title: International Social Work
description: This course provides students the opportunity to conceptualize international social work practice in domestic contexts and abroad. Through four seminars and an experiential learning weekend, students explore social work within a human rights framework; develop cultural competency; grasp specific international social issues, strengths, and intervention strategies; and contextualize social work skills with international client systems.

title: International Research and Strategic Planning
description: In this course, students identify and analyze a global social issue of their choice, research systemic causality of the issue, explore best practice models, and create a strategic approach for addressing the issue utilizing accumulated research and governmental, non-governmental, and congregational resources. This multidisciplinary course explores community interventions informed by engineering, nursing, business, and public health.

title: Human Diversity & Social Justice
description: Provides foundational content to understand better how diversity and difference shape the human experience and are critical to forming identity. Dimensions of intersectionality are explored and examined considering privilege and marginalization. Students learn to identify structural mechanisms of oppression and strategies for interrupting systems of power to create equity and inclusion in professional and community contexts.

title: Social Policy For Social Work Practice
description: Introduces the historical context of social policy and services in the United States, the analysis of current developments, and how social workers influence social welfare policy and provide social services.

title: Organizational Leadership & Management
description: Prepares students to perform managerial functions in public, nonprofit, and faith-based human service organizations. Specific attention is given to the topics of leadership, human resources, fundraising, grant writing, organizational development, resource management, structure and governance, and efforts to link human service organizations in an integrated community-wide service delivery system.

title: Theoretical Frameworks for Community Social Work Practice
description: Guides advanced practice social workers in the evaluation and selection of frameworks for community change. Emphasizes values, professional role, and cultural influences on how change is envisioned, enacted, and evaluated.

title: Advanced Clinical Theories and Models
description: Prepares students to learn and apply developmental, sociological, psychological, and therapeutic theories to the needs of individuals, families, groups, and communities. The course explores advanced clinical theories and empirically informed and emerging models for practice.

title: Clinical Diagnosis in Social Work
description: This course utilizes a strengths-based social work perspective to examine common diagnoses of the American Psychiatric Association’s Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, associated assessment tools, and areas for professional, ethical, and cultural consideration in the diagnostic process.

title: Human Behavior Theory: Person & Environment
description: This course prepares students to understand the foundational theoretical base of social work that drives and informs practice. Students gain applicable knowledge of the complex experiences of individuals, families, and informal and formal social networks and communities.

title: Social Work Practice with Individuals and Families
description: In this course, students practice skill development by applying the steps of the generalist intervention model (engagement, assessment, planning, intervention, evaluation, and termination/transition) to create diversitysensitive care management plans for individuals and families. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master’s degree.

title: Social Work Practice with Groups
description: Knowledge, values, and skills needed for working with groups. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master’s degree.

title: Social Work Practice with Communities & Organizations
description: This course prepares students for ethical and effective social work practice with communities and organizations. Students will learn models and skills that relate to macro social work practice. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master's degree.

title: Clinical Social Work Practice I
description: Prepares students with specialized knowledge of theories and practice models and skills for advanced clinical practice within a broad array of practice contexts. Students learn to assess and intervene at the levels of individual, family, and group with some discussion of organizational and community practice. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master's degree.

title: Community Social Work Practice I
description: Prepares advanced-practice social workers to promote community problem-solving and development. Emphasizes community assets; leadership development; and change strategy selection, enactment, and evaluation. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master's degree.

title: Health Practice & Policy
description: Prepares students for practice within health contexts. Uses social work knowledge, skills, and values to prepare students to assess and intervene at individual, family, and group levels in healthcare settings. Discusses policy, justice, and equity issues related to health and healthcare access.

title: Social Work Practice & Advocacy with Children & Families
description: Emphasizes the application of theory, models, and skills in practice contexts with children and families. Includes instruction on models and theories and the impact of crisis and trauma on the family. Exposes students to various themes in child and family practice, such as grief, levels of need, strengths, and collaborative work.

title: Community Social Work Practice II
description: Continuation of 5375. Prepares advanced practice social workers to promote community problem-solving and development. Emphasizes community assets; leadership development; and change strategy selection, enactment, and evaluation. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master’s degree.

title: Advanced Clinical Practice: Individuals, Families, and Groups
description: A grade of B or better must be received in this course in order to complete the requirements for the master’s degree. Prepares students with specialized knowledge and skills for advanced clinical practice within a broad array of practice contexts. Uses social work knowledge, skills, and values to prepare students to assess and intervene at the levels of individual, family, and group with some discussion of organizational and community practice. The course provides an overview of major theoretical clinical practice models.

title: Clinical Social Work Practice II
description: Students learn transdiagnostic clinical treatment models and skills. Emphasis is given to students demonstrating clinical skills that address a wide range of client contexts and goals. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master’s degree.

title: Research for Social Work Practice
description: Critical evaluation and use of research and conducting research in one's own professional practice.

title: Clinical Evaluation for Practice Improvement
description: Emphasizes the importance of systematic, consistent monitoring of client outcomes as a critical part of effective clinical social work practice. Building upon skills of thorough, accurate assessment and effective client rapport, this course focuses on the next steps of collaborating with the client system (individual, family, group) to monitor feasible clinical outcomes that inform the treatment process and the continuous improvement of practice.

title: Program Evaluation for Practice Improvement
description: Apply systematically derived, culturally responsive, and evidence-informed decisions for continuous quality improvement of human service systems, programs, and congregations.

title: Capstone Seminar
description: Integrative seminar at the end of the MSW process to provide students the opportunity to demonstrate key social work competencies at an advanced level in their area of specialization. Students develop a presentation that reflects competency in the goals and objectives of the social work program. Students create professional development plans to demonstrate readiness to enter, contribute to, and develop within the social work profession.

title: Professional Practice with Communities and Organizations
description: A grade of B or better must be received in this course in order to complete the requirements for the master’s degree. Knowledge, values, and skills needed for working with communities and organizations.

title: Part II Advanced Internship
description: Second of two terms of the advanced internship in a specified area of specialization. This is the second part of SWO 5494 .

title: Foundation Internship I
description: Orientation and introduction to internship, at least 240 hours of applied learning, and an integrative seminar. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master's degree.

title: Foundation Internship II
description: Social Work Internship, including at least 240 hours of applied learning and an integrative seminar. This is the second part of SWO 5491 with the addition of more sophisticated social work practice opportunities and expectations. A grade of B or better must be received in this course to complete the requirements for the master's degree.

title: Part I Advanced Internship
description: Introduction and first of two semesters of the advanced internship in a specified area of specialization.

title: Professional Practice with Individuals and Families
description: A grade of B or better must be received in this course in order to complete the requirements for the master’s degree. Knowledge, values, and skills needed for working with individuals and families.

title: Advanced Internship
description: Advanced internship in specialization area

title: Independent Study in Social Work
description: Independent study in Social Work course may be repeated up to six times with different topics of study, not to exceed a maximum of six semester hours to fulfill elective credit.

title: Advanced Special Topics
description: Provides advanced instruction in areas of social work knowledge, values, and/or skills that are not available in the standard social work graduate curriculum. Special topics in social work. Course may be repeated up to six times with different topic of study, not to exceed a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Christianity, Ethics, and Social Work
description: Explores research, theory, and practices related to religion and spirituality as they can inform social work practice. Specific emphasis is placed on the role of religion in contemplating the meaning of well-being and social justice.

title: Social Policy and the Religious Sector
description: Through current research, congregations and religiously affiliated organizations are examined, specifically through the lens of history, social work practice, social capital, and organizational theories, behaviors, and identities.

title: Religious and Cultural Diversity
description: Draws on the world religions to reflect on divergent cultural, ethical, and helping systems for believers.  Offers the foundations for helping by looking within a wide variety of religious traditions for support and understanding.

title: Academic Leadership and Administration in Social Work Education
description: Explores research, theory, and practices of leadership in social service organizations, social work education, and communities.

title: Program Evaluation
description: This course focuses on planning evaluation research for human service and educational programs and will prepare students to develop program evaluations to help agencies document their outcomes.

title: Theory and Model Development for Social Work Practice
description: Students will critically examine historic, philosophical, aesthetic, and social science foundations for classic and current social work intervention theories and models.

title: Higher Educational Teaching and Learning in Social Work
description: This interactive course prepares students for teaching in higher education. Exploration of learning styles, content delivery, teaching methods, curriculum and lesson plan development, and critical thinking stimulation prepares the student to develop and deliver courses in social work higher education venues.  The course uses theory and practical skill development, including demonstration of teaching methods and student learning.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: Students work with their peers and the instructor as they navigate all aspects of planning and executing a successful course of instruction for social work students.

title: Quantitative Research for Social Work
description: Focuses on quantitative research methodology as applied to research in the human services and social work.  Emphasizes the epistemological basis of different research methods, forming appropriate research questions and hypotheses, conducting literature reviews, developing research designs, and selecting and applying preliminary data analysis techniques.

title: Statistical Analysis for Social Work
description: An overview of statistical tests used to analyze data in social work. Emphasizes critical-thinking skills needed to evaluate others’ use of statistical tests as well as to conduct one’s own analyses, choose a statistical test, check that assumptions have been met, and interpret SPSS output. The course covers: correlation, Student’s t-test, the ANOVA family, linear regression, and logistic regression.

title: Qualitative Research for Social Work
description: Study of the philosophical underpinnings of qualitative methods. Students explore the similarities and differences between post-positivism and constructivism as they develop qualitative proposals for social work research.

title: Introduction to Doctoral Research
description: This course assists students in articulating a broad research agenda as a solid foundation for developing a dissertation topic, becoming familiar with and articulating the state of the current literature within their research agenda, and becoming socialized to scholarly and first phase of dissertation proposal writing, the publication process, and ways in which a strong empirically-informed argument can strengthen a research grant proposal.

title: Measurement in Social Work
description: Content prepares students for questionnaire construction and sample selection in conjunction with measurement development.

title: Advanced Qualitative Research
description: Focus is on the theoretical, methodological, and practical aspects of collecting, managing, and analyzing data from different qualitative traditions.  Specific content is tailored to topics relevant for dissertation research.

title: Research Practicum
description: Students refine proposals from the quantitative and qualitative courses, submit them for review, and carry out the studies from problem formulation to submission of two manuscripts suitable for peer-reviewed journal publications.

title: Dissertation Proposal and Prospectus
description: Research for doctoral students studying for preliminary examinations, preparing a topic proposal, or writing a prospectus in anticipation of candidacy. The course may be repeated.

title: Special Topics in Social Work Practice and Research
description: Special topics in social work practice and research.  May be repeated for credit, provided that the topic is not duplicated, for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Dissertation
description: Research, data analysis, and writing and oral/written defense of an approved doctoral dissertation. At least nine hours of SWO 6V99 are required. Students may not enroll for dissertation hours until they have been officially accepted into candidacy for the Ph.D. degree.

title: Arabic Language Workshop
description: For Music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Arabic Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Introduction to Arabic
description: Introduction to Arabic language and culture(s); practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress toward novice proficiency level

title: Intro to Arabic
description: Introduction to Arabic language and culture(s); practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress from novice toward intermediate proficiency

title: Intermediate Spoken/Modern Standard Arabic
description: Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading)

title: Arabic Popular Culture
description: Exploration and familiarization with the most popular elements of  Arabic culture relevant to daily life: (1) Friends and Family Relationship, (2) Celebrations of the Religious Occasions, (3) Business and Shopping, (4) Politics in the Middle East.

title: Advanced Arabic Grammar
description: Review of grammar and study of advanced structures in complex communication.

title: Arabic Conversation and Composition
description: Advanced practice in oral and written communication.

title: Advanced Topics in Arabic Language and Literature
description: Development of advanced skills in areas of writing, reading aloud, and listening in Modern Standard Arabic, on topics of general and professional interests, including aspects of Arabic culture and literature.

title: Arabic for Special Purposes
description: Business, technical, and/or scientific discourses in the context of the Arabic language and Middle Eastern cultures.

title: Special Topics
description: Independent study in Arabic Language, literature, or culture with a professor. With content changed, this course may be repeated once for a maximum of six hours of credit.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Classical Arabic Texts
description: Ideals and traditions of poetry and prose from the sixth to the nineteenth century, using sacred scriptures, early oratory, and masterpieces from the Near East, Egypt, and North Africa.

title: Media and Popular Culture in the Arabic-Speaking World
description: Current newspaper and magazine articles, radio broadcasts, and a variety of multimedia used to increase language skills and understanding of contemporary Arabic-speaking popular culture.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Aramaic
description: See REL 4303 for course information.

title: The Emergence of World Civilizations
description: Cultural processes involved in both the Neolithic and Urban Revolutions that led to the emergence of state-level societies and the world's greatest civilizations.

title: Historical Geography of the Biblical World
description: An in-depth study of the world of biblical times with emphasis on relationships between places and events. The course will involve readings, lectures, and both travel to and archaeological study of important sites in biblical history.

title: Archaeology and the Bible
description: See REL 3303 for course information.

title: Historical Archaeology
description: Integration of archaeological investigation with primary historical sources as applied to subjects ranging from colonial to modern times.

title: Seminar/Research
description: A combination of group seminars and individual or group research projects.

title: Archaeology and the Ancient Near East
description: See REL 4302 for course information.

title: Environmental Archaeology
description: See ANT 4340 for course information.

title: Geoarchaeology
description: See GEO 4348 for course information.

title: Archaeology of North America
description: See ANT 4353 for course information.

title: Archaeological Research
description: See ANT 4V16 for course information.

title: Special Topics in Archaeology
description: Current topics in archaeological research. May be repeated once for credit up to a total of six semester hours provided topic is different.

title: Introduction to Art (for non-art majors only)
description: The visual elements of art, its history, issues, functions, interpretations, materials and techniques. The creative process and the role of art and the artist in society, its reception and critical interpretation.

title: Studies in Art History (for non-art majors only)
description: Studies in art history for the non-major participating in a Baylor approved international program. May be repeated once with a change in content.

title: Survey of Western Art I
description: Architecture, painting, sculpture and other media in relation to styles and cultural patterns from prehistoric times to 1400 CE. Includes the creative process and the role of art and the artist in society, their reception and critical interpretation.

title: Survey of Western Art II
description: Architecture, painting, sculpture and other media in relation to styles and cultural patterns from 1400 CE to the present. Includes the creative process and the role of art and the artist in society, their reception and critical interpretation.

title: Greek Art and Archaeology
description: Greek art and archaeology from the Aegean Bronze Age through the first century BCE with emphasis on stylistic trends, the social functions of Greek art and material objects, and the relationship between art and various aspects of Greek life and thought.

title: Roman Art and Archaeology
description: Roman art and archaeology, beginning with the Etruscans and Archaic Rome, to the end of the fourth century CE. The social and political functions of objects and architecture are highlighted within historical and cultural contexts.

title: Medieval Art and Architecture
description: Art created between the fourth and the fourteenth centuries with an emphasis on architecture.

title: Nineteenth-Century Art History
description: Art of nineteenth-century Europe focusing on technique and style with political and social contexts.

title: Baroque Art History
description: Seventeenth-century painting, sculpture and architecture with Italy as the vanguard country. Significant attention given to Spain and includes paintings from France, Flanders, and Holland.

title: Northern Renaissance Art
description: Special attention will be given to the painters Van Eyck, Dürer, and Bruegel.

title: Eighteenth-Century European Art
description: Art of eighteenth-century Europe focusing on technique and style with political and social contexts.

title: Key Monuments in European Art
description: Selected examples of art studies in situ in art centers of Europe, supplemented by readings, lectures, and collected illustrations and catalogs.

title: Art History Methods and Theory
description: Primary methods and theories used in the study of the history of art.

title: Philosophy and the Arts
description: See PHI 3322 for course information.

title: Women in Modern and Contemporary Art History
description: Major female artists of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Field Studies in Art History
description: For Art majors only. Specialized field study with a specific destination for established art and art history students.  Includes a research component.  Requires concurrent enrollment in a listed co-requisite course.   May be repeated four times with a change of content.  Course does not apply to major or minor credit hours.

title: Special Topics in 18th- and 19th-Century Art
description: A research seminar on a selected topic of 18th- or 19th-century art. Subject varies year to year.  May be repeated once with a change in content.

title: Special Topics in Modern and Contemporary Art History
description: A research seminar on a selected major artist or topic of early modern European art. Subject varies year to year. May be repeated once with a change in content.

title: Modern American Art History
description: Modern art in the Americas from 1880 to 1945. Includes major artistic movements and their political, social, and historical contexts.

title: American Art
description: Artistic expression in the United States from the Colonial Period to 1900.

title: Contemporary Art History
description: A research seminar focused on the major trends in painting, sculpture, printmaking, performance, video, installation, and conceptual art from the 1960s to the present.

title: Special Topics in Art History
description: Topics not covered in other courses. Topic announced for semester or session; may emphasize a particular artist, movement, genre, theme, or problem. May be repeated once.

title: History of Impressionism and Post-Impressionism
description: Art of the Impressionism and Post-Impressionism movements, 1850-1900, focusing on technique and style with social and political contexts.

title: Greek Painted Pottery
description: Techniques, stylistic changes, and social use and function of Greek painted pottery and its imagery from the Late Aegean Bronze Age through the Late Classical and Early Hellenistic periods. Focus is on vessels in the Archaic and Classical periods.

title: Modern European Art
description: Modern art in Europe from Post-Impressionism to 1945.

title: Neoclassicism and Romanticism
description: Art of the Neoclassicism and Romanticism movements, 1750-1850, focusing on technique and style with social and political contexts.

title: Roman Sculpture
description: Roman sculpture from the Republic to Constantine (c. 313 CE) focusing on style and technique with political and social contexts.

title: Italian Renaissance Art History
description: A selective study of the art of Italy during the Renaissance period (c. 1300-1550) as seen within its historical context. Emphasis on the art of Florence and Rome.

title: Mannerism
description: A specialized study of Mannerism (c.1520-85) painting as a transition between the Renaissance and Baroque periods in Italy.  Emphasis on the cities of Florence and Rome.

title: Special Topics in Italian Renaissance and Baroque Art History
description: A specialized study of a major artist(s) or topic of Italian Renaissance or Baroque art. Subject varies year to year. May be repeated once with a change of content.

title: Special Topics in Greek and Roman Art and Archaeology
description: A research seminar on Greek or Roman Art and Architecture. May be repeated once with a change of content.

title: Greek and Roman Sport and Spectacle
description: History, origins, and political and social functions of sport and spectacle in Greece and Rome viewed through art, architecture, and material culture.

title: The Art of Michelangelo and His Contemporaries
description: The sources, development, style and meaning of Michelangelo’s art and the art of his sixteenth-century contemporaries within the historical context.

title: Art since 1945
description: Modern art in a global context from 1945 to 1970, focusing on the development of Abstract Expressionism and its legacies.

title: Internship in Art History
description: A field experience under the supervision of qualified museum or gallery personnel, which will make it possible for the student to receive on-the-job training in an area of art history.

title: Senior Thesis in Art History
description: Thesis preparing qualified seniors for graduate work in art history.

title: Aesthetic Perception and Experience
description: See ENG 43C1 for course information.

title: Special Problems in Art History
description: For Art majors only. Qualified students with established success in art history may research and report on special areas or topics in art history. May be repeated once with a change in content.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Drawing I
description: Realization of form and space through direct observation using a variety of drawing media. Perspective, sighting and measuring, methods of depicting light and shadow, composition, and basic drawing vocabulary.

title: Drawing II
description: Expands on skills learned in ART 1310 . Further investigation into line, mark, and value development and integration of color.

title: 2D Design
description: Designing in two dimensions, achieving unity using the elements and principles of design.  Problem-solving assignments emphasizing visual relationships, incorporating a variety of media.

title: 3D Design
description: Art majors only. Designing in three dimensions, achieving unity using the elements and principles of design.  Problem-solving assignments emphasizing visual relationships, incorporating a variety of media.

title: Figure Drawing I
description: Structural and relational studies of the human figure from observation. Emphasis on anatomy, tensions, proportions, gesture and compositional placement.

title: Figure Drawing II
description: Advanced studies of the human figure with more dependence upon anatomy and complicated figure arrangement.

title: Introduction to Oil Painting (for non-art majors only)
description: An introductory course in oil painting with an emphasis on application, color harmony and composition in a wide variety of subjects and approaches.

title: Introduction to Acrylic Painting (for non-art majors only)
description: An introductory course in acrylic painting with an emphasis on application, color, harmony and composition in a wide variety of subjects and approaches.

title: Introduction to Intaglio (for non-art majors only)
Introduction to Lithography (for non-art majors only)
description: Fine art stone and/or plate lithography. Development of technical skills related to drawing, processing and printing.

title: Introduction to Graphic Design (for non-art majors only)
description: Introduction to typography and concept development using design principles, the creative process, and problem-solving methodologies.

title: Introduction to Digital Imaging for Graphic Design (for non-art majors)
description: Focuses on techniques and principles to enhance and manipulate images. Development of principles of visual organization, aesthetics and composition, using both raster and vector solutions

title: Introduction to Brochure Design (for non-art majors)
description: An exploration of the combination of type and image emphasizing hierarchy, layouts, grid, styles sheets, and other organizational structures of design in the context of brochures and multi-page documents. Production and printing methods explored

title: Introduction to Darkroom Photography (for non-art majors)
description: Black and white fine art photography, emphasizing 35mm cameras and wet lab darkroom processes.  Cameras provided.

title: Introduction to Digital Photography (for non-majors)
description: Color image development, in-house printing, file management, and digital workflow using Adobe Lightroom. Emphasis on aesthetic and conceptual development, study of historic and contemporary photographic genres. Students must have access to a digital SLR camera.

title: Hand-Built Ceramics (for non-art majors only)
description: Hand-built ceramic forms for non-majors.  Includes instruction in pinched, coiled, and slab construction; surfacing/glazing and firing techniques.

title: Wheel-Thrown Ceramics (for non-art majors only)
description: Introduction to wheel-thrown ceramic form for non-majors.  Includes instruction in foundational wheel-thrown forms, surfacing/glazing and firing techniques

title: Wood Sculpture (for non-art majors)
description: Basic materials, methods and conceptual approaches to wood sculpture. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making sculpture.

title: Metal Sculpture (for non-art majors)
description: Basic materials, methods and conceptual approaches to welded steel sculpture. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making sculptures.

title: Jewelry and Metalsmithing (for non-art majors)
description: Basic materials, methods and conceptual approaches to metalsmithing and jewelry. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making jewelry

title: Advanced Drawing (Media)
description: A study of various techniques and different media used in drawing. Students will work on problems that will assist them in further drawing skills.

title: Figure Drawing III
description: A continuation of the study and discipline of drawing the human figure using a variety of media including pencil, charcoal, and ink.

title: Fiber Surface Design
description: Introduction to applied fiber surface design treatments. Hand printing, dyeing, and embellishing techniques include shibori, discharge, appliqué, patchwork, embroidery and art quilt methods.

title: Non-Woven Fiber Structures
description: Introduction to historical and contemporary basketry techniques, felting, and other non-loom produced fiber constructions. Emphasis on expressing three-dimensional ideas.

title: Weaving
description: Introduction to loom weaving through the study of weave structures, fiber types and color relationships. Development of technical skill and design competency.

title: Oil Painting
description: For Art majors only. Emphasis on application, color harmony and composition in relation to conceptual development

title: Acrylic Painting
description: Emphasis on application, color harmony and composition in a wide variety of subjects and approaches.

title: Intaglio
description: For Art majors only.   Explores etching and other fine art intaglio processes. Conceptual and technical skills related to creating, processing and hand-printing the limited-edition fine art intaglio print.

title: Lithography
description: For Art majors. Stone and/or plate lithography. Development of conceptual and technical skills related to creating, processing and hand-printing the limited-edition fine art lithograph print.

title: Relief Printmaking
description: For Art majors.  Development of conceptual and technical skills related to creating, processing and hand-printing the limited-edition fine art relief print.

title: Screenprinting
description: For Art majors only. Development of conceptual and technical skills related to creating, processing and hand-printing the limited-edition fine art screenprint.

title: Typography and Conceptual Development
description: ART 3330 must be taken concurrently with ART 3332 .  For Art majors.. Introduction to typography and conceptual development using design principles, the creative process, problem-solving methodologies.

title: Digital Imaging for Graphic Design
description: Must be taken concurrently with ART 3330 . For Art Majors only. Focuses on techniques and principles to enhance and manipulate images. Development of principles of visual organization, aesthetics and composition, using both raster and vector solutions.

title: Publication Design
description: Combining type and image emphasizing hierarchy, layouts, grid, styles sheets, and organizational structures of design in the context of brochures and multi-page documents. Production and printing methods explored.

title: Photography I: Introduction to the Camera and Darkroom
description: For art majors only. Black and white fine art photography emphasizing 35mm cameras and the wet lab. Aesthetic and conceptual development through the introduction of historic and contemporary photographic genres, critiques, and readings. Cameras provided.

title: Photography II: Introduction to Digital Photography and the Digital Lab
description: Color image development, in-house printing, file management, and digital workflow using Adobe Lightroom. Emphasis on both aesthetic and conceptual development. Introduction of historic and contemporary photographic genres. Students must furnish a digital SLR camera.

title: Medium and Large Format Photography
description: For Art majors only. Traditional and contemporary processes, including Holga and pinhole cameras, medium and large format photography, digital negatives, printing on fiber-based paper; and historical perspectives and theories in alternative processes

title: Hand-Built Ceramics
description: The design, construction, surfacing, firing, and historical traditions of hand-built ceramic form

title: Wheel-Thrown Ceramics
description: The design, construction, surfacing, firing, and historical traditions of wheel-thrown ceramic form.

title: Wood Sculpture
description: Materials, methods and conceptual approaches to wood sculpture. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making sculpture.

title: Metal Sculpture
description: For Art majors only. Materials, methods and conceptual approaches to welded steel sculpture. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making sculpture.

title: Jewelry and Metalsmithing
description: For Art majors or consent of instructor. Materials, methods and conceptual approaches to metalsmithing and jewelry. Emphasis on elements and principles of design, in the context of traditional and contemporary approaches to making jewelry.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Figure Drawing IV
description: For Art majors only. Drawing the human figure as a point of departure in the development of personal content, skills and style.

title: Advanced Fiber I
description: Study of specific fiber techniques and/or media selected by student in consultation with instructor.

title: Advanced Fiber Surface Design
description: Study of advanced fiber surface design processes. Emphasis on design, technical competency, and conceptual development.

title: Advanced Fiber II
description: Advanced problems in fiber with emphasis on conceptual development. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary fiber and the development of a personal vision. Completion of the BFA Senior Exhibition.

title: Watercolor Painting
description: For Art majors only. Techniques of the water-based media. Instruction gives much attention to creative style and individual criticism.

title: Advanced Painting I
description: For Art majors only. Study of a specific painting medium selected by the student in consultation with the instructor

title: Advanced Painting II
description: For Art majors only. Advanced problems in media selected by the student in consultation with the professor

title: Advanced Painting III
description: For Art majors only. Continuation of a self-directed program in consultation with instructor. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary painting and the development of a personal vision. Completion of the BFA Senior Exhibition

title: Advanced Printmaking I
description: For Art majors only. Emphasis on technical proficiency, creative expression and the creation of a body of work.

title: Advanced Printmaking II
description: For Art majors only. A continuation of ART 4324 with emphasis on technical proficiency creative expression and the creation of a personal vision. Completion of the BFA Senior exhibition.

title: Web Design
description: For Art majors only. Introduction to internet web page design with emphasis on process, concept, aesthetics, user experience, mark-up languages, and internet access software.

title: Illustration
description: For Art majors only. Illustrative art for graphic design. Emphasis on media, technique, style, and concept in advertising and editorial illustration.

title: Package Design
description: For Art majors only. Exploration of advanced principles of design when applied to three-dimensional packaging forms. Emphasis on perspectives of aesthetics, form, manufacturing processes, sustainability, utility, marketing, and regulations.

title: Identity Design
description: For Art majors. Exploration of theories and terminology of symbols and icons with an emphasis on branding and identity development.

title: Portfolio Preparation
description: For Art majors only. Development of the final graphic design portfolio. Additional emphasis on self-assessment, professional skills such as job search strategies, interviewing, and presentation skills. (Should be taken the last semester prior to graduation.)

title: Advanced Photography: Digital
description: Color image development using Adobe Photoshop. Conceptual exploration of photography as a fine art medium through guided projects, and historical and contemporary readings. Students must furnish a digital SLR camera.

title: Advanced Photography: Special Topics
description: For Art majors only. Emphasis on development of personal voice using Adobe Software, advanced photographic techniques and concepts. Creation of self-directed projects building on knowledge and understanding of photography as a fine art medium. Research current trends and technologies used in contemporary lens-based media.

title: Advanced Photography: Portfolio and Exhibition
description: Capstone seminar for photography majors. Emphasis on professional standards, technical mastery and individual artistic expression informed by historical perspectives and theories. Development of conceptually coherent body of work for BFA Exhibition.

title: Studio Topics in Graphic Design
description: For Art majors only. A category of topical courses designed to meet special needs, address issues in the professional field, and focus on research and experimentation in specialized media.

title: Advanced Ceramics I
description: Advanced study of ceramics building upon foundational content, skills, and perspectives developed in ART 3340 and ART 3341 .  Includes study of ceramic materials and glazes.

title: Advanced Ceramics II
description: For Art majors only. Advanced study of ceramics building upon content, skills, and perspectives developed in ART 4340 .  Includes development of personalized body of artwork and study of high temperature firing dynamics.

title: Advanced Ceramics III
description: For Art majors only. Continuation of a self-directed program in consultation with instructor. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary ceramics and the development of a personal vision.

title: Advanced Ceramics IV
description: For Art majors only. Continuation of a self-directed program in consultation with instructor. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary ceramics and the development of a personal vision. Completion of the BFA Senior Exhibition.

title: Advanced Sculpture I
description: For Art majors only. Sculpture emphasizing the combination of materials, the use of alternative materials, casting approaches, and the development of a personalized body of artwork

title: Advanced Sculpture II
description: For Art majors only. Continued emphasis on the combination of materials, the use of alternative materials, casting approaches, and the development of a personalized body of artwork.

title: Advanced Sculpture III
description: For Art majors only. Self-directed program in consultation with instructor. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary sculpture and the development of a personal vision.

title: Advanced Sculpture IV
description: For Art majors only. Continuation of a self-directed program in consultation with instructor. Examination of aesthetic issues in contemporary sculpture and the development of a personal vision. Completion of the BFA Senior Exhibition.

title: Internship in Studio Art
description: For Art majors only. A field experience under the supervision of a recognized professional artist, which will permit the student to receive concentrated and sufficiently lengthy training to become proficient in that art activity.

title: Special Problems in Studio Art
description: For Art majors only. Qualified students with established success in specified areas of art, especially their major concentration, may work on approved projects. May be repeated once with a change of content.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to Anthropology
description: A survey of human past and present physical and cultural diversity. The student is introduced to cultural anthropology, physical anthropology, archaeology, and linguistics, which provide a basis for understanding cultural diversity both within the U.S.A. and abroad.

title: Cultural Anthropology in Global Context
description: An introduction to global cultures with emphasis on socio-economic arrangements, religious beliefs, and responses of indigenous groups to modernization.

title: The Evolution of Human Societies: An Interdisciplinary Perspective
description: Explores the big-picture questions of how and why human societies evolved the way that they did and what factors contributed to the distinct trajectories in which those societies developed, and equally, those issues that contributed to their decline.

title: Cultural Geography
description: An introductory course in human or cultural geography. Topics include geographical concepts and factors; systematic and regional approaches; geographic tools and techniques, stressing spatial interactions; reciprocal relations between peoples and geographical environments; geographical graphics; major earth regions and their interrelations.

title: Research Writing: Anthropology
description: Develops student writing skills through the discipline of Anthropology. Students will learn how to write first-person accounts, analysis, and interpretation and how to apply theoretical approaches to explain culturally and/or biologically patterned events. Students will also learn how to locate and cite relevant research materials in response to a question and how to implement a research methodology.

title: Introduction to Global Health
description: An introduction to global health and illness from a cross-cultural and cross-border perspective.

title: Religion, Magic and Witchcraft
description: Cross-cultural variability in beliefs and practices related to the supernatural, including theories about social, psychological, and ecological functions.

title: Introduction to Human Evolution
description: An introduction to the study of humans as biological organisms. Emphasis on evolutionary theory and natural selection as driving forces in human evolutionary history.

title: Introduction to Archaeology
description: A survey of archaeology, including the nature of archaeology, what archaeologists do, and major milestones in human cultural evolution.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: The Emergence of World Civilizations
description: See ARC 2302 for course information.

title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
description: A survey of the sub-field of biological anthropology, including anthropological genetics, primatology, paleoanthropology, human ecology, and bioarchaeology.

title: Research
description: . Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Science, Society, and Culture
description: Anthropological perspectives on controversial issues in the history of scientific thought as conveyed through original texts, popular films, and cultural critique.

title: Historical Archaeology
description: See ARC 3307 for course information.

title: Methods of Archaeology
description: Methods in contemporary archaeology.

title: Introduction to Linguistics
description: See ENG 3310 for course information.

title: Environment and Human Behavior
description: Interrelationships between cultural and ecological systems, with focus on food production, economic exchange, and religious beliefs.

title: Human Osteology
description: The detailed examination of the elements of the human skeleton, with an emphasis on identifying and siding individual bones and their bony structures.

title: Indigenous Cultures of Modern Mexico and Central America
description: The cultural history of the indigenous peoples of central Mexico, including their adaptation to, and conflict with, forces of modernity.

title: Native North Americans
description: A study of the natives of North America including prehistory, cultural development, and contemporary relationships with other cultural ethnic groups.

title: Ancient Civilizations of Mesoamerica
description: The emergence and evolution of Meso-American civilizations in Mexico and Central America. Focus on Olmecs and Teotihuacan, as well as the Toltecs, Mayas, and Aztecs.

title: Ethnographic Methods
description: The multiple ethnographic methods used in cultural anthropological field research.

title: Forensic DNA Analysis
description: See FORS 3394 for course information.

title: Archaeology of Texas
description: Texas prehistory, from the first inhabitants to the arrival of the Europeans. Chronology, theoretical approaches, and regional developments in North America affecting the cultures of the prehistoric peoples of Texas.

title: Social Statistics
description: An introduction to descriptive and inferential statistics for social science majors. Development of skills in research data analysis.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Assessment Test
description: Anthropology assessment test for graduating seniors. Credit is given upon completion of the test.

title: Senior Thesis I
description: Thesis project planning, including topic identification, literature review, bibliography compilation, and thesis proposal.

title: Senior Thesis II
description: Primary data collection, processing, and analysis, culminating in the thesis.

title: Economic Anthropology
description: Traditional food production systems worldwide are compared to patterns in modern capitalist societies.

title: World Food Problems
description: See course information for ENV 4310 .

title: Human Biological Variation
description: Mechanisms that contribute to biological diversity between and among human populations.

title: Human Genetics: Evolution and Health
description: Explores major topics in human genetic and genomic research including human population history, evolution, and genetic correlates of health and disease will be emphasized through reading, discussion, and analysis of the primary literature in the field.

title: Evolution of Human Behavior
description: An examination of major evolutionary approaches to the study of human behavior, including human reproduction, parenting, cooperation, and warfare.

title: Global Health Ethics
description: Focus on the ethics of global health to examine the motivations and worldviews driving how we define, research and address global health problems.

title: Formal Decision Analysis & Health Policy
description: Introduction to formal decision analysis, the administrative allocation of scarce resources in health care, and the application of decision-making strategies in public policy among indigenous peoples.

title: Global Health Policy
description: History, theory, and practice of global health policies from the perspectives of public health, economics, and anthropology.

title: Development and Indigenous People
description: See ENV 4350 for course information.

title: Death, Injury and Physical Remains
description: See FORS 4359 for course information.

title: Bioarchaeology
description: Advanced osteological approaches to the study of human skeletal remains from archaeological sites, with an emphasis on the study of human diet and health in prehistory.

title: Anthropology Capstone
description: Integration of knowledge and experiences acquired from anthropology courses, research, and field school.

title: Field School in Global Health
description: Field training in the methods and techniques of Global Health research at either domestic or international locations. Topics will vary by year and location.

title: Research Methods in Biological Anthropology
description: Lecture and lab experience in the methods and techniques of biological anthropological research.  May be repeated for a total of six semester hours with different topics.

title: Research in Global Health
description: Lecture, lab, or field research in various aspect of Global Health. May be repeated for a total of six semester hours with different topics.

title: Epidemiological Research Design
description: Application of epidemiological skills and methods to research questions. Emphasis on designing studies that will address real world clinical problems in cross-cultural context.  May be repeated for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Fundamentals of Air Traffic Control
description: The course will introduce the student to the Air Traffic Control to include a brief story of Air Traffic Control, an introduction to the Federal Aviation Administration, an introduction to the FAAO/JO 710.65, terms of reference, general control, flight progress strips, radio and interphone communications, route and NAVAID description, weather information, altimeter settings, and automatic terminal information service procedures.

title: The Peoples and Culture of Asia
description: An introduction to contemporary Asian economy, politics, and society.

title: Traditional China
description: See HIS 3305 for course information.

title: Modern Japan
description: An overview of the internal and external forces which have affected the development of Japanese history from ancient times to the twentieth century. Emphasis on political, economic, and cultural developments that have shaped modern Japan.

title: Politics and Problems of Developing Countries
description: See PSC 3314 for course information.

title: Geography of East Asia
description: Geographical analysis of physical, cultural, economic, and political factors in East Asia, including land and resource usage, population changes, and economic development.

title: World Religions
description: See REL 3345 for course information.

title: Special Studies and Projects
description: Special studies and research projects in Asian studies; prior approval is required.  With content changed, this course may be repeated once for a maximum of six semester hours credit.

title: Special Topics in Asian Studies
description: A study of special topics relating to Asia.  With content changed, this course may be repeated once for a maximum of six semester hours credit.

title: Modern China
description: An overview of cultural, economic, literary, political, social, and religious developments in China from the seventeenth century to the present. Emphasis on the late imperial state, revolution, and the multiple attempts to remake state and society.
