Discussion of how these authors address questions about basic human goods, frequently under conditions of enslavement, oppression, poverty and the abuse of authority. Reflection on new forms of artistic beauty and literary excellence emerging from these conditions.

title: Master Works in Art
description: Survey and analysis of master works of art, including painting, sculpture, architecture, and photography, in their relationship to the six eras of the Great Texts Seminar Sequence.

title: Master Works in Drama
description: Survey and analysis of a selection of the most important dramatic works of the Western intellectual tradition.

title: Great Texts in the Origins of Science
description: An historical overview of the significant developments in the science of the ancient and medieval periods. Students will read selections from Euclid, Archimedes, Pythagorus, Copernicus, Galileo, and others.

title: Great Texts in Leadership
description: An undergraduate seminar devoted to an examination of leaders and the virtues of leadership as found in the Great Texts. Students will read selections from the Bible, Aristotle, Cicero, Plutarch, St. Thomas Aquinas, Pascal, Machiavelli, Dickens, Hardy, Bernanos, Conrad, O'Connor, and others.

title: Great Texts in Business
description: An undergraduate seminar devoted to an examination of business, management, and entrepreneurship as presented through some of the great texts of the Western and Eastern traditions.

title: Great Texts in the Principles of the Liberal Arts
description: Readings may extend from antiquity to the present, tracing connections among these disciplines in ways that are not usually contained within the customary modern treatment of a single liberal art.

title: Great Texts in the Practice of the Liberal Arts
description: Readings focus on a selection of major texts or direct instruction in a liberal arts tradition that may range from antiquity to the present, considering how these works address perennial questions of human formation that are debated among Great Texas.

title: Great Texts in Medicine
description: Examines classic works in the history of medicine (Hippocrates, Galen, Vesalius, Hildegard von Bingen, and others) as well as important philosophical and literary investigations into the nature of illness and the work of the healing arts (Camus, Woolf, Tolstoy, Solzhenitsyn, Lahiri, and others).

title: Happiness, Morality, and the Good Life
description: Instruction in the key texts and questions which address the relation between morality and happiness.  Included will be an understanding of the historical evolution of the understanding of happiness and its relation to human flourishing, pleasure, deferred gratification, and the “good life.”   Authors studied may include Aristotle, Lucretius, Boethius, Boccaccio, Catherine of Siena, de Pizan, Luther, Goethe, Milton, Montaigne, Austen, Kierkegaard, Woolf, Murdoch, Percy, Williams, and others.  Designed to enhance the skills and insights necessary for seeking wisdom in the context of the conversation among Great Texts and throughout life.

title: Great Texts and the Great Outdoors
description: Experiential learning combines an outdoor activity such as fishing or stargazing with seminar-style discussion of classic works on the activity drawn from the western tradition.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Twentieth Century
description: An undergraduate seminar in the Great Texts of the twentieth century. Students will read selections from Yeats, William James, Weber, Freud, Barth, Woolf, Beckett, Faulkner, O'Connor, Lewis, Eliot, Wiesel, Frost, Camus, Solzhenitsyn, Hemingway, Arendt, King, and others.

title: Dante and the Italian Renaissance
description: An undergraduate seminar focusing on the work of Dante Alighieri, especially The Divine Comedy. Other writers and artists such as Petrarch, Boccaccio, Catherine of Siena, Bruni, Ariosto, and Machiavelli may be read. Students will read texts in translation but with frequent reference to the Italian.

title: Augustine and Aquinas
description: An undergraduate seminar in the central texts of two of the chief authors of the Western Christian tradition, Augustine and Aquinas. Attention may be paid to predecessors such as Plato, Aristotle, Virgil, or Cicero, as well as to the influence of Augustine and Aquinas upon other thinkers such as Dante, Petrarch, Machiavelli, Descartes, Nietzsche, Joyce, Eliot, and O'Connor. Attention may also be paid to the influence of Augustine upon Aquinas.

title: Master Works in Theology
description: An undergraduate seminar devoted to the study of seminal works in theology, in connection with one or more historical periods, ranging from antiquity to the present. Emphasis will be placed on theological interactions with the literature, philosophy, social science, and/or artistic expression in these eras.

title: Master Works in Music
description: Survey and analysis of seminal landmarks of music in their relationship to the six eras of the Great Texts Seminar Sequence.

title: Great Texts in Modern Science
description: An historical overview of the history and development of the natural and social sciences from the Enlightenment to the present. Students will concentrate on the Great Texts of science from Bacon, Vico, Newton, Lavoisier, Darwin, Einstein, Heisenberg, Kuhn, and others.

title: Great Texts Capstone Course
description: An undergraduate senior seminar culminating in a research project which draws upon the works and texts of the Great Texts curriculum. Students will present and defend their senior projects before student colleagues and a faculty panel.

title: Confession and Autobiography
description: An undergraduate seminar devoted to the genre of confession and autobiography, with a focus on texts that address questions about the kinds of communities, activities and virtues that cultivate true human flourishing. Students will read selections from Augustine, Teresa of Avila, Rousseau, Tolstoy, Henry Adams, John Henry Newman, Dorothy Day, Malcolm X, Will Campbell, Nelson Mandela, Wendell Berry and others.

title: Internship in the Liberal Arts
description: Combines readings with observation and practical experience to improve instruction through apprenticeship.

title: Epic and Romance in the High Middle Ages
description: Explores epic and romance texts from France and England in their native vernaculars. Reading competence in Latin (classical or medieval), French (old or modern), or Middle English required.

title: Special Topics in Great Texts
description: Research projects to be undertaken by students or by classes under the direct supervision of the professor. Course may be repeated twice with a different topic of study.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introductory Hebrew I
description: An introduction to the orthography, morphology, and syntax of Biblical Hebrew.

title: Introductory Hebrew II
description: Continuation of HEB 1301 .

title: Intermediate Hebrew I
description: Selected readings in the historic prose of the Old Testament with Hebrew composition.

title: Intermediate Hebrew II
description: Selected readings in the Prophets, Psalter, and Wisdom literature of the Old Testament.

title: Dimensions of Health Science Studies
description: This course, mandatory for all Health Science Studies majors, presents essential introductory information to prepare the student for success in determining a field of study and pursuing a healthcare career including knowledge of the healthcare environment, qualifications for pursing specific fields, professionalism expectations, the importance of communications, cultural competence in healthcare, the use of professional literature, and the basics of healthcare ethics.

title: Anatomy & Physiology for Healthcare Professionals: Structure, Movement & Neural Control
description: This course is designed for students aspiring to enter healthcare and allied healthcare professions. It provides a comprehensive investigation of the structure and function of the human body, exploring its various systems and organizational structure. The course begins with an introduction to the chemical and cellular levels of organization, followed by an in-depth investigation of the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems. Additionally, the course includes an investigation of the special senses.

title: Anatomy & Physiology for Healthcare Professionals: Circulation, Metabolism & Reproduction
description: This course is designed for students aspiring to enter healthcare and allied healthcare professions. It provides a comprehensive investigation of the structure and function of the human body, exploring its systems and organizational structure. The course begins with an in-depth investigation of the cardiovascular, lymphatic, endocrine, respiratory, urinary, digestive, and reproductive systems. Additionally, it includes essential topics such as nutrition, metabolism, fluid-electrolyte and acid-base balance, genetics, and heredity.

title: Clinical Skills Training and Assessment
description: Students from other majors will require consent of the instructor. Introduces the concepts and techniques of performing a multi-system physical assessment, collecting a patient history, and documenting subjective history and objective findings.

title: Foundations of Dentistry
description: An overview of dental anatomy and essential clinical skills for students aspiring to enter the field of dentistry.  This course combines theoretical knowledge with practical applications to build a strong foundation for understanding oral structures and developing basic dental techniques. Students will be introduced to key concepts and sciences related to dentistry, including anatomy, physiology, pathology, and microbiology.

title: Essentials of Ethics in Healthcare
description: See HP 3311 for course information.

title: Clinical Research Collaboration
description: The purpose of this course is to allow undergraduate Health Science Studies students the opportunity to work with a clinical research professional and participate in the clinical research process.

title: A Biblical Theology of Suffering for Health Care Practitioners
description: A study of the biblical meanings and purposes of suffering with theological reflections and application to those served by health care professionals with consideration to disability-related ministries.

title: Service, Leadership, and Organizational Wellness in a Healthcare Career
description: This course will explore the many facets of leadership, as well as leadership principles, through community outreach experiences. Students will learn the framework of crafting a community service project based on a strengths-perspective of the community. This course requires off-campus service learning.

title: Professional Internship in Health
description: Designed to provide students with planned experiences with professionals in various health, allied health and/or community health settings including public, private, and health agencies.

title: Honors Engaged Learning Seminar
description: An introductory course that helps students apply their skills and learning in meaningful ways through study and practical application. Course elements include research or service learning with community partners outside the traditional classroom.

title: Introductory Topics in Honors
description: Introduces students to subject areas as part of their Honors curriculum. Through readings and research, students will explore topics of significance while laying the foundation for future academic inquiry. With content changed, this course may be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Honors Research Experience I
description: Research under the supervision of a faculty member, or specialized readings/research as part of a small group. This course can also be used to indicate research undertaken outside the university as part of an internship or fellowship with approval (zero credit hours). With content changed, this course may be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Special Topics in the Honors Program
description: This course provides students the opportunity to pursue various lines of study as part of their Honors curriculum.  Topics of the course are determined by the instructor in coordination with the Honors Program Director.  With content changed, this course may be repeated for credit for a maximum of six hours.

title: Honors Research Experience II
description: Research under the supervision of a faculty member, or specialized readings/research as part of a small group. This course can also be used to indicate research undertaken outside the university as part of an internship or fellowship with approval (zero credit hours). With content changed, this course may be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Advanced Readings & Research
description: Advanced readings and research toward an Honors thesis.  The course is conducted under the direction of a faculty mentor from the student's major department, concentration, or area of research.

title: Advanced Readings & Research
description: Continuation of HON 3100 , as well as participation in proseminars on thesis research and writing.  Includes submission of a formal thesis proposal.

title: Colloquium
description: Informal group discussion of important books and issues in various branches of knowledge under the direction of faculty members or others who are specialists in the area of discussion.

title: Colloquium
description: Continuation of HON 3200 .

title: Research Design
description: Within the context of providing Honors students the architecture of laboratory, clinical, and social science research, this course has the goals of: introducing an epistemological and methodological strategy for students to conduct scientific research; enabling them to conceptualize and plan their Honors Thesis; facilitating the student's beginning work with their faculty mentor; and requiring the participants to write the initial draft of their first four chapters of their Honors Thesis - Introduction, Review of Literature, Methodology, and Hypotheses.

title: Readings in the Honors Program
description: Material selected will serve needs of students within the Honors Program. With content changed, this course may be repeated once for credit.

title: Honors Research Experience III
description: Research under the supervision of a faculty member, or specialized readings/research as part of a small group. This course can also be used to indicate research undertaken outside the university as part of an internship or fellowship with approval (zero credit hours). With content changed, this course may be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Honors Exit Review
description: Students complete the Honors Exit Review course during their final semester.  They will receive a grade of Credit once they have submitted all necessary paperwork and completed all other steps necessary to graduate from the Honors Program.

title: Honors Thesis
description: Students complete Honors Thesis while conducting research and writing their thesis.  The course is supervised by a faculty member in the student's major department or concentration.  As a variable hour course, HON 4V87 may be completed for 1-4 hours of credit during a given semester depending upon the consent of the instructor and approval from the Honors Program.  Typically, students will register for two hours of Honors Thesis during their penultimate semester and two final hours during their last semester.

title: Honors Research Experience IV
description: Research under the supervision of a faculty member, or specialized readings/research as part of a small group. This course can also be used to indicate research undertaken outside the university as part of an internship or fellowship with approval (zero credit hours). With content changed, this course may be repeated for a maximum of six credit hours.

title: The United States in Global Perspective
description: Introduction to history, historical thinking and the history of the United States within a global context, examining the ways a distinct American society developed within larger patterns of world history.  Themes will vary by instructor but may include democracy, freedom and equality; global conflict; imperialism; industrialization and economic systems; migration and immigration; nationalism; and revolution.

title: World History to 1500
description: A chronological, thematic, and analytical survey of the political, economic, social, cultural, and diplomatic history of Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Americas from prehistory to approximately 1500 CE.

title: World History since 1500
description: A chronological, thematic, and analytical survey of the political, economic, social, cultural, and diplomatic history of Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Americas and the growth of the modern global community from approximately 1500 CE to the present.

title: United States History to 1877
description: A chronological, thematic, and analytical survey of the political, economic, social, cultural, and diplomatic history of the United States from colonial origins and early nationhood through the era of Reconstruction.

title: United States History since 1877
description: A chronological, thematic, and analytical survey of the political, economic, social, cultural, and diplomatic history of the United States from the end of Reconstruction to the present.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to Digital Humanities
description: An introduction to how digital methods can supplement traditional humanities research as well as how to navigate the technical and ethical considerations of these emerging methodologies. Topics will vary by instructor.

title: Introduction to the Middle East
description: A regional geographic introduction to the Middle East region. An overview of the physical, cultural, ethnographic, economic and geopolitical components of the region followed by a more in-depth description of selected political states.

title: Introduction to African History
description: An introduction to the political, social, and cultural history of Africa from the ancient to the modern period.

title: Introduction to the History of Childhood
description: An introduction to the global history of childhood from prehistory to the present with a focus on the methods historians use to explore the history of children and the conceptualization of childhood.

title: Mexican American History to 1848
description: An introduction to the social, political, and cultural history of Mexican Americans from the colonial period to the Mexican-American War.

title: Mexican American History since 1848
description: An introduction to the social, political, and cultural history of Mexican Americans from the Mexican-American War to the present

title: African American History to 1870
description: An introduction to the beliefs, policies, events, and debates that shaped the history and legacy of people in the United States who descended from West and Central Africa through the ratification of the 15th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution.

title: African American History since 1870
description: An introduction to the major events and developments in African American history from the ratification of 15th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution in 1870 to the present.

title: History of Warfare
description: An introduction to military conflicts and practices throughout history and around the world with emphasis on broad strategy and tactics.

title: Introduction to Slavic and East European Studies I
description: See SEES 2380 for course information.

title: Introduction to Slavic and East European Studies
description: See SEES 2381 for course information.

title: Introduction to East Asian History
description: An introduction to the history of East Asia from ancient times through today. Emphasis on the interrelated histories of Korea, Japan, and China and their relationships with the wider world, including trade and cultural exchanges, reform and revolution, war, colonialism, Cold War geopolitics, and globalization.

title: Introduction to Women’s and Gender History
description: An introduction to central themes and debates in Women's and Gender History from European, American, and global perspectives. Provides theoretical background and analytical skills for advanced coursework.

title: Research Writing: History
description: An introduction to historical thought and historiographical practice through the process of research writing.

title: Introduction to Model Organization of American States
description: Course prepares students for first-time participation in a Model Organization of American States simulation. Stresses historical and contemporary hemispheric issues. May be repeated for a total of six credit hours under different topics.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Internship in History
description: Internship at an institution or program engaged in public history.

title: Modern China
description: An overview of cultural, economic, literary, political, social, and religious developments in China from the seventeenth century to the present. Emphasis on the late imperial state, revolution, and the multiple attempts to remake state and society.

title: Traditional China
description: A survey of Chinese history from its origins to 1700. Treatment of artistic, cultural, economic, literary, political, social and religious developments in China that have shaped East Asian civilizations.

title: Modern Japan
description: An overview of the internal and external forces which have affected the development of Japanese history from ancient times to the twentieth century. Emphasis on political, economic, and cultural developments that have shaped modern Japan.

title: Middle East History, 600-1798
description: An overview of the history of the Middle East from the rise of Islam to Napoleon's invasion of Egypt in 1798.

title: Modern Middle East History
description: An overview of the political, religious, intellectual and social history of the Middle East during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

title: Global Maritime History
description: An overview of maritime history from a global perspective, including interactions of economics, societies, militaries, transport, and technology with the maritime environment.

title: West African History
description: An overview of West African history, culture, customs, and societies from the earliest times to the structural adjustment programs of the 1980s, the end of military regimes, and the emergence of democratic governance.

title: Modern Africa
description: An overview of the history of Africa since the early nineteenth century including analyses of contemporary African issues in a wider historical context.

title: History of Global Epidemics
description: An in-depth exploration of the history of pandemics and their political, economic, cultural, and scientific impacts from the early modern period to the present, with a global perspective.

title: Greek History and Civilization
description: See CLA 3302 for course information.

title: Hellenistic History and Civilization
description: See CLA 3303 for course information.

title: Roman Republic
description: Explores the culture, ethics, religious beliefs, history, literature, and social issues of ancient Rome from its origins as a small village on the banks of the Tiber River through the fall of the Republic at the end of the first century BCE.

title: European Imperialism, 1800-1975
description: An overview of the establishment of global empires by European countries and the experiences of people living in those empires from the early nineteenth century to the era of decolonization in the twentieth century.

title: European Colonialism, 1400-1800
description: An overview of the development and maintenance of permanent contacts by Europeans with other peoples and cultures around the world and the establishment of transcontinental empires between the late Middle Ages and the turn of the nineteenth century.

title: Roman Empire
description: Explores the history, culture, literature, and religion of the Roman Empire from the age of Augustus (31 BC – AD 14) through the division of empire following the death of Theodosius I (AD 379 – 395). Special attention will be paid to the development of the imperial office, the role of the provinces and their governors, and the evolution of the literary tradition.

title: Modern India
description: An overview of the cultural, religious, social, economic, and political history of India from the eighteenth century to today. Emphasis on national identity, economic development, gender issues, and environmental sustainability.

title: Festivals of Renaissance Europe
description: Examines festivals, spectacles, celebrations, rituals, and ceremonies of Renaissance Europe, revealing their roles in forging social identities, demonstrating power, and expressing emotion.

title: Early Medieval Europe, 300-1000
description: An overview of the emergence of medieval European societies and states through the blending of Roman, Christian, and Germanic institutions, customs, and beliefs.

title: Late Medieval Europe, 1000-1450
description: An overview of the flowering of medieval politics, society, and culture in Europe. Emphasis on the medieval church and the origins of the modern European state.

title: Early Modern Europe
description: An overview of the history of Europe from the age of absolutism to the Enlightenment. Emphasis on the major political, economic, social, cultural, scientific, and intellectual developments of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

title: Nineteenth-Century Europe
description: An overview of the political, social, and economic development of the European nations from roughly the Congress of Vienna to the outbreak of the First World War. Emphasis on the rise of liberalism, the growth of nationalism, imperialism, and the development of international rivalries.

title: Twentieth-Century Europe
description: An overview of the history of Europe during the twentieth century. Emphasis on problems of peace making and international organization, the rise of Fascism and Communism, World War II and post-war reconstruction, the East/West divide during the Cold War, post-communist Eastern Europe, and the making of the European Union.

title: Byzantine History and Civilization
description: See CLA 3304 for course information.

title: Russia to 1861
description: An overview of society, politics, and culture in Russia from its dynastic origins to the mid-nineteenth century.

title: Russia since 1861
description: An overview of society, politics, and culture in the Russian Empire, the Soviet Union, and present post-communist Russia.

title: History of Ukraine
description: An overview of the political, cultural, and social history of Ukraine from the first kingdom to post-Soviet restoration of sovereignty. Emphasis on imperial occupations and partition, national revivals, and mass violence.

title: Modern Germany
description: An overview of the political, cultural, and economic history of German-speaking Central Europe since the eighteenth century.

title: European Military History
description: An overview of the precedents, innovations, and legacies of European warfare in the early modern and modern eras. Emphasizes the impact of institutions, culture, and technology on tactics, operations, and strategy.

title: Special Topics in Global History
description: An overview of a specialized area of global history not covered by regular course offerings. May be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Special Topics in European History
description: An overview of a specialized area of European history not covered by regular course offerings. May be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Special Topics in U.S. History
description: An overview of a specialized area of U.S. history not covered by regular course offerings. May be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Pre-Columbian and Colonial Latin America
description: An overview of pre-Columbian and Spanish and Portuguese colonial societies in the Americas. Emphasis on Meso-American and Andean civilizations, the creation of colonial societies, and the forces leading to the end of political colonialism in Latin America.

title: Modern Latin America
description: An overview of the history of Latin American countries since independence. Emphasis on political and social factors influencing national development, race relations, the emergence and development of political parties, and how ethnic and social minorities negotiated their positions as full citizens in their communities.

title: Brazil since 1808
description: An overview of Brazilian history since independence. Emphasis on Brazilians’ active participation in shaping their nation and themes such as race, gender, and class.

title: History of the American Presidency
description: An overview of the history of the U.S. presidency and the presidents. Emphasis on their backgrounds, parties and elections, opinions about presidents, selected case studies, and the vice presidency.

title: Religion in America to 1877
description: An overview of religion in America from the colonial period to 1877. Emphasis on the diversity of American religion, the origins and developments of major denominations, and the interaction of religion and society

title: Religion in America since 1877
description: An overview of religion in America from the end of Reconstruction to the present. Emphasis on race, gender, and religious pluralism.

title: Slavery in Film
description: An overview of the history of slavery in the United States and how that history has been portrayed in feature films, television series, and documentaries in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.

title: History of Black Americans
description: A survey of the history of black Americans from their African origins to the present. Emphasis will be given to the economic, social, and political impact of the presence of black people in the American colonies and the United States; attention will be given to the institution of slavery, the emancipation movement, the rise of segregation, black nationalism, and the ideologies and personalities of the civil rights movement.

title: American Colonial History
description: An overview of the settlement, growth, and development of Anglo-American colonies in North America during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

title: American Revolution and Constitution
description: An overview of the creation of the American nation out of thirteen colonies. Emphasis on the social, economic, political, and ideological roots of the colonists' resistance to imperial power, the decisions for revolution and independence, the fighting of the Revolutionary War, the rise and fall of the Confederation, and the drafting and ratification of the Constitution.

title: The Early Republic, 1789-1860
description: An overview of the challenges related to creating the new nation of the United States in the first decades of its independence. Emphasis on political, diplomatic, social, economic, and cultural issues and controversies.

title: The Civil War and Reconstruction
description: An overview of political, social, and cultural transformations in the U.S. in the mid-nineteenth century. Emphasis on the causes, military operations, and aftermath of the American Civil War.

title: The United States, 1877-1920
description: An overview of U.S. history from the end of Reconstruction through the First World War. Emphasis on industrialization, urbanization, immigration, Western migration, Populism, Progressivism, Empire, World War I, race and gender relations, and changes in science and higher education.

title: The United States since 1920
description: An overview of U.S. history from the end of World War 1 to the present. Emphasis on the post-Progressive decade, the Great Depression, the New Deal, domestic developments since the New Deal, World War II, and the rise and development of the Cold War.

title: Texas History
description: An overview of the political, economic, and social history of Texas in its regional setting in the American Southwest from the period of colonization to the present.

title: History of East Central Europe
description: Examines the region stretching between Germany and Russia in the 19th and 20th Century with emphasis on the interaction of transnational forces, domestic movements in politics, culture, and nation-building, and the changes in how the region’s distinctiveness is perceived within Europe.

title: A Cultural History of Hunting in North America
description: An overview of interactions between North American people and the animals and environments of the continent through the activity of hunting.

title: History of Indigenous Peoples of America
description: An overview of the history of indigenous peoples in the Americas, focusing on the area known now as the continental United States.

title: History of the South
description: An overview of the history of the U.S. South in both the ante-bellum and post-bellum periods.

title: American Military History to 1890
description: An overview of major military conflicts in North America from European colonization to the close of the Plains Indians Wars.

title: American Military History since 1890
description: An overview of major military conflicts in United States history from the Spanish-American War to the present day.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: History of Medicine
description: A review of the origins and development of medicine and medical science from prehistoric times to the present. Consideration will be given to the impact of major diseases on civilization and the emergence of successful measures of control.

title: The Mexican Revolution and Its Legacies
description: An in-depth exploration of the Mexican Revolution of 1910 and its legacies of violence, popular resistance, and negotiated state rule. Emphasis on power and revolution, gender, race, class, religion, youth activism, and indigenous mobilization.

title: Women and Gender in Chinese History
description: An in-depth exploration of the history of women and gender in Modern China and the impact on that experience by factors such as class, ethnicity, and geography.

title: Women and Gender in Modern Africa
description: An in-depth exploration of the social, political, economic, religious, and cultural experiences of women living in Africa from the pre-colonial era to today.

title: Capstone Seminar in Global History
description: An advanced undergraduate research seminar on a topic in global history. Involves the creation and presentation of a substantial research paper and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Capstone Seminar in European History
description: An advanced undergraduate research seminar on a topic in European history. Involves the creation and presentation of a substantial research paper and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Capstone Seminar in U.S. History
description: An advanced undergraduate research seminar on a topic in U.S. history. Involves the creation and presentation of a substantial research paper and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Informal Learning at History Museums and Historic Sites
description: Explores how history is interpreted and presented in public settings, with a focus on history museums and historic sites as spaces of informal education. Students engage with public history theory, hands-on learning practices, and TEKS-aligned educational frameworks while analyzing how institutions address both engaging and difficult historical topics. Students gain practical experience creating exhibit interactives, educational resources, and programming that interpret history in meaningful, interdisciplinary ways.

title: European Women's History to 1200
description: An in-depth exploration of the history of the status and roles of women in European society from ancient Greece to the early medieval world. Emphasis on the ideas, customs, and laws that affected the lives of women, the roles that women performed, and how women both contributed to and were affected by European culture and society.

title: European Women's History since 1200
description: An in-depth exploration of the status and roles of women in European society from the Crusades to the present.

title: The Holocaust
description: An in-depth exploration of the history of Nazi Germany’s systematic mass murder of European Jews and other minorities during World War 2. Emphasis on anti-Semitism, the political cult of personality in Nazi Germany, the perpetrators of the Holocaust, Jewish reactions to the Holocaust, and the memory of the Holocaust

title: Totalitarian Regimes in Europe
description: An in-depth exploration of totalitarian regimes in Europe during the twentieth century. Major topics of concentration will include totalitarian states in Italy, Germany, and the USSR, the ideological foundations of totalitarianism, economics, personality cults, propaganda, wars, and the ways that ordinary people perceived of and opposed these regimes.

title: Readings in European History
description: A tutorial course for undergraduates with in-depth study of a given topic in European history. The nature and precise limits of the study will be established after consultation between professor and student. No student may take more than one history reading course in a given semester. A maximum of six semester hours of reading courses may apply toward a history major.

title: Advanced Special Topics in European History
description: A detailed investigation of a topic in European history not covered by regular course offerings. Involves independent research and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Advanced Special Topics in U.S. History
description: A detailed investigation of a topic in U.S. history not covered by regular course offerings. Involves independent research and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Advanced Special Topics in Global History
description: A detailed investigation of a topic in Global history not covered by regular course offerings. Involves independent research and may be repeated once for credit provided topic is different.

title: Readings in Global History
description: A tutorial course for undergraduates with in-depth study of a given topic in global history. The nature and precise limits of the study will be established after consultation between professor and student. No student may take more than one history reading course in a given semester. A maximum of six semester hours of reading courses may apply toward a history major.

title: The Latino Civil Rights Movement
description: An in-depth exploration of the major ideas, movements, and events in Latino Civil Rights history. Emphasis on the ways in which Latino communities have forged community, organized for civil rights, and claimed citizenship throughout the twentieth century.

title: The Vietnam War
description: An in-depth exploration of the origins, course, and legacy of the U.S. experience in the Vietnam War.

title: Readings in American History
description: A tutorial course for undergraduates with in-depth study of a given topic in American history. The nature and precise limits of the study will be established after consultation between professor and student. No student may take more than one history reading course in a given semester. A maximum of six semester hours of reading courses may apply toward a history major.

title: Advanced Model Organization of American States
description: Course prepares advanced students for leadership roles in a Model Organization of American States simulation. May be repeated for a total of six credit hours under different topics.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Theory and Practice in Gymnastics
description: An introductory skill course beginning with educational gymnastics and progressing to Olympic gymnastics.

title: Theory and Practice in Track and Field
description: An introductory skill course in track and field meeting during the spring semester of the freshman year.

title: Theory and Practice in Dance
description: A comprehensive skills course in the fundamentals of dance. Included are folk, aerobic, and modern dance skills.

title: Elementary School Physical Activities
description: This is an activity course for students interested in working with elementary age students. It is designed to introduce Baylor students to developmentally appropriate activities for elementary age children in the areas of fundamental skills, perceptual motor skills, games and sports skills, rhythmic activities and gymnastic skills. Through the context of movement, students will be exposed to the techniques, behaviors and approaches used by effective elementary physical education teachers.

title: Theory and Practice in Tumbling and Dance
description: A course designed to develop fundamental skills in tumbling and dance (folk, modern and aerobic) and to introduce students to the responsibilities of a cheerleader and/or drill team sponsor.

title: Fall Sports Officiating
description: A course designed for the individual desiring an in-depth understanding of the rules and regulations of football, volleyball, soccer, and basketball.

title: Driver Education and Training
description: A comprehensive driver education course covering all aspects of driving-learning to drive, traffic laws, mechanics of driving, the automobile, proper driving attitudes, and defensive driving. Upon successful completion of course requirements, students receive a Driver Training Certificate for insurance purposes.

title: First Aid
description: A course designed to teach the knowledge and techniques of first aid and emergency procedures. A Red Cross Standard First Aid Certificate is granted on completion.

title: Red Cross Water Safety
description: The most advanced aquatic course offered for certification by the American Red Cross. Prerequisites include prior certification in CPR and either Emergency Water Safety or Life Guarding. This course includes a review of swimming and lifesaving skills, analysis and remediation of stroke mechanics, lesson planning, classroom management, and teaching technique for the aquatic environment.

title: Dance Choreography
description: See required courses for the major.

title: Human Anatomy
description: An introductory course designed to teach the basic life systems in the human body. Detailed examination of the circulatory, muscular, skeletal, respiratory, and endocrine systems will be emphasized.

title: Introduction to Human Physiology
description: Provides the scientific foundation for the field of medicine, exercise physiology and all professions related to human health. Basic chemical and biological concepts will provide students the framework to comprehend physiological principles.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Fitness Concepts and Testing Procedures
description: The acquisition of essential skills and knowledge in fitness education that lead to excellent physical conditioning and the ability to administer a variety of fitness tests. Students must demonstrate specified levels of physical fitness to complete the course.

title: Advanced First Aid
description: Acquisition of skills and knowledge to qualify for Advanced First Aid certification through the American Red Cross or equivalent agency. Additional material in emergency care from a supplementary text is included. Practical application skills are performed.

title: Anatomical Kinesiology
description: A course designed to develop an understanding of human movement through the study of neuromuscular control, anatomical structures, and mechanical principles.

title: Biomechanics
description: An overview of the principles and methods of analyzing human movement from a mechanical and quantitative perspective.

title: Medical Conditions and Pharmacology
description: Athletic Training Majors Only. This course covers the evaluation and prevention of the most common medical conditions. It also covers medications commonly encountered in the practice of physical medicine. It will include categories of drugs; generic and trade names of common drugs; the use, effects and precautions of common drugs; and drug-drug-interactions and pharmacokinetic principles.

title: Prevention and Care of Athletic Injuries
description: The course is designed to teach the basic athletic training needs, to teach fundamental skills and methods in athletic training, and to train an individual to take care of both injured and uninjured athletes.

title: Emergency Medical Technician - Basic
description: This is a Texas Department of Health approved course leading to state certification as an Emergency Medical technician. The class requires classroom and field studies in applicable skills and environment. National Registry Exam available on completion.

title: Exercise Physiology
description: This course provides lecture and lab experience in that aspect of sports medicine which involves the study of the body's response, adjustments, and adaptations to exercise.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Innovative Games and Adventure Activities
description: Designed to assist the student in studies of the mechanics and techniques of each activity. Includes the skills, rules, principles and purposes of each activity. First semester sophomores and above are eligible to enroll in this course.

title: Pedagogy of Team Sports
description: This course is designed to provide practice in teaching skills, to increase knowledge of games and difference sports rules, and to develop an appreciation for the basic skills in a variety of team sports. First semester sophomores and above are eligible to enroll in this course.

title: Pedagogy of Individual Sports
description: This course is designed to provide practice in teaching skills, to increase knowledge of the games and different sports rules, and to develop an appreciation for the basic skills in a variety of individual sports. First semester sophomores and above are eligible to enroll in this course.

title: Pedagogy of Dance
description: Course includes history, pedagogy, curriculum design, music selection, costuming, creative activities, and dance resources. Field experience includes observation, journal entries, and assisting in a dance environment.

title: Stress, Resilience & Human Performance
description: This course will examine evidence-based coping strategies relevant to health, human performance and well-being in many different settings. Settings would range from coaching, participation in sport, basic job demands, sport medicine, or similar fields. Students will develop personal and professional strategies for managing stress, promoting resilience both in their lives and in the lives of those with whom they will be working with specific to these areas.

title: Essentials of Ethics in Healthcare
description: This course presents students with the opportunity to explore current healthcare ethical dilemmas, consider bias, and participate in the formulation of Christian responses in difficult healthcare situations. REL 1310 The Christian Scriptures and REL 1350 The Christian Heritage are prerequisites.

title: Introduction to Standardized Patient Methodology
description: Prior to enrollment students must audition and be selected. In this course students will be provided an introduction to the methodology of standardized patient encounters. Students will learn to be Simulation Performers (e.g., patients, family members) to provide standardized, real-life healthcare scenarios in the training of healthcare providers to improve medical and communication skills.

title: Fundamentals of Exercise Biochemistry
description: Credit or concurrent enrollment in HP 2420 .  HHPR (EP, HSS, PHSS, HKLS, PEDA, REC) and Public Health (PH) majors only.       An overview of biochemistry and metabolism related to exercise, training adaptations, and nutrition.

title: Research Methods and Design in Exercise Science
description: Introduction to research methods in exercise science including research designs, collection of data with appropriate statistical techniques, as well as the critique of peer reviewed research.

title: Elementary School Physical Education
description: This course is designed for elementary education majors (EC-6 Generalist) and for those interested in working with young children.  Students are introduced to the techniques and approaches used by effective physical education teachers as they design and implement elementary physical education programs.  Class time is spent in the classroom, the gym, and in local schools exploring how children learn motor skills and adopt physically active lifestyles.

title: Adapted Human Performance
description: A study of the theory of corrective human performance with special emphasis on the analysis of movement and postural deviations and the choice of and application of exercises and activities for their correction.

title: Perceptual Motor Skills Development
description: This course will identify normal developmental sequences and performance patterns of primary and intermediate age children. The emphasis will be the correlation of this information with skills.

title: Fundamentals of Human Performance
description: This course examines the goals, objectives, and evaluation of exemplary programs.

title: Risk Management in HHPR
description: Course explores the legal principles and rules of laws affecting administration and operation of health, physical education, recreation, sports programs, resources, areas, and facilities. Risk management and legal concepts are applied to contracts, human rights, property, products liabilities, negligence, and torts.

title: Clinical Research Collaboration
description: See HED 3V50 for course description.

title: Seminar in HHPR
description: hrs.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Choreography and Production
description: This course is designed to enhance a student's performance skills in modern, ballet, and jazz dance. "Choreography" will include original routines using music selection, development of choreography, costuming, lighting, peer teaching, and written step instructions. "Production" will include performance in class as well as experience in production of a show at the end of the semester.

title: Exercise and Sport Nutrition
description: An overview of the role of nutrition as a means to enhance health and performance in exercise and sport. Topics to be covered include principles of healthful nutrition, energy metabolism, and nutrients, regulation of metabolism by vitamins and minerals, weight control, and analysis of the validity of proposed nutritional ergogenic aids.

title: Techniques of Strength Training and Conditioning
description: This course is to study current principles and procedures essential to strength training and conditioning practices. Emphasis is placed on the development and practical applications of aerobic conditioning, joint flexibility, muscular strength, power, and endurance programs.

title: Philosophy, Principles, and Administration of Human Performance
description: Understanding of the fundamental principles and philosophy of human performance as they relate to program development, methodology, organization, and administration. Historical concepts, objectives, and purposes of human performance are also emphasized.

title: Motor Learning and Development
description: HHPR (EP, HSS, PHSS, HKLS, PEDA, REC) and Public Health (PH) majors only.                      Skill learning and skill performance, covering the gradual development of human physical abilities over the centuries. The states of an individual's development from childhood to maturity will be studied. Included are factors affecting physical development, the nature of the learning process, and the methods suitable for both the early stages of skill learning and the advanced skill levels.

title: Sports Psychology
description: Examines psychological theories and techniques applied to sport to enhance the performance and personal growth of athletes and coaches. Lecture with an emphasis on discussion. The course is designed to help both present and future coaches to use sound psychological principles to be more effective. Emphasis is given to understanding personality, motivation, confidence, discipline, imagery use, psyching techniques, relaxation training, anxiety and choking, attention and concentration, the psychology of injury and rehabilitation, and clinical issues common in athletics.

title: Successful Coaching
description: The course is designed to provide a foundation in the practical application of sport science and management. Specifically, the class will address the sport psychology, sport pedagogy, sport physiology and sport management aspects of successful coaching.  A 20-hour field-based experience with a middle or high school sports team is required.

title: Sociology of Sport
description: A course that studies the interaction of various social influences such as the mass media, economics, race, gender, religion, group behavior, politics, and education on sport.

title: Exercise Prescription in Health and Disease
description: Emphasis will be given to developing exercise programs for individuals with chronic diseases and disabilities. This course will enable the student to apply the skills learned to writing exercise prescriptions for healthy and diseased populations.

title: Qualitative Analysis of Human Movement
description: Advanced instruction in qualitative analysis of human movement, with training in methods of perceiving and measuring errors of movement in a variety of environments (teaching, coaching, and clinical evaluation).

title: Facilities and Equipment in HHPR
description: Structural standards, operation, maintenance, function, design, and planning of human performance and recreation resources, areas, and facilities; and the selection, purchasing, and care of equipment appropriate to these programs and services.

title: Religion and Sport
description: See REL 4396 for course information.

title: Sports-Related Ethics
description: Perspective of the Judeo-Christian tradition of the moral concerns related to sports in American society. Various dimensions of the organized sports phenomenon, including those at the youth, secondary school, intercollegiate, and professional level. This course will not be accepted for credit toward a major or minor in religion or toward the six hour religion requirement by the University.

title: Exercise Testing and Interpretation I
description: Lectures will address the principles of common physical assessments used in clinical and fitness settings, the selection of appropriate assessments, results interpretation and the application of assessment results for exercise prescription and chronic disease risk reduction. Laboratory experiences are designed to develop competencies in physical assessment and results interpretation.

title: Exercise Testing and Interpretation II
description: Lectures and laboratory experiences build on information covered in HP 4450 . This course is designed to develop and refine core competencies in conducting common physical assessments and interpreting results for exercise prescription and chronic disease risk reduction. Laboratory experiences include student engagement in comprehensive health and fitness profiles.

title: Internship in Human Performance
description: The internship provides hands-on experience for the human performance major in the area of his or her concentration. The experience includes a special project determined jointly by the student and the agency intern supervisor.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: New Student Seminar in Human Sciences and Design
description: History, interdependence and integrative nature of HSD program areas and their role in the study of health and human sciences. A New Student Experience (NSE) course examining techniques for academic success, finding purpose and connection with Baylor, and professional and career options.

title: Design and Creative Technologies
description: The study of art and design principles through the use of creative technologies including image creation and editing software, digital sketching and illustration, and project management systems, to develop communication and marketing materials.

title: Directed Study in Human Sciences and Design
description: Individual study of selected areas of interest in human sciences and design.

title: Directed Study in Human Sciences and Design
description: Individual study of selected areas of interest in human sciences and design.

title: Applied Undergraduate Research in Human Sciences and Design
description: In-depth undergraduate research. This class is intended for application-oriented, individualized professor-supervised undergraduate research applications.

title: Global Perspectives in Housing
description: Research and exploration in housing differences around the world as a factor of cultural, political, environmental, economical and social influences.

title: Special Studies in Human Sciences and Design
description: Current issues in human sciences and design will be examined through seminars, special topics, practicums and individual research experiences. May be repeated with different content for a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Interior Design Graphics I
description: Introduction to basic techniques of architectural drafting with an emphasis on industry standards, architectural symbols, lettering, and line weights.  Graphics will focus on interior floor plans, elevations, sections, details, and axonometric drawings.

title: Interior Design Graphics
description: Introduction to techniques of graphic communication for interior architecture & design. Emphasis is placed on reading and understanding architectural graphic communication, learning techniques for producing architectural orthographic drawings, axonometric drawings, and one and two-point perspective drawings utilizing graphic standards. Students will learn skills for field measuring and quick hand sketching as communication methods.

title: Introduction to Interior Design
description: An introductory survey of the Interior Design profession orienting students to an evidence-based approach to the design process, including programming, spatial relationships, furniture and finishes, universal and accessible design, and sustainability. Particular attention is given to learning the principles and elements of design.

title: Studio I: Foundations of Design Communication
description: Introduction to techniques of graphic communication for interior architecture & design. Emphasis is placed on reading and understanding architectural graphic communication, learning techniques for producing architectural orthographic drawings, axonometric drawings, and one and two-point perspective drawings utilizing graphic standards. Students will learn skills for field measuring and quick hand sketching as communication methods.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: SketchUp for ID
description: Introduction to basic techniques of SketchUp modeling.

title: Second Year Review
description: Successful completion of the Second Year Review with a minimum GPA of 2.85 in their Art and Interior Design Courses. Students will not be allowed to register for upper-level studios unless they have completed this requirement.

title: Building Materials and Finishes
description: Examines materials, finishes, and textiles used in the built environment with an emphasis on performance characteristics, testing standards, and sustainability. Students evaluate product applications by considering life cycle and supply chain impacts while integrating cultural and global influences.

title: Digital Representation I: Architectural Drawing & Modeling
description: Introduction to digital methods of drafting, modeling, and visual communication. Students produce and annotate orthographic and paraline drawings in accordance with professional graphic standards, progressing toward three-dimensional modeling and the creation of coordinated drawing sets that communicate design intent.

title: Building Systems
description: Study of structures, building materials, construction techniques, mechanical, plumbing and electrical systems, and their impact on the design of the built environment.

title: Building Codes & Regulatory Systems
description: Study of building and interior codes used in the design of the built environment, which includes model codes, standards, regulations, Life Safety, ADA, sustainability, electrical, and plumbing.

title: Architectural & Design History I
description: Survey of influential architectural movements, interiors, furnishings, and the decorative arts related to major cultures from Antiquity through the early 19th century, with special emphasis on periods of design influenced by Classicism.

title: Architectural & Design History II
description: Survey of architectural and design movements from the turn of the nineteenth century to the late twentieth century. The course examines relationships among architecture, interiors, furnishings, and the decorative arts within their social, cultural, and technological contexts.

title: Studio II: Space Planning
description: Introduction to the methods of spatial organization and planning that shape interior environments. Students apply anthropometrics, ergonomics, and proxemics to develop design solutions through iterative modeling.

title: Studio III: Digital Communication & Visualization
description: Studio builds skills in visual communication for design of the built environment. Students learn to craft clear, compelling graphic narratives through renderings, diagrams, and presentation layouts. Emphasis is placed on composition, clarity, and impact to produce professional-quality presentations that effectively convey design intent.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Professional Presentation & Portfolio Design
description: A preparatory course for the undergraduate internship focused on the development of the design portfolio and establishing professional habits for the emerging design professional through interviewing and communication skills.

title: Theory of Architecture & Design
description: Course explores key theories that shape architectural and interior design practices. Students will examine historical and contemporary frameworks addressing cultural, social, environmental, and technological dimensions of design. Through readings, discussions, and applied analysis, the course highlights the connection between theory and design processes. Students will apply these theories to analyze, critique, and inform design decisions.

title: Lighting Systems & Design
description: Focuses on lighting principles, technologies, and applications in architectural design, with emphasis on technical specifications, documentation, and implementation.

title: Digital Representation II: BIM & 3D Modeling
description: Focus on 3D modeling and documentation using Building Information Modeling (BIM). Through hands-on projects, students learn to create and manage models, apply materials and lighting, and produce coordinated drawing sets. The course prepares students to integrate BIM workflows into professional practice.

title: Studio IV: Contextual & Human Centered Design
description: Application of design for residential and small-scale commercial projects informed by research and user needs. Students integrate context, programming, and human factors to create designs responsive to cultural and environmental conditions. Projects address materials, budgets, and collaborative workflows to prepare for advanced studios.

title: Studio V: Creative Problem Solving in Commercial Environments
description: Studio exploring design of commercial environments with advanced programming and code integration. Students engage in conceptual development and creative problem-solving while addressing life safety, accessibility, and sustainability. Emphasis is placed on applying building codes, material and finish specifications, and lighting systems.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Interior Design Capstone Research
description: Introduction to methods and techniques of interior design research.  Students develop a research framework which they will apply to the design of their capstone project the following semester. Various types of qualitative, quantitative, and historical methods of design research will be discussed.

title: Capstone Research
description: Introduction to research approaches that inform the design process. Students investigate cultural, historical, and environmental contexts to analyze how precedent, place, and the built environment shape design outcomes, developing a framework to guide their capstone project.

title: Professional Practice
description: Survey of business practices within the interior design profession, including business development, contracts, and ethical responsibilities. Topics cover selling services, managing obligations, and global factors shaping the profession.

title: Sustainable Systems & Design
description: A study of sustainable design practices emphasizing health, resilience, and environmental stewardship. Students examine chemical exposure, indoor air quality, the microbiome, and building performance, and apply frameworks such as LEED, WELL, Fitwel, and GreenGuard to understand how these systems influence design decisions and profession

title: Interior Design Internship
description: Supervised internship in an approved design-related work environment. Minimum of 240 hours.

title: Studio VI: Complex Environments
description: An advanced studio focused on complex commercial and institutional projects. Students address programming, research, and concept development while integrating life safety, environmental performance, and universal design. Projects emphasize codes, materials, lighting, and interdisciplinary collaboration.

title: Studio VII: Capstone
description: A culminating studio synthesizing research, design, and coordination of spatial, material, and building systems into a comprehensive project. Students demonstrate professional readiness through detailed documentation, clear visual communication, and polished presentations.

title: Special Topics in Interior Design
description: Current issues in Interior Design will be examined through seminars, special topics, practicums, and individual research experiences. May be repeated with different content for a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Integrated Science: Physics, Earth & Space Science
description: Prepares students to convey concepts in physics, earth, and space science.  Contains integrated lab component.

title: Integrated Science: Chemistry & Life Science
description: Prepares students to convey concepts in chemistry and life science.  Contains integrated lab component.

title: Global Business
description: An introductory survey for all majors, exploring how companies and nations operate in the global economy. Topics include the cultural, legal, financial, logistical, and ethical challenges that companies encounter in the global market and the strategies designed to address those challenges. Current global developments and company practices are emphasized.

title: Business in the Global Economy
description: An overview of the global economy, with an emphasis on how current developments affect international business. A brief history of foreign trade, basic international trade theory, instruments of trade policy and their economic effects, trade policies for developing nations, regional economic integration arrangements, balance of payments, and foreign exchange markets. A student may not earn credit in both ECO 3331 and INB 3332 . BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course

title: Doing Business Across Cultures
description: A practical survey for all majors of how business practices vary across different cultures. Ways of doing business are compared and contrasted on a regional basis, including cultures in Europe, Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America. Case discussion is emphasized. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: International Entrepreneurship
description: See ENT 3350 for course information.

title: Global Business Strategy
description: A survey on the formulation of international business strategy to magnify firms’ competitive advantages in global markets. Introduces basic economic frameworks to understand the relationships between policies and domestic and global strategies to guide international business decisions. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: International Business Law
description: Legal issues relating to international business transactions. Topics include major organizations and conventions/treaties related to international business, differences between U.S. and foreign contract and employment laws, methods of entry into foreign markets such as direct foreign investment or franchising, techniques to minimize risk in international transactions, the need for cultural awareness as it affects business transactions, trade regulations including import and export requirements and tariffs, and how to resolve disputes effectively. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: International Marketing
description: A study of the international dimensions of American enterprise and the background of the international environment. Includes international trade concepts, cultural dynamics, business customs, multinational markets, development markets, and influence of political, legal, and geographic factors on international marketing. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: International Trade Theory and Policy
description: World economy with particular emphasis upon international trade system and the trend toward economic regionalism. Basic theories of international trade are developed as a framework for analysis of policy issues.

title: International Business Finance
description: A study of international financial management, including multinational corporations (MNCs), international financial markets, international trade, foreign exchange rate, currency futures, forwards, options and swaps, currency exposures, and short- and long-term financing decisions of MNCs. With a focus on the enhancement of analytical skills based on the tools and theory of international finance, this course will promote critical thinking skills of the student. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Economic Problems of Latin America
description: Evolution of the economies of Latin America with particular emphasis on present structures and policies. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Foreign Exchange Markets
description: Description and analysis of currency markets, emphasizing their roles in the business environment. Topics include spot forward, futures, and options markets, currency, and interest rate swaps, models of exchange rate determination, the operation and relative merits of fixed and flexible exchange rate systems, and the management of exchange rate risk by business. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Economic Development
description: Critical analysis of current explanations of economic growth and development, involving historical aspects, policies for achieving development in emerging countries, and conditions necessary for continued growth in advanced countries. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Global Trade Compliance
description: A practical introduction to the regulatory requirements that firms must fulfill to participate in international trade. The course will survey the role of trade compliance in the operations of international firms, an overview of U.S. export and import regulatory regimes and agencies, discussion of selected case studies, and information on the additional requirements for a career in global trade compliance.

title: Internship in International Business
description: Internship in International Business fulfilled in cooperation with an approved organization. With the sponsoring organization, the student participates in formal training and education and directed activities in international business. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Special Studies in International Business
description: Independent study under the direct supervision of an international business faculty member. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Special Studies in International Business
description: With the permission of the chair of International Business, this course may be taken for one to six hours of credit. BBA students must have a declared major to enroll in this course.

title: Japanese Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Japanese Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Introductory Japanese
description: Introduction to the Japanese language and culture; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Designed to help students progress through novice proficiency levels

title: Elementary Japanese
description: Introduction to the Japanese language and culture; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Designed to help students progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Intermediate Japanese
description: Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading) to help students attain intermediate-level proficiency in Japanese.

title: Exploring Japan
description: Practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading) to help students develop language skills in Japanese at the Intermediate Level.  Explores aspects of Japanese culture and society.

title: Japanese Culture through Reading
description: Development of reading skills in Japanese through selected texts that focus on some aspect of Japanese culture, including food, literature, cultural identity, current events, and film and criticism. May be repeated once for a maximum of 6 hours credit when topic varies.

title: Japanese Conversation and Composition
description: Further development of communication skills through conversation and composition.

title: Japanese Civilization
description: Japanese history and culture in the target language.

title: Japanese for the Professions
description: Development of cultural competence and Japanese language proficiency in business and other professional workplace situations.  Includes authentic and current materials to develop understanding of the business practices, corporations and various industries in Japan.

title: Japanese Cinema
description: Critical analysis of Japanese films from 1950s to present.  Designed to develop cultural competence and Japanese proficiency through discussions and presentations.

title: Topics in Japanese Society
description: Topics in Japanese Society is offered through Baylor in Japan Summer Program. Through project-based experiential learning and Japanese language study, students engage in a topic that relates to their majors and interests while in Japan.

title: Japanese Theatre and Culture
description: See THEA 3352 for course information.

title: Special Topics in Japanese
description: Special topics in Japanese language, literature, and culture. With content changed, may be repeated for a total of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Special Topics in Japanese
description: An independent study course in Japanese language and culture or literature on a topic selected by the student in consultation with the instructor. With content changed, this course may be repeated once for up to six semester hours of credit.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Italian Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Italian Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Elementary Italian
description: Introduction to Italian language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Elementary Italian
description: Introduction to Italian language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Intermediate Italian
