description: Introduction to Italian language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency.

title: Pathways in Italian Culture
description: An exploration of the numerous facets of Italian culture including art, history, geography, and influence while expanding upon the language skills developed in the preceding three semesters.

title: Mastering Italian Grammar
description: Review of familiar grammar and the study of grammatical structures in complex communication.

title: Italian Conversation, Reading, and Composition
description: Development of communication skills through the study of Italian civilization, culture, and selected works of literature.

title: Italian Food Culture
description: Survey of the rich landscape of Italian gastronomy from the Roman Empire to present times.

title: Italian Pop Culture
description: Popular culture in Italy as represented in film, music, popular literature, and mass media.

title: Introduction to Italian Literature
description: Introduction to Italian literature through the study of selected works.

title: Conversation and Composition in Tuscany
description: Further development of oral and written skills within the context of the Baylor in Tuscany program.

title: Italian Through Film
description: Development of communication skills through viewing and discussing Italian movies.

title: Italian Civilization
description: The social, cultural, historical, and intellectual aspects of Italian civilization from its origin to the present.

title: Giro d'Italia: Regions of Italy
description: The geographic, historical, cultural, and economic aspects of Italy's regions.

title: Florence and Its Cultural Treasures
description: The history and cultural heritage of Florence, from antiquity to the present, within the context of the Baylor in Tuscany program.

title: Special Problems
description: Independent study in Italian language or literature. With content changed, this course may be repeated once for credit.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Korean Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Korean Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Elementary Korean
description: Introduction to the Korean language and culture; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Designed to help students progress through novice proficiency levels

title: Elementary Korean
description: Introduction to the Korean language and culture; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Designed to help students progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Intermediate Korean
description: Designed to help students attain intermediate-level proficiency in Korean. Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).

title: Intermediate Korean II
description: This course will continue to help students develop spoken and communicative skills in Korean at the Intermediate Level. Practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).

title: Introduction to Mass Communication
description: See FDM 1303 for course information.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Media Literacy in a Democratic Society
description: Designed to increase media knowledge and function and improve ability to discern reliability of sources.

title: Reporting and Writing for Media
description: Professional writing skills used for a variety of media. Research and interviewing techniques for storytelling for multiple mediums. Evaluation of different media for content, bias, ethics, news values, diversity and objectivity.

title: Multimedia Storytelling: Crafting Digital Narratives Across Platforms
description: Explores storytelling across digital platforms using photography, audio, video and graphics. Students learn to craft compelling, audience-driven narratives for journalism, advertising and public relations. Emphasizes hands-on projects with industry tools.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Sports Media Writing
description: Historical and multi-media perspective on sports reporting through lectures and reporting assignments covering intercollegiate athletic competition.

title: Broadcast Writing and Reporting
description: Not open to Pre-Film and Digital Media students. The theory and practice of broadcast news reporting including gathering, critically processing, and enterprising unique stories with compelling video and storytelling.

title: Advertising Procedures
description: See MKT 3320 for course information.

title: Advertising Copywriting
description: The process of developing effective advertising copy for the mass media, including concept generation, research, copy testing, advertising execution, media placement and assessment.

title: Editing
description: Development of skills and resources used by editors in preparing content for publication in the print or digital world. Emphasis on editing and design using relevant programs. Also covers legal and ethical issues as well as diversity in the newsroom.

title: Media Photography
description: This course builds upon the foundational visual skills developed in the Multimedia Storytelling survey course. Students will delve deeper into the practical application of photographic theory, focusing on camera operation, lighting techniques, and composition, and editing through hands-on laboratory exercises. Assignments will explore a range of media, utilizing both still and motion visuals – with a particular emphasis on developing a strong visual narrative. Laboratory required.

title: Photojournalism
description: Building upon the foundational photographic skills developed in ‘Media Photography,’ this course immerses students in the practice of photojournalism. Students will undertake individual projects exploring real-world issues, focusing on location assignments and documentary work designed for publication. This course moves beyond basic technique, demanding critical observation, ethical storytelling, and the ability to capture compelling narratives through visual documentation. Laboratory work will be utilized to refine skills in image selection, sequencing, and the overall impact of a photojournalistic story. Laboratory required.

title: Foundations of Videography
description: Master the fundamentals of video production – from camera operation and lighting to editing and visual storytelling. This course focuses on developing your skills in creating compelling visual content through composition, movement, and a strong understanding of visual techniques.

title: Public Relations for Non-Profit Organizations
description: Communication techniques, public relations, news writing and advertising procedures applied to needs of non-profit organizations or local churches. Includes laboratory work, research, and individual and team projects in social service or church public relations.

title: Principles of Advertising and Public Relations
description: Professional communication principles applied in contemporary public relations and advertising concepts. Theory, history, and practice of public relations and advertising, including persuasion, evaluation of public opinion, and the planning, implementing, and evaluating of public relations and advertising programs.

title: Design for Social and Digital Media
description: Introduction to foundational graphic design principles within the context of contemporary media landscapes. Students gain hands-on experience with the Adobe Creative Suite as well as other industry-leading tools like Figma. Through practical exercises, students learn core concepts of design and typography while exploring digital approaches to publishing for public relations, advertising and journalism. This entry-level course assumes little prior experience with professional tools, but provides a solid foundation for future design exploration.

title: Public Relations Agency
description: Application of public relations principles in an agency setting.

title: Writing for Media Markets
description: Not open to Journalism, Public Relations, and New Media majors or minors. Not open to Pre-Film and Digital Media majors.  Specialized writing for media markets, including public relations releases, brochures, news reports, and speeches. Credit may not be earned for this course if credit is earned in JOU 2303 .

title: Strategic Writing and Design
description: The use of audience, market, and media analyses to create relevant messaging and related designs in the advertising and public relations professions.

title: Advanced Reporting and Writing
description: Experience in news gathering and writing with emphasis on relationships with professional news sources. Lariat laboratory included.

title: History of American Journalism
description: Development, impact, and importance of communication groups and of individual journalists and media in the United States.

title: Editorial and Column Writing
description: Fundamentals of writing informative and persuasive editorials and in writing appealing newspaper columns.

title: International Communication
description: International communication: its history, the flow of news and information, its role in international relations, a comparison of press systems, the role of new World Information Order, and communication in developed and developing nations.

title: Magazine and Feature Writing
description: The nature and functions of magazine and feature article writing and editing, with emphasis on identifying article ideas and taking projects through the writing, editing and placement process.

title: Sports Media Internship
description: Open to Sports Media majors on approved internships with various media under faculty supervision. Record of work done and report of media supervisors required.

title: News Internship
description: Open to journalism majors on approved internships with various media under faculty supervision. Record of work done and report of media supervisors required.

title: Photo Internship
description: Open to journalism majors on approved internships with various media under faculty supervision. Record of work done and report of media supervisors required.

title: Religious Communication Internship
description: Open to journalism majors on approved internships with various media under faculty supervision. Record of work done and report of media supervisors required.

title: Advertising Internship
description: Open to journalism majors on approved internships in advertising.

title: Public Relations Internship
description: Open to journalism majors on approved internships in public relations.

title: Magazine Publishing
description: In-depth examination of magazine function, research, copy editing and layout, with practice in each. Strong emphasis on editorial decision-making and formulation of magazine editorial policy. Consumer, special interest, professional, trade, and company-sponsored magazines are studied.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Global Advertising & Public Relations
description: Encourages students to shift their thinking from Western-centered ways of practicing public relations and advertising in order to understand how to effectively communicate with stakeholders in other nations with different cultures, government structures and channels of communication.

title: Media Management
description: The theory and methods of modern media management, including advertising sales and production, news-editorial organization and operations, and business and distribution functions.

title: Internship - Baylor in New York
description: Provides undergraduate students enrolled in the Baylor in New York program the opportunity for application of film & digital media skills and knowledge carried out under the supervision of a professional employer in a media-related organization.

title: Professional Development - Baylor in New York
description: Not open to Pre-Film and Digital Media students. Provides students enrolled in the Baylor in New York program opportunities to expand their professional development in their chosen field under the supervision of the professor and in conjunction with their work in a media-related professional field.

title: Communication and Culture
description: Not open to Pre-Film and Digital Media students. An examination of the reciprocal functions of communication and urban culture as they continue to shape and define each other. Specific areas of inquiry vary each semester. May be repeated once with a different topic (maximum six semester hours).

title: Advanced Visual Production
description: Building on your foundational skills in photography and media creation, this course focuses on advanced lighting techniques (studio and location) and the development of a comprehensive portfolio. Students will explore self-promotion strategies and refine their ability to craft impactful visual narratives for a professional audience.

title: Social Media for Strategic Communication and Journalism
description: Examination of social media in public relations, advertising, journalism, organizations, and personal branding. Development of social media plans and use of analytical tools for products, services or organizations.

title: Social Media for Sports: Fan Engagement and Digital Strategy
description: Explores the dynamic intersection of sports and social media, including strategies for fan engagement, athlete branding, and content creation across social media platforms. Students gain hands-on experience developing campaigns, analyzing metrics, and managing live event coverage to drive audience growth and amplify sports narratives.

title: Advanced Broadcast Reporting & Producing
description: Focuses on advanced skills for compelling broadcast stories with strong characters and storytelling skills. Uses news values to build newscast segments, link stories and supporting content, including graphics, data lists and maps.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: An Introduction to Latin American Studies
description: An interdisciplinary introduction to Latin American Studies.

title: Introduction to Model Organization of American States
description: Course prepares students for first-time participation in a Model Organization of American States simulation. Stresses historical and contemporary hemispheric issues. May be repeated for a total of six credit hours under different topics.

title: Special Studies in Latin American Studies
description: Studies

title: Inter-American Court of Human Rights (IACHR) Moot Court
description: Prepares students to represent the State or Petitioner in a Human Rights Moot Court competition. Stresses legal research of the Inter-American Human Rights Convention and national law as well as courtroom argumentation. May be repeated for a total of six credit hours under different topics. (Spring).

title: Latin American Studies Seminar
description: An interdisciplinary seminar to focus on topics related to Mexico, Argentina, Brazil, Central America, and developing Latin American nations. This course can be taken more than once since its content is different every year.

title: Traditional Music and Culture in Latin America
description: See MUS 4364 for course information.

title: Advanced Reading and Research in Latin American Studies
description: Advanced reading and research in Latin American studies. The content and methodology of this course should be approved by the Latin American Studies Committee.

title: Advanced Model Organization of American States
description: Course prepares advanced students for leadership roles in a Model Organization of American States simulation. May be repeated for a total of six credit hours under different topics.

title: Elementary Latin I
description: The basic elements of classical Latin grammar and Roman culture, with readings emphasizing ethics, history, morality, philosophy, and mythology.

title: Elementary Latin II
description: Continuation of LAT 1301 .

title: Beginning Latin
description: Introduction to the Latin language including the same inflection, syntax and easy reading as LAT 1301 . Teaching strategies differ from those of the traditional Latin class, including more contact hours and a variety of teaching techniques with emphasis on repetition of particular structures.

title: Beginning Latin
description: Introduction to the Latin language including the same inflection, syntax and easy reading as LAT 1302 . Teaching strategies differ from those of the traditional Latin class, including more contact hours and a variety of teaching techniques with emphasis on repetition of particular structures.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Conversational Latin
description: Build fluency and understanding in Latin by learning to use Latin actively, in speech and in writing, enabling students to read and understand written Latin texts more quickly and easily.

title: Intermediate Latin Prose
description: Selections from Latin prose authors, such as Cicero; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Intermediate Latin
description: Selections from Julius Caesar; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Intermediate Latin Poetry
description: Selections from Latin poets, such as Virgil and Catullus; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Intermediate Latin (Prose)
description: Selections from representative Latin authors, including Caesar, Cicero, and Apuleius; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Intermediate Latin (Poetry)
description: Selections from representative Latin authors including Vergil, Ovid, Catullus, Propertius, and Tibullus; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Lucretius
description: Selections from the De Rerum Natura.

title: Livy
description: Selections from the Roman historian Livy.

title: Tacitus
description: Selections from the Roman historian Tacitus.

title: Pliny and Martial
description: Selections from the Letters and the Epigrams.

title: Cicero, Philosophical Writings
description: Selections from the philosophical writings of Cicero.

title: Sallust
description: Detailed study of selections from the works of Sallust.

title: Julius Caesar: Reading the Ancient Sources
description: Readings on the life and times of Gaius Julius Caesar.

title: Readings in Imperial Epic
description: Study of imperial epic poetry (selections from Lucan, Statius, Silius, Valerius Flaccus) and its significance for the development of Roman imperial ideology.

title: Ovid's Metamorphoses
description: Study of Ovid's epic poem, Metamorphoses.

title: Epistles and Epigraphy of Rome
description: Latin inscriptions and selected letters of Cicero, Seneca, and Pliny.

title: Augustine
description: Selected readings of St. Augustine.

title: Seneca
description: Study of the works of Seneca the Younger.

title: Roman Comedy
description: Selected plays of Plautus and Terence.

title: Roman Lyric and Elegy
description: Selections from the poetry of Catullus, Propertius, Tibullus, and Ovid.

title: Roman Satire
description: Selected satires of Horace and Juvenal.

title: Horace
description: Selections from the poetry of Horace.

title: Medieval Latin Prose
description: Selections from representative prose authors, including Peter Abelard, Bede, Ekkehard of Aura, Petrarch, Erasmus, Thomas More; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Medieval Latin Poetry
description: Selections from representative poetic styles and poets; authors may include St. Columba, Fortunatus, Paulus Diaconus, Reinardus Vulpes, Walter Map, Hrotsvitha of Gandersheim, and Gulielmus Gnapheus; review of inflection and syntax.

title: Classical and Medieval Drama: Terence and Hrotswitha of Gandersheim
description: A Comparative Reading in Latin of selections of Terence's six comedies and Hrotswitha's six medieval plays on Christian themes, inspired by Terence and transformed into a Christian context.

title: Medieval Latin Religious Drama
description: A Reading in Latin of selections of mystery, morality, and miracle plays from the 10th to the 14th century on Old and New Testament topics.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Latin Language Seminar
description: Latin grammar, morphology, syntax, and phonology, prose composition, and reading of theoretical texts and analysis of Latin words in passages from ancient authors.

title: Latin Prose Composition
description: Practice in analysis and composition of Latin prose to improve grammar and syntax and promote knowledge and appreciation of Latin prose style.

title: Statius in Medieval and Renaissance Latin Epic
description: The influence of Statius and his masterpiece, the Thebaid on Medieval Latin and vernacular epics.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Children's Literature
description: An exploration of the development of a distinct literature for children. An analysis of books and illustrations for preschool through the elementary level.  Reading, evaluation, and application of the various media for children.

title: Curriculum Applications in Children's Literature
description: Research in the field of children's literature and its application to the elementary classroom, emphasizing activity approaches and curriculum planning.

title: Multicultural Literature
description: Students will explore a framework for examining the impact and influence of cultural, linguistic, and ethnic diversity in literature. Instructional lessons and activities are developed with the goal of enhancing students' critical lens for increasing culturally relevant and affirming practices that are authentic, intentional, and engaging for learners.

title: Young People's Literature
description: Reading and reacting to print and non-print media relevant to interests and problems of adolescents. Attention to interest and maturity levels, developmental values, curriculum uses, discrimination of literary tastes, and the dynamic significance of literature for young people.

title: Introduction to Leadership
description: Provides insights into a student's present leadership strengths and developmental needs. Prepares students for leadership positions, focusing on topics such as a philosophy of leadership, the role of power and authority in leadership, and goal setting for leadership development.

title: Leadership and Vocation
description: This course explores personal strengths, values and aspirations for influencing the world through a unique vocation or calling. Through this experience, students will become committed to their own personal growth as a human being, as well as to the personal growth of others in the class. In addition, applications of a variety of historical and contemporary perspectives on vocation and calling will be emphasized.

title: Christian Leadership in Residential Communities
description: The purpose of this class is to explore how Christian Leadership can be used to develop residential communities.   Specifically, we will explore how authentic Christian community is formed by leaders who employ techniques related to spiritual wellness, God-given strengths, relational approaches, inclusivity, and other aspects of community development.  Using the Community Leader position at Baylor University as an experiential framework, this class will provide both philosophical and application opportunities to hone a student’s ability to be a Christian leader of communities.

title: Organizational Leadership
description: This course is designed for student leaders with a desire to explore organizational theory and systems.  The course will further develop a student’s understanding of how these systems and theories influence culture, behaviors, and organizational effectiveness.  Students will examine their own leadership styles and how that approach fits into the organization, its members, and mission.

title: Peer Leadership for Health and Wellness
description: This course uses an experiential pedagogy to broaden students’ development as leaders.  Experiential learning provides a comprehensive approach to learning that integrates theory, practice, implementation, and reflection.  Through your experience as peer leaders you will be challenged to examine your understanding of leadership, as well as reflect on your own leadership and that of others. In the Health and Wellness Leadership course specifically, students will gain a broad understanding of current health topics of college students and ways to properly address and respond to these topics in both a presentation and mentoring setting.

title: Peer Leadership for Community Engagement and Service
description: This course uses an experiential pedagogy to broaden students’ development as leaders; a comprehensive approach to learning that integrates theory, practice, implementation and reflection. Through experience as a peer leader, students will examine their understandings of leadership, and reflect on their own and other’s leadership development.

title: Peer Leadership for Student Experience
description: This course uses an experiential pedagogy to broaden students’ development as leaders; a comprehensive approach to learning that integrates theory, practice, implementation and reflection. Through experience as a peer leader, students will examine their understandings of leadership, and reflect on their own and other’s leadership development.

title: Leadership and Student Thriving
description: This course explores the topic of leadership by examining ideas about people at their best. Foundational to the course are principles from Positive Psychology about well-being, optimal functioning and thriving during the college years, as well as approaches to leadership that leverage individual strengths and passions. The class will focus on six themes: thriving in college and beyond, understanding yourself, leading yourself, understanding others, engaging in community, and living out leadership and calling.

title: Interfaith Leadership
description: This course will explore theoretical and practical issues related to leadership across and inclusive of different faiths. We will discuss various civic and spiritual practices related to leadership and religious identity. Students will consider their own sense of formation and worldview through reading, writing, class discussion, and co-curricular experiences in order to cultivate spiritual growth, civic capacity, and intercultural skills.

title: Mental Health Peer Leadership
description: This course uses experiential learning to educate Mental Health Peer Leaders on the pertinent topics of mental health and illness and to equip them to break down mental health stigma and empower their peers to also challenge negative beliefs or attitudes.

title: Talent and Development
description: Talent, development, and success are fascinating topics, but few understand the theory and research behind what leads to excellence in any field. This course examines the science, theory, and research behind talent, development, success, critical thinking, and expertise. Students will learn what is known and unknown by scholars about these phenomena, as well as pathways for developing talent in themselves and others.

title: Leadership Through Informed Engagement
description: This interdisciplinary course is designed to engage students in a critical conversation about leadership and community engagement. It challenges students to reflect on their understanding of leadership, community engagement, and the intersection of the two. Students will engage in this discussion drawing specifically on their experiences as a Baylor student. They will participate in critical discussions about what it means to participate in informed engagement.

title: Leadership for 21st Century America
description: This course will address the changing demographics of our global community of the 21st century. The future of leadership will be complex in terms of practices that are impacted by a changing diversity of "world citizens" and it will demand an understanding of geographic, economic, cultural, political, religious, and linguistic mediums.  This course will address these skills  and areas of knowledge.

title: Aviation Leadership
description: See AVS 3370 for course information.

title: Special Topics in Leadership
description: Each Special Topics in Leadership course will focus on a specific area of leadership (e.g. Women in Leadership or Leadership in a Multicultural Society). May be repeated under different topic for a total of 9 credit hours.

title: Advanced Leadership
description: Leadership course focusing on advanced leadership theories and applying those theories in leadership practice.

title: Language in Society
description: Core principles of language as a social act and its employment in the construction of social identity.  Examines linguistic variation in relation to multilingualism and notions of gender, power, ethnicity, race, and social class.

title: Elementary Special Studies in Less-Commonly Studied Languages
description: Consent of instructor. Study of a less-commonly studied language at the elementary level. May be repeated for a maximum total of sixteen hours with different content.

title: Intermediate Special Studies in Less-Commonly Studied Languages
description: Consent of instructor. Study of a less-commonly studied language at the intermediate level. May be repeated for a maximum total of twelve hours with different content.

title: Introduction to Linguistics
description: See ENG 3310 for course information.

title: English Words
description: See ENG 3301 for course information.

title: Modern English Grammar
description: Study of college-level grammar, with emphasis on syntax, morphology, and usage.

title: Linguistics of Social Media: Language in Digital Spaces
description: Examines how social media platforms shape linguistic practices related to genre, register, style, identity, politeness, and multilingualism. Students will engage with authentic online data and apply linguistic frameworks to understand how social meaning is constructed and negotiated in contemporary digital communication.

title: Sociolinguistics
description: The study of language as it is used in its social context for marking an individual's group (e.g., race, gender, age, class, profession) membership.

title: Language and Culture
description: The rich interaction between our language and cultural values in defining who we are and how we relate to one another. Understanding this complex interaction is seen as the key to effective cross-cultural communication.

title: Special Studies in Less-Commonly Studied Languages
description: Consent of instructor. Study of a less-commonly studied language at the advanced level. May be repeated for a maximum total of six hours with different content.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Language Teaching/Tutoring Experience
description: Supervised practicum that provides students with structured experiential engagement in language teaching or tutoring in approved local or international contexts. Provides opportunities to apply theoretical knowledge and instructional strategies in authentic educational or community settings. Placements may vary in format and duration, including short-term international experiences or semester-long local engagements. Faculty mentorship and guided reflection ensure integration of theory, practice, and professional development.

title: Semantics and Pragmatics
description: Basic concepts, methods, and controversies in the study of word meanings, sentences, and discourse, including the interaction of language and context.

title: Contemporary Syntax
description: Principles of contemporary structural linguistics; concepts and operations of transformational syntax and case grammar as they are used to analyze the structure of language systems.

title: Phonetics and Phonology
description: Principles and methods for studying sound systems of languages.

title: Special Topics in Linguistics
description: Topics in Linguistics not covered in the Language and Linguistics courses. Topic announced for each semester or session. Course can be repeated once with a change of topic and consent of the instructor.

title: Old English Language
description: Old English language and an introduction to early medieval history and culture. Selections from Old English poetry and prose will be read in the original.

title: First Language Acquisition
description: Children's acquisition of sounds, lexicon, sentence structure, and contextual usage of their first language.

title: History of the English Language
description: See ENG 4304 for course information.

title: Psycholinguistics
description: Psychological processes involved in understanding, producing, and learning language; topics include speech perception, mental representations of language, the influence of language on thought, bilingualism, aphasia, and dyslexia.

title: Bilingual Language Acquisition and Education
description: Examines childhood bilingual language acquisition and education and highlights how this understanding is critical to examining issues in bilingual education and promoting multilingual communities in an increasingly diverse U.S. society.

title: Second Language Acquisition
description: Theories of language acquisition, taking into account such fields as linguistics, neurophysiology, psychology, sociology, and anthropology, as those disciplines illuminate the process of acquiring language. The course will also familiarize the student with problems and issues in the teaching of second languages.

title: American English Dialects
description: Introduction to dialectology, focusing on the origins of American English dialects and their development through the spread of linguistic variation.

title: Language Course Design and Assessment
description: Explores the theoretical and practical foundations of language assessment and curriculum design, emphasizing using assessment to inform curriculum decisions and improve language teaching and learning. Students analyze learner needs, contexts, curricular frameworks, and assessment tools in diverse educational settings.

title: Independent Study in Linguistics
description: Supervised individual research and study of a linguistic subfield or linguistics problem. Repeat once with change of topic for maximum of six hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Adaptive Lifetime Fitness
description: Lifetime fitness activity class for students needing accommodation due to injury or disability.  Prior to registration, students must present a medical statement from their physician outlining both the nature of the disability and recommendations for a variety of exercises to be done in class.  The individualized exercise program is a total-body program with appropriate accommodations.  A physical therapy exercise protocol that does not require an attending physical therapist may be utilized in class sessions. Participation in physical therapy sessions in a clinical setting will not substitute for an activity class. There is no waiver of the LF requirement.  Course may be repeated for credit if necessary due to ongoing medical condition.  Course is graded pass-fail.

title: Beginning Aerobics
description: Concepts of physical fitness and the development of movement routines are fundamental to this course. Material covered includes a basic understanding of fitness with various assignments relating to that knowledge. Purchase of a lab manual is required.

title: Intermediate Aerobics
description: This course incorporates all of the aspects of the beginner course. In addition, it teaches complex dance/exercise routines to music and provides a vigorous and complete workout for the more advanced student. Purchase of a lab manual is required.

title: Intermediate Aerobic Running
description: Designed for students aiming to run more than a 5K, this course covers topics such as proper running form, injury prevention, nutrition, and various training techniques. Students are expected to run 10-15 miles per week after registering for the course. They will also participate in a 10K or half-marathon, either in a community race outside of class or as a class run on a day outside of regular class hours.

title: Aqua Fitness
description: This course provides an enjoyable alternative to exercising on land. A water exercise class is an extremely efficient means of achieving fitness (particularly muscle tone) while using the natural resistance of water to exercise your body to the music.

title: Aerobic Walking
description: This course is designed to provide students with a fitness foundation through the means of walking.  Students will be given a fitness assessment profile, develop an exercise program, and participate in a series of small group and individual walks of varying lengths conducted over various terrains.  Walking techniques and fitness strategies will be stressed. Conditioning emphasis will be placed on stamina, strength, flexibility, body composition, and muscle endurance.  Student's progress of fitness and walking skills will be monitored and assessed regularly to assure completion of their goals and objectives.

title: Aerobic Running
description: Students must be capable of running a mile without stopping at the beginning of the course. This class provides runners the opportunity to train and increase aerobic running capacity. Workouts are designed to meet individual fitness levels and goals. Health-related fitness components, safety, injury prevention, and training programs will be taught. Students develop an ability to maintain a high degree of aerobic fitness through long-distance running.

title: Backpacking and Camping
description: A course designed to teach the values of wilderness experiences and the knowledge and skill necessary for enjoyable and safe basic wilderness living. Topics receiving emphasis include: safety, fitness, ecology, clothing, equipment, food preparation, emergency care, navigation and survival.

title: Advanced Wilderness Pursuits
description: Application of baseline knowledge of land based activities to more specialized/advanced pursuits like thru-hiking, winter and/or glacier travel, canyoneering, or long expedition-style backpacking experiences. Development of physical fitness and specialized skills, techniques, concepts, and terminology requisite in adventure pursuits. A multi-day wilderness experience is required.

title: Beginning Badminton
description: A course designed to teach the fundamental skills in badminton including appropriate stroke mechanics, serving, strategy, game variations, equipment selection, and rules and regulations.

title: Intermediate Badminton
description: This course allows students to refine the skills previously developed, to participate at a higher level of competition, including refereed games, to increase their knowledge of court strategy, and to improve physical conditioning through increased performance.

title: Beginning Ballet
description: This is an introductory course to ballet. The course provides training in technique, body placement, movement theory and terminology. Progressive classical ballet barre center work and floor combinations enable students to train their bodies in the proper classical technique of basic ballet.

title: Intermediate Ballet
description: This course reviews the material present in basic ballet. More advanced ballet moves are used in an effort to progress to more difficult ballet routines.

title: Advanced Ballet
description: This course is designed for the student who has had advanced level experiences in school or private ballet academy or has successfully completed the intermediate ballet class. Skills include refining basic moves and extensive work on dance routines.

title: Beginning Bicycling
description: An introductory course covering bicycling knowledge, skills, and safety. Class rides will be from eight to ten miles. Required equipment: bicycle (safe, dependable, functioning, ten or more speeds), approved helmet, and means of repairing flat tire (frame pump, tire tools, patch kit, and wrench to get hub nut off, if needed).

title: Intermediate Bicycling
description: An advanced-level course covering bicycling knowledge, skills, and safety. Class rides will be ten to fourteen miles. Although LF 1121 , Beginning Bicycling, is not a prerequisite, students are expected to have mastered basic bicycling skills before enrolling in this course.  Required equipment: safe, dependable, efficient bicycle with ten or more speeds; approved helmet; tire pump (frame type); and basic tool kit.

title: Beginning Mountain Biking
description: This course is designed to prepare students with the basic skills needed for a lifetime of enjoyable mountain bike riding.  Students are provided with an overall view of mountain biking, rules of the trail and road, safety, riding technique, clothing, equipment, and basic bike maintenance.  Emphasis will be placed on the development of skills necessary to safely climb, descend, and maneuver a mountain bike through technical areas on various terrains.

title: Beginning Bowling
description: The instructional format for this course includes skill development in grip, stance, approach, and ball delivery. Ball selection, scoring, rules and regulations, and practice will also be important ingredients of this class.

title: Intermediate Bowling
description: This course incorporates the concepts learned in the beginner class with increased analysis and corrective feedback of present bowling techniques. Additional performance time allows students to enhance their skills.

title: Varsity Sports Conditioning I
description: This course is designated as an entry level course for varsity athletes participating in intercollegiate sports.

title: Varsity Sports Conditioning II
description: This course is a continuation of Varsity Conditioning I and allows athletes the opportunity to demonstrate the skills and knowledge acquired in Varsity Conditioning I.

title: Social Dance
description: Introduction to social dance for the purpose of developing a lifetime recreational activity, often linked to social and professional situations.  Various styles of social dance, such as fox trot, waltz, swing, rock and roll, country western, cha-cha, and tango, will be covered.

title: Contemporary Dance
description: An introduction of movement principals as the body relates to space, time, energy, and relationships. Students will explore how the body moves through patterns, phrase work, improvisation, and choreography.

title: Fitness Theory and Practice
description: A comprehensive course designed for the student desiring an in-depth understanding of the concepts of physical fitness including strength development, muscular endurance, cardiovascular endurance, body composition, stress, mood modifiers, and nutrition. A variety of energetic, practical experiences are incorporated to enhance the lectures.

title: Beginning Golf
description: This course offers the students the opportunity to develop skills appropriate to the enjoyment of this healthy, lifetime sport. Students learn the proper stance, grip, and swing techniques for all clubs, as well as rules, terminology, and equipment selection.

title: Intermediate Golf
description: A course designed for students who already possess the basic skills and knowledge necessary to play golf. Topics at the intermediate level include rules, productive practice, mental aspects, course management, ball flight laws, and continued in-depth look at the short game and full swing. Video analysis is utilized to enhance student learning.

title: Advanced Golf
description: This course is for those students who own a set of clubs and have a handicap of less than twenty.

title: Beginning Jazz Dance
description: A dramatic, disciplined, and versatile style of theatrical dance. Students will gain a broader base for creative development, expression, and movement than ballet. The major concepts of jazz dance include principles of proper body alignment, strength, and flexibility development, and knowledge of movement theory.

title: Intermediate Jazz Dance
description: The intermediate-level jazz class greatly strengthens and builds upon a student's dance technique, increases knowledge and application of body alignment, flexibility and muscle control. Intermediate level includes more complex progressions, dance combinations, choreography, dance routines, and teaching skills.

title: Beginning Martial Arts
description: This is a course designed to improve beginning self-defense techniques. Skills include kicking, punching, blocking, and offensive and defensive maneuvers. Martial Arts improve strength flexibility, body control, and self-confidence.

title: Intermediate Martial Arts
description: This course reviews material covered at the beginning level of Martial Arts. The practice of fluid movements, sweeps, throws, falling and rolling, joint-locks and beginning self-defense maneuvers are introduced. This course also develops kata applications and introduces free sparring.

title: Advanced Martial Arts
description: Students must have successfully completed Intermediate Martial Arts or have special permission of the instructor to enter advanced class. The advanced class will focus on applying the techniques of self-defense, as well as introducing the strategy and tactics used in free sparring. New martial arts techniques appropriate for each skill level will also be taught.

title: Beginning Racquetball
description: Equipment selection, safety procedures, serves, strokes, volley, court strategy, rules, and actual play are combined to give the basics needed for enjoyable and knowledgeable participation.

title: Intermediate Racquetball
description: This course refines the basics and offers the more advanced student the opportunity for increased participation and analysis and corrective feedback.  Advanced shots such as overhead, backwall, Z, and drills strategy.

title: Paddle Sports
description: This course is designed to provide the student with the knowledge and skills needed to participate in and enjoy kayaking, canoeing and stand up paddle boarding (SUP) as lifetime fitness and recreational activities. Special emphasis will be given to the development of sound fundamental knowledge and skills such as paddling and steering techniques; rolling; fitting, care and use of equipment; personal safety and fitness skills; and reading moving water.

title: Sailing and Canoeing
description: A highly enjoyable recreational activity that provides the basic skills of seamanship necessary to canoe or sail safely and efficiently. The course content includes nautical terminology, types of sailing vessels, proper sailing mechanics, weather conditions, and emergency first aid care. American Red Cross certification is available upon completion of the course.

title: Beginning Rock Climbing
description: This course is intended to provide students with the fundamentals of rock climbing for lifelong leisure involvement. Course content will address personal fitness, knowledge of sport, equipment, climbing safety, and technique. The majority of this course will take place within the indoor rock wall environment but skills taught will be transferable to a top rope, outdoor environment.

title: Intermediate Rock Climbing
description: This course is intended to provide students with intermediate rock climbing skills and practices including multi-pitch belaying and rappelling, anchor systems, partner and self-rescue, route finding, minimal impact climbing, climbing hazards, and safety, as well as an introduction to sport lead climbing (indoor lead climbing). The majority of this course will take place within the indoor rock wall environment.  A class weekend field trip is required.

title: Outdoor Adventure Sports
description: Introduction to a variety of outdoor adventure sports, e.g., mountain biking, cycling, canoeing, kayaking, caving, rock climbing, bouldering, hiking, ice climbing, snow-shoeing, and geocaching. Focus will be on two to four activities to develop the entry level skills and fundamental techniques necessary for lifelong engagement in those pursuits. The course may include an overnight culminating experience.

title: Beginning Self Defense
description: Practical self-defense for people with no previous training in martial arts. The course will emphasize risk avoidance, risk recognition, and escape. The tactics and strategy of physical confrontation will be introduced - falling, throws, punching, blocking, and kicking.

title: Soccer
description: A comprehensive course in the fundamentals of soccer including dribbling, passing, kicking, and tackling skills, offensive and defensive strategies, rules and regulations, and conditioning.

title: Intermediate Self Defense
description: Intermediate self-defense strategy and tactics for students who have already been introduced to the basic techniques of karate and/or self-defense training.

title: Advanced Self Defense
description: Advanced self-defense strategy and tactics for students who already have an intermediate level of skill in karate and/or self-defense training with an emphasis on self-defense in a professional setting (teacher, therapist, counselor, etc.).

title: Softball
description: This course stresses the fundamentals of throwing, catching, fielding, batting, rules and regulations, equipment selection, drills, and actual play.

title: Beginning Swimming
description: This course in basic water safety is designed to make adults reasonably safe while in or near the water. Topics include: physical and mental adjustment to water, breathing and breath holding, kick on front and back, buoyancy and body positioning, propulsion and coordinated stroking, entries to water, and personal safety.

title: Intermediate Swimming
description: A course that develops correct stroke technique, introduction to breast stroke, side stroke, butterfly, turns, diving, basic and emergency water safety, and daily conditioning through lap swimming.

title: Advanced Swimming
description: This course is designed for the swimmer possessing sufficient skills in aquatics to allow for an understanding of the hydrodynamic principles associated with six strokes. This course will enable the individual to increase physical conditioning by designing individualized programs incorporating distance and interval training techniques into daily swim routines. Emergency water safety is stressed.

title: Lifeguard Training
description: An advanced aquatic course that prepares the individual to deal with life-threatening situations in various aquatic environments. Advanced swimming skills are required. Skill areas include assists, carries, defenses, releases, equipment rescues, facility safety, and many others. National Red Cross Lifeguarding certification is offered upon successful completion.

title: Beginning Tai Chi
description: Beginning Tai Chi focuses on slow body movements used to improve balance, coordination, concentration, awareness and timing. Instruction follows a practical scientific approach yielding illustrations of applications for each of the movements in daily life. The classical Tai Chi form will be used as a model to facilitate the known benefits of exercise and stress reduction. Cardiovascular training, strength and flexibility training, balance and coordination are interval parts of Tai Chi training, as well as the meditative/stress management techniques that are a part of the form.

title: Beginning Tennis
description: This course teaches the basics for tennis. Included are tennis terminology, rules and regulations, etiquette, equipment selection, serve, volley, ball control, forehand and backhand strokes. In-class practice of drills and individual play is provided.

title: Intermediate Tennis
description: This class refines previously learned skills of ball control, serve, forehand and backhand strokes. Included in intermediate tennis is doubles play and class games and tournaments.

title: Advanced Tennis
description: The advanced tennis player must possess a detailed knowledge and understanding of stroke mechanics and corrective drills, rules, equipment, and terminology. The advanced tennis class provides an opportunity for play with others of the same proficiency in the game. Refining strokes and physical conditioning are stressed.

title: Racquet Sports
description: Introduction to a variety of racquet sports for the purpose of developing the basic skills and knowledge necessary for lifelong activity. Various racquet sports include tennis, pickleball, badminton, table tennis, etc.

title: Beginning Pickleball
description: Instruction in the skills, rules, tactics, and techniques involved in playing pickleball as a lifelong sport. Basic fitness and training principals will be discussed as applicable to the sport of Pickleball and health.

title: Volleyball
description: This exciting team sport incorporates knowledge of the rules and court strategy with skills such as setting, bumping, serving, and conditioning to make a well-rounded player.

title: Multi-terrain Volleyball
description: For the student who has experience in volleyball through competitive play, Multi-terrain Volleyball offers analysis and development of techniques and game strategy for Wallyball, played in racquetball courts; Outdoor Volleyball, played on sand courts; and Indoor Volleyball. Emphasis is on refinement of skills of the game, conditioning, safety, court strategy, rules, and actual play for enjoyable and knowledgeable lifelong participation.

title: Weight Control
description: A course designed to teach weight control through a combination of proper diet, nutritional advice, and exercise. Toning and conditioning your body is accomplished through weight training, circuit training and exercise routines performed to music.

title: Beginning Weight Training
description: This course incorporates basic anatomic and physiological principles to specific lifts, exercises, muscles, and training techniques pertinent to the development of an individualized weight training program.

title: Intermediate Weight Training
description: This course is designed for the advanced students and will review basic concepts covered in the beginning course. In addition, more advanced techniques and training principles will be incorporated into each student's individual exercise routine.

title: Performer Wellness
description: This course is designed to address music wellness issues and help musicians adopt a healthy approach to performance that will allow them to perform at their best. Regular performance practice will be integrated with relaxation techniques such as stretching, deep breathing, imagery, and progressive relaxation. Practice and memorization techniques to assist with performance preparation will also be covered.

title: Beginning Relaxation/Fitness
description: The beginning level provides a basic set of exercises which form the foundation of a regular daily routine.  Relaxation techniques provide an effective approach to stress reduction.  Emphasis is on improving postural alignment increasing strength, flexibility, endurance, improving concentration, and reducing stress.

title: Intermediate Relaxation/Fitness
description: The intermediate level provides a comprehensive series of exercises designed to maintain the proper curvature of the spine, and to keep all systems of the body healthy.  A wide range of intermediate exercises are presented for students to incorporate into their basic exercise routine. Therapeutic exercises are introduced for specific disabilities.

title: Advanced Relaxation/Fitness
description: The advanced level is structured for the student who has developed strength, stamina and flexibility. Advanced exercises are introduced to maximize the potential for good health and vitality. Students learn to adapt the exercises to suit the various life cycles. Concentration on postural refinement is emphasized.

title: Introduction to the Middle East
description: A regional geographic introduction to the Middle East region. An overview of the physical, cultural, ethnographic, economic and geopolitical components of the region followed by a more in-depth description of selected political states.

title: Biblical Heritage and Contemporary Society in the Holy Land
description: A tour and intensive study of the Holy Land, focusing on the geological, historical, archaeological, cultural, and theological contexts of the land of the Bible.

title: Religion & Politics in the Middle East
description: An examination of the relationship between religion and politics in select countries of the Middle East.

title: Readings in Mid-East Studies
description: A tutorial course, individually designed to either allow in-depth study of special topic of student/faculty interest; or allow for a student to have individualized instruction in a broad area of Middle East studies. Details arranged. The course may be repeated once when content differs.

title: Freshman Academic Seminar: Medicine, Meaning, and the Patient/Physician Relationship
description: Introduces first-year students to the world of academics and the scholarly community through exploration of the major themes intrinsic in the physician/patient relationship. Emphasizes critical inquiry and thinking, research, writing, and problem solving.

title: Introduction to Medical Humanities
description: Introduction to the interdisciplinary and wide ranging field of medical humanities, its subjects and scope, and the way in which it can expand and enrich the experiences of patients and practitioners

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Christian Spirituality and Health Care
description: An historical framework for Western spiritual traditions, especially the Christian tradition, stressing the relationship between Christian spirituality and healing. (For students interested in Medical Humanities.)

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Visual Arts and Healing
description: An overview of the connections between visual art, healing and health care, the role of imagination and creativity in medicine, including the critical viewing of art, class discussions, experiential learning, and a service-learning project. This course fulfills the College of Arts and Sciences Engaged Learning requirement.

title: Medicine, Missions, and the Gospel
description: An overview of the biblical mandate to meet physical and spiritual needs in the context of Christian health care missions.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Teaching and Mentoring in Medical Humanities
description: An applied course designed to cultivate teaching, mentoring, and leadership skills as students support instruction in Medical Humanities.

title: History of Medicine
description: A review of the origins and development of medicine and medical science from prehistoric times to the present. Consideration will be given to the impact of major diseases on civilization and the emergence of successful measures of control.

title: History of Women in Medicine
description: This course introduces the historical contributions and challenges of women in medicine and the U.S. health care system. Special emphasis is given to societal expectations and norms and how those evolved to reflect different understanding of gender roles in medicine. This is accomplished through exposure to key figure biographies, literature, and recent peer-reviewed literature highlighting the historical influence of women in medicine, as well as contemporary gender issues in clinical practice.

title: A Biblical Theology of Suffering for Health Care Practitioners
description: A study of the biblical meanings and purposes of suffering with theological reflections and application to those served by health care professionals with consideration to disability-related ministries.

title: Writing about Sickness and Health
description: Explores multiple themes of the human experience in healthcare through reading, discussion, and writing in response to selected poems, short stories, essays, and creative nonfiction. Themes include social disparities, mortality, mental illness, and the patient’s lived experience with illness.

title: Supervised Clinical Medicine
description: Supervised weekly clinical service through various medical fields complemented by weekly seminar sessions. This course fulfills the College of Arts and Sciences Engaged Learning requirement.

title: Meaning, Money, and Medicine
description: How personal finance concepts relate to a career in health care against the backdrop of Christian spirituality and values. The course connects "vocational calling" to personal character and discusses spending, debt, giving, paying taxes, and investing.

title: End-of-Life Care & Bereavement
description: This course critically analyzes how healthcare professionals are involved in the dying experience with patients, families, and communities. Topics include causes of death, trauma, spirituality in grief, complicated bereavement, hospice and palliative care, physician-assisted dying, futile treatment at the end of life, and cross-cultural sensitivities.

title: Supervised Hospice Care
description: Hands-on hospice experience including shadowing hospice professionals.  Must meet hospice employment criteria. This course fulfills the College of Arts and Sciences Engaged Learning requirement.

title: U.S. Health Care System
description: Current state of health care in the United States including function, economic impacts, payment systems, ideas for reform, and issues related to patient safety and quality measures

title: Bench to Bedside: Clinical Trials and Translational Science
description: An overview of how basic science is translated into novel therapies with an emphasis on "real world" clinical trials.

title: Disrupting Disparities in Healthcare
description: Overview of health disparities in the United States from different disciplinary perspectives, to examine causes and consequences and to explore solutions and strategies that will make us all effective as advocates for justice and equity in healthcare. Students are connected with a local partner to participate in a community service initiative. This course qualifies for credit in the Engaged Learning distribution list.

title: Disability and Society
description: Critical reflections on questions of humanity, human well-being, understandings of disability, suffering, and care of those with disabilities, with emphasis on cultural, religious, medical and social perspectives.

title: Advanced Topics in Medical Humanities
description: Study of advanced topics in Medical Humanities. This course may be repeated once with different topic for a total of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research in Mechanical Engineering
description: Undergraduate research supervised by a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Statics
description: Study of forces, moments, free-body diagrams, friction, equilibrium, first and second moments of lines, centers of pressure, mass and gravity, and moments of inertia.

title: Dynamics
description: Kinematics and kinetics of particles and rigid bodies including Newton's Second Law, work-energy methods, impulse-momentum, and central and oblique impact.

title: Thermodynamics
description: Thermodynamic properties, heat and work, first and second laws, processes, ideal and non-ideal cycles.

title: Introduction to Aeronautics and Astronautics
description: An introduction to air and space flight, including history, basic aerodynamics, aircraft performance, and orbital mechanics. Principles of stability and control, aircraft and spacecraft structures, and propulsion are explored.

title: Research in Mechanical Engineering
description: Undergraduate research supervised by a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Materials and Manufacturing Processes Lab
description: Laboratory experiments in strength of materials, property of materials, and manufacturing processes. Application of statistics and probability to material properties and manufacturing.

title: Thermal/Fluids Laboratory
description: Laboratory measurements of devices and systems in thermodynamics and fluid mechanics.  Physics and operation of temperature, pressure, and flowrate measurement devices, and application of measurement concepts to analyzing the performance of pumps, pipe networks, airfoils, and thermodynamic cycles.

title: Mechanics of Materials
description: Introduction of stress and strain, stress transformations, analysis of stresses, strain, and deflections in axial members, beams, and torsional shafts. Analysis of pressure vessels.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Introductory concepts of fluid motions, fluid statics, control volume forms of basic principles, and applications basic principles of fluid mechanics to problems in viscous and compressible flow.

title: Mechanical Engineering Materials and Manufacturing Processes
description: Properties of the principal families of materials used in mechanical engineering design with an introduction to the manufacturing processes used to convert these materials into finished products.

title: Machine Design
description: The fundamentals of machine elements in mechanical design. Includes the analysis of components under static and fatigue loadings, and the analysis, properties, and selection of machine elements such as shafts, gears, belts, chains, brakes, clutches, bearings, screw drives, and fasteners.

title: Thermodynamics II
description: Second law analysis, gas power cycles, vapor power cycles, refrigeration cycles, property relations, gas mixtures, gas-vapor mixtures, combustion, design of cycles. (3-0 )

title: Aerodynamics
description: Study of aerodynamics including potential flow theory, airfoil theory, and viscous flow effects.  Topics include boundary layers, streamlines, pathlines, wingtip vortices, lift, drag, flow separation, and stall.

title: Aerospace Structures
description: Study of aircraft and spacecraft structures, including load paths and distribution, stress and strain, failure criteria, and structural design applications.  Topics include thin-walled beam theory, buckling, critical load, composite materials, and effects of space radiation on material properties.

title: Instrumentation and Measurements
description: Introductory mechanical engineering laboratory experience: measurement system concepts, statistical and uncertainty analyses, survey of measurement devices, experimental design and planning.

title: Research in Mechanical Engineering
description: Undergraduate research supervised by a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to Materials Science and Engineering
description: Introduction to the field of Materials Science and Engineering (MSE) with a focus on principles and applications. The course will explore the relationships between processing, structure, properties, and performance of materials and includes applications for metals, polymers, and ceramics.

title: Sustainable Engineering
description: This is an introduction to the context, concepts, and practice of sustainable engineering, and the importance of sustainable systems in the modern world. Topics will include an overview of resources and sustainability, technological systems, complexity, industrial ecology, green design principles, and life cycle assessment.

title: Computer-Aided Structural Analysis
description: Structural analysis using the matrix stiffness method with applications to 2-dimensional and 3-dimensional beams, trusses and plates.

title: Computer-Aided Engineering and Design
description: Design and analysis of engineering components and systems using interactive computer programs with emphasis on computer simulation.

title: Mechanical Vibrations
description: The theory and analysis of vibrating systems including single and multi-degrees of freedom, free and forced, vibrations, with and without damping.

title: Introduction to Finite Element Methods
description: Introduction to the basic theory and techniques of finite element analysis beginning from energy concepts and the foundational constitutive equations.  Engineering applications will focus on one- and two-dimensional formulations for classical beams, frames, trusses and electrical network applications.  Introduction to typical workflow of finite element analysis using modern computer technologies.

title: Dynamic Systems
description: Theory, analysis and simulation of dynamic systems including application of Newton's Laws and conservation of energy to model single and multiple degree-of-freedom mechanical and other dynamic engineering systems.  Solutions obtained using advanced engineering mathematics and computational software.

title: Applied Computational Fluid Dynamics
description: This course will teach the students how to use CFD (Computation Fluid Dynamics) software and will cover a variety of fluid flow problems.  The main emphasis will be getting practical results which will include how to simplify geometry so that it can be analyzed, as well as properly meshing the model being generated.  After the simulation has been solved, another major part of the learning process will be understanding how to ensure that the results are valid.

title: Numerical Methods for Engineers
description: Introduction to engineering computational methods for design, from theory to algorithm to implementation.  The course will discuss the following numerical methods from the engineering design perspective: roots of equations, optimization, linear systems, integration and differentiation, curve-fitting, and systems of ordinary differential equations.

title: Introduction to Nondestructive Testing and Evaluation
description: This course will begin by introducing the context of NDE as well as a brief history, followed by an introduction of some typical failures for various material systems.  The course will then focus on individual testing techniques, including: visual testing, penetrant testing, magnetic particle testing, radiographic testing, ultrasonic testing, eddy current testing, thermal infrared testing, acoustic emission testing, and digital radiography.

title: Introduction to Optimum Design
description: This course presents various optimal design methods that are common in simulation-based engineering design. The focus is on the development and application of design optimization numerical methods for engineering problems. Topics include fundamentals in optimal design methods, algorithm development, software application, and may include topics in multidisciplinary design optimization and structural optimization as time allows.

title: Engineering Ceramics
description: This course introduces students to engineering ceramic materials, mainly focusing on structural ceramics. Topics include general characteristics of ceramics, ceramic preparation and characterization, powder processing and sintering, ceramic phase diagrams, mechanical behavior of ceramics, ultra-high temperature ceramics and ceramic matrix composites.

title: Mechanical Behavior of Materials
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the mechanical behavior of engineering materials. Topics include stress-strain relationships, deformation mechanisms, fracture, fatigue, creep, and the influence of microstructure on material performance. Emphasis is placed on linking material properties to design applications and processing methods, with practical examples from structural and manufacturing contexts.

title: Gas Dynamics
description: This course focuses on compressible flows, where the Mach number is high enough such that the assumption of incompressibility is no longer valid. Compressible flows occur externally and/or internally in many applications, including supersonic/hypersonic jets, rockets, missiles, and more. Topics covered include supersonic flows, shock waves, shock tubes, expansion fans, and compressible flows with friction or heat transfer.

title: Mechanical Engineering Laboratory
description: Measurement of fluid flow, heat transfer, power and other properties of mechanical equipment. Design of experiments, selection and use of data acquisition systems, data reporting and presentation.

title: Thermal Systems Design
description: Design and analysis of thermal energy systems such as pipe networks, HVAC systems, and steam power plants. Specification of energy system components such as pumps, pipes, control valves, and heat exchangers.

title: Tribology
description: Experimental, analytical, and computational analysis of tribology, which is the study of friction, lubrication, wear, and fatigue between contacting and sliding surfaces. Topics include the nature of rough surfaces, contact mechanics between nonconformal and nominally-flat surfaces, nature of friction, lubricants and lubrication theory, and surface damage and fatigue. Computational analyses of surfaces and lubricant flow will be performed using Python.

title: Introduction to Computational Fluid Dynamics
description: Study of numerical methods tailored to solve thermo-fluids governing equations. Classification of partial differential equation (PDE). Finite difference method.  Basic concepts of discretization, consistency, and stability. Applications of numerical methods to selected model PDE.  Numerical methods for inviscid flow, boundary-layer flow, and the Navier-Stokes equations.  supersonic compressible and subsonic incompressible flows.

title: Composite Materials
description: Introduction to advanced fiber-reinforced composite materials for engineering design. Topics include applications, material properties, stress analysis techniques, failure theories, and design methodologies.

title: Heat Transfer
description: Steady and unsteady heat conduction including numerical solutions, thermal boundary layer concepts and applications to free and forced convection. Thermal radiation concepts. Heat exchanger design.

title: Aircraft Structural Analysis
description: Introduction to aircraft structures, including semi-monocoque and thin-walled structures, and the analysis techniques for these specialized structures. Understand the basis for airworthiness certification, aircraft loads, and design considerations in aerospace structures. Topics include elasticity, torsion, bending and shear stresses in thin walled structures; shear flow, and shear center.

title: Aircraft Flight Dynamics and Control
description: Development of aircraft equations of motion. Examination of aircraft dynamic modes based on both limited and full degree of freedom models utilizing analytical and numerical methods. Aircraft design considerations. Determination and evaluation of aircraft flying qualities. Application of control system theory to the design of aircraft stability augmentation systems and autopilots.

title: Rocket Propulsion
description: Introduction to performance analysis of liquid and solid chemical rockets systems. Thrust, efficiency and other derivations, along with Thrust and Flight profile development.

title: Orbital Mechanics and Space Flight
description: Study of spacecraft motion, including Kepler’s laws, orbits, and orbital maneuvers.  Other topics include history, Newton’s laws, transfer efficiency, escape velocity, and re-entry.

title: Introduction to Space Flight
description: An interdisciplinary introduction to the basics, concepts, methods, and applications of space flight.  Topics include fundamental principles, history, space environment, orbital mechanics, launch vehicles, propulsion systems, spacecraft (e.g., satellites, probes, space stations), applications (Earth observation, astronomy, interplanetary exploration, commercial utilization), international space efforts, regulations, and future activities.

title: Cardiovascular Engineering and Instrumentation
description: See BME 4357 for course information.

title: Renewable Energy Devices
description: Introduction to the basic concepts, principles, potential, and limitations of several energy conversion and storage devices with a focus on solar cells, fuel cells, batteries, inverters, wind power, and hydropower with real world examples. Design and/or application of various renewable energy sources, materials, and devices.

title: Principles of Process Engineering
description: Overview of the principles of process engineering, where process engineering is loosely described as converting matter from raw materials to more valuable forms.  Provides an understanding of the major aspects of process engineering as embodied in chemical, environmental, or biomolecular engineering.

title: Advanced Concepts of Process Engineering
description: Provides a deeper examination of some process engineering topics initially covered in the “Principles of Process Engineering” course.  Topics include reaction engineering, unit operations, chemical measurements, process data analysis, and flowsheeting of a process.

title: Introduction to Additive Manufacturing
description: This course introduces various aspects of additive manufacturing, which has become prominent in industry over the past two decades.
