description: Course introduces the human needs framework. Students are introduced to the role of the nurse as a facilitator of the nursing process. Case studies provide opportunities for learners to explore relationships among human needs, identify nursing interventions including those related to pharmaceuticals and plan care for a variety of physiologic, psychosocial and spiritual client needs.

title: Human Needs II
description: This course continues the introduction to the role of the nurse as facilitator of the nursing process within the Human Needs Framework. Case studies provide opportunities for learners to actively explore relationships among human needs, identify nursing interventions including those related to ethics and pharmaceuticals, and plan care for a variety of physiologic, psychosocial, and spiritual needs within the contact of family.

title: The Maturing Family
description: A study of topics related to human needs fulfillment of older adults, their families, and significant others in their lives. An overview of theories of aging, and survey of social, legal, and political issues and trends in the United States will provide a foundation for the nursing assessment, planning, and delivery of nursing care for the older adult. Learners will also have the opportunities to explore the application of the human needs framework to the aging process.

title: Mental Health Nursing: Individuals, Groups, and Family
description: Continued study of human needs framework describing selected human needs categories, relationships among human needs, interruptions in human need fulfillment, and the basis for and techniques of nursing intervention across all levels of care.

title: Introduction to Professional Nursing Practice
description: An introduction to the concepts of professional nursing practice, emphasizing the establishment of the nurse-patient relationship, application of the nursing process, and development of psychomotor skills.

title: Pathophysiology and Pharmacology I
description: Course introduces the pathophysiological basis of illness as a hindrance to human needs fulfillment. Epidemiological and genetic factors influential on pathophysiology and pharmacological therapy are addressed. Interferences with human physiologic functioning and adaptive processes are identified. Pharmacological principles are included as an essential element of nursing care.

title: Pathophysiology and Pharmacology II
description: This course continues the introduction to the pathophysiological basis of illness as a hindrance to human needs fulfillment. Epidemiological and genetic factors influential on pathophysiology and pharmacological therapy are addressed. Interferences with human physiologic functioning and adaptive processes are identified. Pharmacological principles are included as an essential element of nursing care.

title: Professional Nursing Practice: Care of the Childbearing Family
description: Professional nursing practice with individuals and families experiencing less defined and/or more complex unmet human needs from diverse populations. Practicum experiences will focus on obstetric and newborn nursing.

title: Professional Nursing Practice: Care of the Childrearing Family
description: Professional nursing practice with individuals and families experiencing less defined and/or more complex unmet human needs from diverse populations. Practicum experiences will focus on pediatric nursing.

title: Perioperative Nursing
description: Professional nursing practice roles and skills used to promote human needs fulfillment in the preoperative, intraoperative and postoperative phases of a client's surgical experience.

title: High Risk Perinatal Nursing
description: This course focuses upon nursing care to promote human needs fulfillment for families experiencing prenatal, intrapartal, postpartal and/or neonatal complications.

title: Nursing Clients with Substance Use Disorder
description: This course focuses on nursing care of a variety of client populations experiencing substance use disorders, including the elderly, adolescents, women, and veterans.  Nursing research related to the role of the nurse in prevention and management of substance use disorders will be investigated. Nursing interventions address health promotion, illness prevention, and treatment.

title: Complementary Therapies and Traditional Chinese Medicine
description: The purpose of this course is to provide an opportunity for Nursing and health professions students to experience a study abroad program with a focus on complementary and alternative therapies. Students and faculty will explore health and Traditional Chinese Medicine practices within the context of the Asian culture and health care delivery settings.

title: The Childbearing Family
description: Study family process, childbearing and nursing care to promote human needs fulfillment of the developing family.

title: Professional Nursing Practice: Transition to Practice
description: Integrates professional nursing practice while delivering care to diverse clients experiencing complex unmet needs. Clinical settings for this concentrated, 135 hour capstone will be determined following course guidelines with consideration given to student’s preferences.

title: Analysis and Synthesis of Complex Human Needs
description: Analysis of individuals with multiple and/or complex disruptions of human needs and the effect on individuals, families, groups, and/or communities. A case-study/discussion format will be used to integrate physiological, psychosocial, and spiritual needs across the lifespan.

title: The Childrearing Family
description: Identify nursing care to promote fulfillment of human needs in the childrearing family. Specific health problems of the individual from infancy  through adolescence which disrupt the integrity of the family are addressed.

title: Nursing Professional Development: Leadership and Management
description: Overview of leadership styles and management theories will assist students in identifying and applying management concepts to promote personal and professional growth, develop professional communication skills, manage conflict, implement change, make evidence-based decisions, promote safe and effective patient care, and create a healthy work environment.

title: Professional Nursing Practice: Population Health Nursing
description: Professional nursing practice emphasizing meeting the health needs of culturally diverse and vulnerable individuals, families, groups, and communities. Population health principles are examined and applied as students integrate the nursing process while providing culturally relevant care. Population focused nursing practice may include experiences in family, school, occupational, home health/hospice, faith community, and public health. Nursing.

title: Professional Development: A Consumer of Research
description: Concepts and methods of research.

title: Community and Culture
description: The nurse's responsibility in the delivery of health care to communities. Emphasis is placed on identifying unmet human needs and designing and evaluating culturally appropriate nursing care.

title: Oncological Nursing
description: The application of the nursing process for clients experiencing unmet human needs related to cancer. Nursing care will encompass health promotion, maintenance, restoration, rehabilitation, and illness prevention. Laboratory experiences with oncology clients will be provided.

title: Nursing and Health Care in Great Britain
description: Becoming more familiar with health care systems outside the United States equips nurses to expand their perspective and provide care that is competent, compassionate and current. This course will examine nursing within the British health care system, including nursing education, nursing roles within the health care system and health policy.

title: The Role of the Clincial Research Nurse
description: Course will serve as an introduction to the specialty practice of Clinical Research Nursing (CRN) and provide the foundation on concepts essential to the dimensions of CRN practice. Course will be hybrid; students to participate in prescheduled in-class seminars and online class assignments.  Course also includes one-to-one mentorship with assigned CRN. Honors College students will develop thesis proposal in addition to course requirements.

title: Introduction to Global Health
description: Cultural, community, environmental, economic, political, and policy influences on global health will be explored.  The course focuses on key global health concepts to enhance effectiveness in health care related policy and practice for interdisciplinary students interested in global health.

title: Introduction to Critical Care Nursing
description: Focus on nursing care and issues that are commonly associated with clients admitted to Critical Care Nursing Units. Clinical experiences will provide an opportunity to enhance previously learned nursing skills for application within the framework of nursing practice in the complex setting.

title: Special Topics in Nursing
description: The special topics, variable credit course provides opportunity for independent and individualized courses of study. This course does not satisfy the elective credit in the major.

title: Recital Attendance
description: This course provides a means to record attendance requirement credit by semester.

title: Academic Division Colloquium
description: This course is oriented to the development and practical application of the student's critical thinking process through lectures and presentations related to the academic field in music. This course may be repeated as necessary.

title: Music Education Convocation
description: This course provides a means to record attendance requirement credit of music education convocations by semester.

title: Church Music Forum
description: A course that meets several times each semester to hear guest speakers on a variety of topics related to church music.

title: Freshman Music Seminar
description: This course prepares entering college freshmen admitted to the School of Music for success in pursuing a degree in music. Designed for first semester students, the course covers degree expectations, study skills, long term planning and vocational preparation, and begins content instruction in the discipline. This course is available to music majors only.

title: Introductory Musicianship
description: Designed as the aural skills complement to MUS 1200 ; emphasizes rudimentary skills in sight singing, rhythm reading, and ear training.

title: Musicianship I
description: The aural skills complement to MUS 1301 , this course emphasizes solfege singing of major and minor melodies at sight, rhythm reading in both simple and compound meters, and melodic, rhythmic, and harmonic dictation.

title: Musicianship II
description: A continuation of MUS 1101 , designed as the aural skills complement to MUS 1302 .

title: Performer Wellness I
description: The goal of this course is to help you develop a balanced, healthy lifestyle and optimal performance with a holistic perspective on practice and performance preparation. The mind-body connection will be explored through relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, imagery, progressive relaxation, meditation, and yoga. Students will have the opportunity to integrate relaxation and imagery practice into live performance settings. Regular journal-writing and individualized routines will allow you to develop a deeper understanding of your personal performance profile, uncovering a unique approach to music-making that will help you achieve more confidence and conviction.

title: Applied Lessons in Natural (Baroque) Trumpet
description: Introduces the student to the traditions, fundamental techniques and performance practices of the instrument. The opportunity to perform standard repertoire from Baroque literature, in solo and ensemble settings, will be provided.

title: Undergraduate Piano for Non-Music Majors
description: Individual instruction in piano for non-music majors with some prior musical training.

title: Introductory Music Theory
description: The study of rudiments of written theory, including notation, scales, key signatures, music terminology, intervals, rhythm and meter, chord construction, and music reading. Does not count as credit toward a degree with a major in music.

title: Basic Guitar
description: Group instruction in beginning guitar. A comprehensive approach which includes accompanying, solo playing, and ensembles, history of the guitar, and fundamentals of music. Open to non-music majors only.

title: Band Techniques for String Majors
description: This class is designed to provide string majors with knowledge, skills, and competencies in teaching wind/percussion instruments individually and in an ensemble setting.

title: Introduction to Music
description: A course for the non-music major in which representative masterpieces of music are explored.  Non-technical discussions of the music, as well as the times and lives of the composers, are presented to enhance the enjoyment of the listening experience.

title: Theory I
description: The first course in a systematic study of the organization of music of the Common Practice Period. Students acquire a basic understanding of the structure of tonal music through analysis and written exercises in harmony and counterpoint.

title: Theory II
description: A continuation of MUS 1301 , including non-chord tones, 2:1 counterpoint, seventh chords, and basic formal structure.

title: Engaging with Music
description: Development of skills and insights necessary to appreciate and understand music and the vital role that that music plays in a variety of historical and cultural contexts.  Fosters creative approaches to active listening.  No previous music experience required.

title: Introduction to Music Education
description: An introductory study of learning theory and human development, an overview of the total school music program, and a survey of the history and philosophy of American music education.

title: Introduction to Technology for Musicians
description: This course, designed specifically for musicians, balances quantitative technology knowledge with practical technology experience.  Units in this course include fundamentals of: computer science, sound synthesis, notation software, sound reinforcement, digital audio recording/mixing/editing, copyright/licensing, graphic design, photography, and videography.

title: Applied Organ
description: Applied lessons in organ performance for organ majors.

title: Musicianship III
description: A continuation of MUS 1102 , designed as the aural skills complement to MUS 2301 .

title: Musicianship IV
description: A continuation of MUS 2101 , designed as the aural skills complement to MUS 2302 . Emphasizes singing and dictating melodies, rhythms, and chords commonly encountered in late nineteenth and twentieth-century music.

title: Intermediate Guitar
description: This course is designed for non-music majors who have successfully completed Basic Guitar 1202 and have a working knowledge of open position chords and scales, basic rhythm accompaniment and melody playing techniques, as well as reading skills up to and including eighth-note based rhythms in the first position. Emphasis is placed on expanding technical range and repertoire on the guitar through the understanding and assimilation of movable chord forms and scale fingerings.

title: Elementary Composition I
description: A basic approach to twentieth-century composing through analysis and exercises in melody, harmony, counterpoint, etc. Composing in small forms.

title: Elementary Composition II
description: Continuation of MUS 2207 .

title: Elementary Conducting (Music Education Elementary)
description: Fundamentals of conducting techniques and related score study in rehearsal and performance.

title: Theory III
description: A continuation of MUS 1302 , emphasizing chromaticism in tonal harmony and introducing standard musical forms.

title: Theory IV
description: A survey of late nineteenth and twentieth-century compositional techniques and methods of analysis, including scales, chords, rhythmic techniques, approaches to tonality, set theory, and serialism.

title: Music and Urban Society
description: A study of the past and present states of music in several urban centers. This is usually a study tour conducted abroad, including lectures, concerts, and first-hand encounters with the culture.

title: Formation for Christian Musicians
description: An introduction to selected aspects of Christian music ministry, including personal spiritual formation, the call to ministry, careers in Christian music, philosophy and history of church music, and practical skills for collegiate music ministry.

title: Music Education Evaluation
description: This course is designed to evaluate readiness to matriculate into upper-level Music Education study and to determine admission into the Teacher Education Program at Baylor University. This course requires sophomore standing (or upon transfer enrollment).

title: Organ Pedagogy
description: Fundamentals of organ teaching, and examination of the learning process and its application to students of different levels. Review of teaching materials and healthy practice habits. Directed teaching in group lesson settings.

title: Choral Methods I
description: Emphasis will be placed on implementation of the teaching cycle, as it applies to pre-repertoire instruction in the choral classroom.

title: Inclusive Music Education for Students with Differences and Disabilities
description: This course explores effective instructional strategies for engaging students with disabilities and diverse learning needs in music education. Emphasis will be placed on inclusive practices within both general music classrooms and ensemble settings.

title: Sight-Reading for Pianists
description: This course focuses on the development of sight-reading skills for pianists. In-class activities and regular practice assignments will help the student identify and improve sight-reading weaknesses. Intended for piano majors and piano concentrations.

title: Russian Lyric Diction
description: Special laboratory course for voice students dealing with pronunciation and enunciation as applied to singing Russian.

title: Guitar for Music Ministry
description: Designed to give the church music student functional guitar skills. This skill-based course will assure competency in playing chords for common guitar keys and basic strumming patterns. Students will also be exposed to other aspects of guitar playing such as tablature, chord charts, different types of guitars, and other relevant functions. Students who are experienced guitar players will move at their own pace.

title: Choral Arranging and Composition
description: Creative work in choral arranging and composition. Analysis of various styles of choral composition.

title: Arranging for Popular and Jazz Idioms
description: A study of standard practices of notation, scoring, and instrumentation with regard to jazz and popular music. Attention will be focused on writing for a rhythm section, scoring for woodwinds and brass, and on relating these to vocal idioms. A lab format will be used in which student will be performed.

title: Composition
description: A first course in composition for non-composition majors.

title: String Methods (For String Majors)
description: A survey of the most widely-used pedagogical methods for heterogeneous group string instruction. Emphasis will be placed on observation, practice teaching, and program building.

title: Choral Methods II
description: Addresses a wide range of issues involved in administering a school choral music program.

title: Instrumental Methods II
description: Addresses a wide range of issues in administering school instrumental music programs.

title: Instrumental Conducting
description: Application of basic conducting skills to the direction of instrumental ensembles.

title: Choral Conducting
description: Application of basic conducting skills to the direction of choral ensembles.

title: Theory V
description: Harmonic and structural analysis of larger forms, including variations, rondo, sonata, and contrapuntal forms.

title: Orchestration
description: Ranges, notation, and characteristics of orchestral instruments. Exercises in orchestrating for combinations of string and wind instruments, including full orchestra. Styles of orchestration. Orchestral score reading.

title: Intermediate Composition
description: Writing in the larger forms for piano, voice, choir, and small instrumental combinations.

title: Intermediate Composition
description: Writing in the larger forms for piano, voice, choir, and small instrumental combinations.

title: Beginning Jazz Improvisation
description: An introduction to the techniques of jazz improvisation. Study of jazz theory and Afro-American artists and styles.

title: Piano Pedagogy I
description: Piano Level IV Fundamentals of piano teaching, and examination of the learning process and its application to the elementary student. Review of teaching materials and music technology for the elementary student. Observation and directed teaching in the Piano Laboratory Program, both individual and group lesson settings.

title: Piano Pedagogy II
description: Examination and evaluation of beginning piano methods. Survey, analysis, and classification of pre-college teaching literature. Independent studio management and planning. Observation and directed teaching in the Piano Laboratory Program, both individual and group lesson settings.

title: Piano Pedagogy III
description: Examination and evaluation of standard literature, technical approaches, and teaching materials for the late intermediate, early advanced, and beginning adult student. Additional topics include injury prevention and performance anxiety. Undergraduate students will teach a community student in the Piano Laboratory Program.

title: Survey of Music Literature
description: This course surveys the general history of music. It requires students to be able to follow a musical score, and includes the completion of a research project during the course of the semester.

title: Music in World Cultures
description: Addresses music as a significant part of various world cultures through lectures, videos, class listening and discussion, and hands-on experience with various musical systems.  Music will be studied in relation to religions, political systems, economics, languages and other arts.  When possible, workshops with visiting non-Western ensembles will be arranged.

title: Popular Musics
description: Examination of the musical and cultural significance of popular musics, exploring how musical sound can be considered “text,” and analyzing how and why popular music is created.  No previous music experience required.

title: Jazz and its History
description: The study of jazz from its West African and American blues roots through ragtime, urban blues, big bands, bebop, cool, 1960s avant garde, and present developments.

title: The History of Music before 1600
description: Survey of European music from Antiquity through the Renaissance, including some related materials from outside the Western tradition.

title: The History of Music from 1600 to 1800
description: Survey of European and American music from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

title: The History of Music from 1800 to World War I
description: Survey of European and American music from 1800 to World War  I, including some related materials from outside the Western concert tradition.

title: The History of Music from World War I to the Present
description: Integrated survey of European and American art music since World War I, along  with selected topics outside that tradition.

title: Music in the Elementary School
description: For music education majors and elementary education majors with a teaching field in music.

title: Secondary Choral Music
description: Topics of study include music reading strategies, rehearsal technique, curriculum design and administrative issues related specifically to choral music programs.

title: Instrumental Music in the Secondary School
description: Organization, administration, and promotion of band and orchestra programs. Care and selection of instruments, uniforms, music, and related materials. Units on testing and grading, rehearsal procedures, programming, and financing.

title: Music for Elementary Classroom Teachers
description: Required of all elementary education majors. A study of the musical experiences and activities through which children acquire basic musical concepts and skills.

title: Elementary Music Education
description: Includes observation and field teaching.

title: The Musician as Educator in the Church
description: A survey of music education in the church, with particular emphasis upon the planning, implementation and ministerial facets of teaching, as well as a look at the practical philosophical and psychological aspects within a church setting. (3 hours)

title: Leadership in Music Ministry
description: Survey of the principles involved in organizing and implementing a comprehensive music ministry, including budgeting, programming, and multiple staff relationships.  An introduction to pastoral skills including visioning, hospital visitation, and conflict management. (3 hours)

title: Instrumental Music in the Church
description: A study of the use of instruments in the church, seeking to provide students with both knowledge and skill set in the field of instrumental music arrangement and performance practice specifically in the context of Christian worship.

title: Technology and Keyboards in the Church
description: A survey of the use of technology, keyboard instruments, and handbells in the church; including the purchase, maintenance, and use of the instruments/equipment.

title: Band Led Worship
description: A study of the musical components of band-led worship.  The course will break down the role of each instrument, including voice, and equip students with practical skills for working and communicating with each instrument.  These skills will be applied to arranging and rehearsal techniques.

title: Advanced Undergraduate Organ
description: Advanced applied lessons in organ performance.

title: Music Research III
description: This course equips undergraduate students with the essential skills necessary for conducting a systematic and comprehensive literature review, IRB request, and appropriate methodology for their research proposal. As part of the course requirements, students will collaborate with faculty members on undergraduate research projects. Faculty members will serve as research mentors while students develop skills in authentic research contexts. This course serves as the first of two upper-level courses in the undergraduate music research sequence.

title: Music Education Fieldwork
description: For completion of field experience and other requirements prior to student teaching.

description: Presentation of a recital in fulfillment of degree requirements.

description: Presentation of a recital in fulfillment of degree requirements.

title: Internship in Piano Teaching I
description: Special assignments in piano teaching; children's classes and lessons in the Preparatory Laboratory program; group and individual instruction of college students; adult leisure piano. Faculty direction and evaluation of teaching. May be repeated for credit.

title: Internship in Piano Teaching II
description: Special assignments in piano teaching; children's classes and lessons in the Preparatory Laboratory program; group and individual instruction of college students; adult leisure piano. Faculty direction and evaluation of teaching. May be repeated for credit.

title: Opera Project I
description: Training class for singers and choral conducting students in stage techniques.

title: Hearing, Experiencing, and Learning 21st Century Vocal Repertoire
description: Each unit of this class will consist of a lecture component, a listening component, and a practice component. Students will read and hear about composers writing vocal music in the twenty-first century, will listen to examples of performance, and will work together on assigned excerpts in class. Students will have the opportunity to hear current practitioners talk about recent trends in classical music, including artist-driven projects and video production. Students will engage in finding music written during their lifetime to present in class. Finally, each student will present a final project by performing music written during their lifetime. In keeping with their more advanced experience level, graduate students will have longer and more challenging musical assignments for in-class coachings and final projects. Additionally, they will have a writing component to the final project. They will submit a final paper of 750-1000 words on their final musical project, including background on the composer, the circumstances of the work’s creation and premiere, and details about the composition.

title: Practicum in Instrumental Music Education
description: This is an experiential course aimed at providing upper level instrumental music education students with real world teaching opportunities in a monitored environment.

title: Vocal Coaching
description: Advanced individual study of solo vocal repertoire for upper-level students preparing recitals, competitions, auditions, etc. May be repeated for a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Special Topics
description: An in-depth study of a narrowly circumscribed topic, such as a composer or genre. This course provides the opportunity to utilize special skills and knowledge of outstanding resident or visiting faculty. May be repeated six times with different topics of study.

title: Music Theory Pedagogy
description: A survey of materials and methods for teaching theory and aural skills at high school and college levels.

title: Theoretical Systems
description: Systems currently employed in music-theoretic scholarship for analysis of various musical styles. Application and comparison of the systems.

title: Induction to Music Teaching
description: This course, designed to be taken concurrently with all-level student teaching in music, examines practical issues involved in successful music teaching, such as organization, administration, classroom management, and school governance. Additionally, current controversial issues in education are considered in the context of direct field experiences in music. Students must earn at least a grade of C in this course.

title: Music Ministry Internship
description: A four-week internship (one hundred sixty hours) in a church under the direct supervision of the minister of music. Periodic observations by the faculty.

title: Senior Seminar and Paper
description: Introduction to bibliography, research methods, and scholarly writing, providing the opportunity for specialized work in the student's major area in music history and literature or theory degrees.

title: Vocal Pedagogy
description: A study of the singing voice including anatomy and physiology of the larynx and the breathing mechanism, phonation, basic acoustics, vocal registers, vocal pathologies, and hygiene, among other related subjects.

title: Aesthetic Perception
description: See ENG 43C1 for course information.

title: All-Level Student Teaching in Music
description: Practice in all student teacher proficiencies at the elementary, middle school, and secondary levels, including classroom organization and management, assessment, planning/delivery of instruction, adapting instruction for individual needs, collaboration, technology, and professionalism. A one-semester practicum for music education students. The course provides fifteen weeks of full-day supervised practice in all student teacher proficiencies at the elementary, middle school, and secondary levels, including classroom organization and management, assessment, planning/delivery of instruction, adapting instruction for individual needs, collaboration, technology, and professionalism.

title: All-Level Student Teaching in Music (out-of-town)
description: Practice in all student teacher proficiencies at the elementary, middle school, and secondary levels, including classroom organization and management, assessment, planning/delivery of instruction, adapting instruction for individual needs, collaboration, technology, and professionalism. A one-semester practicum for music education students. The course provides fifteen weeks of full-day supervised practice in all student

title: Workshop in Elementary School Music
description: Designed especially for music teachers and elementary classroom teachers, and open to music education majors.

title: Workshop in Instrumental Music
description: Designed especially for music teachers and others who are involved in instrumental music direction or instruction, and open to music education majors.

title: Workshop in Choral Music
description: Designed especially for music teachers and others who are involved in choral music direction or instruction, and open to music education majors.

title: Independent Studies
description: Independent research undertaken by the student under the supervision of one faculty member. This course is limited to superior students working in their major area. It cannot duplicate the content of another course or substitute for degree requirements.

title: Music Research IV
description: This course equips undergraduate students with the essential skills necessary for data collection and preparing presentations or publications of the results. As part of the course requirements, students will collaborate with faculty members on undergraduate research projects. Faculty members will serve as research mentors while students develop skills in authentic research contexts. This course serves as the capstone in the music research sequence.

title: Foundations of Medicine
description: A broad survey of the premedical studies, the preparation for medical school admission, and the medical profession.

title: Introduction to the Health Professions
description: A blended classroom format with lectures exploring various careers and graduate level study in health related professions and online content focusing on professionalism in healthcare, assessment of appropriate personal attributes, integrity, interpersonal communication, and career strategies.

title: Introduction to the Health Professions
description: A blended classroom format with lectures exploring various careers and graduate level study in health related professions and online content focusing on professionalism in healthcare, assessment of appropriate personal attributes, integrity, interpersonal communication, and career strategies.

title: Prehealth Clinical Experiences and Service Learning
description: Prehealth clinical and service-learning activities and professional leadership development. Students engage in supervised professional shadowing, clinical volunteering, and outreach programs that cultivate a deeper understanding of the social dynamics of healthcare through practical, real-world experiences. Reflective assignments, seminars, and evaluations complement the fieldwork to develop competencies in empathy, cultural humility, ethical practice, interprofessional collaboration, and awareness of health disparities. Prerequisite: Prehealth designation and approval of the Prehealth Studies Office or instructor. May be repeated for credit with different experiences and approval

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Foundations of Medical Research
description: Introduction to clinical/medical research in an academic medical setting, including medical ethics, research modalities, and evaluation tools.

title: Special Topics in Pre-Health
description: Internship or research in a clinical setting focusing on formulating a research project, including developing the hypothesis, creating an IRB proposal, gathering and interpreting data, and publishing results.

title: Nature of Healthcare Relationships
description: Examines the nature of healthcare relationship and leadership from a number of perspectives, including both the patient, the healthcare provider and the healthcare administrator. Explores both professional and ethical issues and the implications in delivering healthcare in the United States.

title: Premedical Professional Development
description: Professionalism within healthcare including appropriate personal attributes and expectations, integrity, interpersonal communication, and self-appraisal.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to the Profession of Nutrition and Dietetics
description: NUTR 1201 is designed to introduce students to the profession of Nutrition and Dietetics and to provide the students with resources to promote academic success at Baylor University.

title: Introduction to Food Science
description: Comprehensive study and experimental laboratory investigation of food constituents with a focus on chemical and physiochemical properties and reactions, including sensory evaluation, microbial safety, and food preservation.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Nutrition
description: Scientific approach to the essential nutrients for human growth and development.  Macronutrients, micronutrients, and human metabolism have details coverage; a computer diet analysis is conducted.

title: Intro to Food Delivery Systems
description: Introduction to meal service in the setting of the home, institutions, childcare, and restaurants.  Management of meal planning, preparation, and service.  Emphasis on equipment usage and maintenance and time, money, and nutrient management.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to the Nutrition Care Process
description: Introduction to the Nutrition Care Process (NCP) with an emphasis on the Nutrition Assessment and Nutrition Diagnosis components of this standard protocol, including application of nutrition-focused physical assessment.  Medical terminology pertinent to the NCP will also be discussed.

title: Nutrition Systems Management
description: This course provides an introduction to managerial and leadership processes vital to nutrition/food-focused organizations through a study of the organization as a social system.  Emphasis is placed on presenting practical skills that will facilitate the student's transition to the workplace. Topics examined include management and leadership philosophy, ethical decision-making, employee motivation, work attitudes, teamwork and group dynamics, managerial communication, and organizational culture and change.

title: Consumer Nutrition
description: Survey of basic nutrition and current issues focusing on personal nutrient needs.  Not open to nutrition sciences majors, minors or other students who have taken NUTR 2351 .

title: Nutrition Counseling and Education
description: Nutrition counseling and education strategies that can be used for dietary behavior change, emphasizing practical aspects of nutrition counseling.

title: Experimental Foods
description: Investigation of the composition, functional properties, and reactions of food components.  The study of chemical-physical changes which result from processing materials comprising foods.  Study of experimental techniques for product development.

title: Introduction to Medical Nutrition Therapy
description: In-depth study and proficiency testing of the nutrition care process, medical vocabulary, medical records, clinical math, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.

title: Food Service Production
description: Quality food production techniques, procedures, and management of meals serving fifty or more people.  The function and methods of food management (for profit) will be emphasized in this course.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Nutrition Practicum
description: Supervised field experiences in nutrition education and program delivery.  A minimum of one hour each week is required in a seminar setting.  May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours.

title: Seminar in Nutrition Sciences
description: Professional aspects of nutritional sciences with concentration on ethics, credentialing, communications, marketing, and public policy techniques.

title: World Nutrition
description: World hunger as a major international problem.  The effects of malnutrition on growth, health, and economic output will be examined.

title: Medical Nutrition Therapy for Eating Disorders
description: This course provides an overview of the etiology, diagnosis, and evidence-based treatment of eating disorders with emphasis on the role of the nutrition professional within the interdisciplinary team.

title: Nutrition for Sport and Fitness
description: Nutritional concepts for individuals and team sport participants across the life cycle with a particular focus on selection of optimal dietary approaches as related to performance needs, maximizing performance, body comparison, energy balance, and unique nutrient needs for specific sport participants.  Food and nutrition quackery in sports will also be addressed.

title: Advanced Nutrition
description: Nutrients and their roles in human health.  Emphasis on trends in nutritional research.

title: Medical Nutrition Therapy
description: In-depth study of various disease states with a focus on pathophysiology and evidence-based medical nutrition therapy interventions.  Field experience in clinical dietetics is required.

title: Special Studies in Nutrition, Food science and food production
description: Current issues in nutrition, food science or food production can be explored through seminars, special topics, and practicums.  May be repeated with different content for a maximum of 6 semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Nutrition Sciences
description: Current issues in nutrition will be examined through seminars, special topics, practicums and/or individual research experiences. May be repeated with different content for a maximum of 6 semester hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research Writing: Introduction to Philosophy
description: A writing-intensive introduction to the major philosophical topics, themes, and thinkers. The primary aims of this class are two-fold. First, to learn important skills  involved in effective writing, and by extension, critical reading, and reflective thinking. Second, to become acquainted with some important figures in the history of philosophy and to evaluate how they wrote as a means of learning the recursive process required to write well. This course may be repeated once if there is substantively different content with departmental approval required.

title: Introduction to Philosophy (Non-writing Intensive)
description: An introduction to the major philosophical topics, themes, and thinkers.  Students develop the ability to read texts critically.

title: Logic
description: A study of the basic principles and methods for distinguishing good and bad reasoning across a broad range of contexts, with an emphasis on deductive reasoning.  Students develop formal tools to identify, reconstruct, and evaluate arguments, and to compose argumentative essays of their own.

title: Critical Thinking
description: The development of critical, coherent, and creative thinking, including understanding, analyzing, and evaluating the claims of others, organizing ideas clearly, and constructing sound arguments. Development of sensitivity to argumentation technique and to the language in which arguments are expressed, with particular attention to the persuasive techniques of advertising and other controversial issues in the mass media.

title: Contemporary Moral Problems
description: An introduction to philosophical issues and theories associated with the normative assessment of human behavior through engagement with one or more contemporary social issues such as poverty, war, immigration, affirmative action, drug legalization, abortion, sexuality, animal rights, and the environment. This course may be repeated once if there is substantively different content with departmental approval required.

title: Introduction to Medical Ethics
description: Introduction to philosophical reasoning regarding medical ethics, including confidentiality, intervention in the beginning and ending of human life, and just distribution of medical resources.

title: Computer Ethics
description: Analysis of ethical problems for information technologies. Topics include ethical implications of new possibilities in information technologies, privacy, ownership, professional codes of conduct as they relate to society, and role of information technologies in shaping morality of government, education, politics, business, and society.

title: Engaged Approaches to Ethical Theory
description: Students are introduced to issues in ethics—the study of how to live well—through participating in local community work and study of philosophical theories. Coursework includes regular engagement with organizations working in areas that impact human thriving such as health, education, environment, inequality, and immigration. Students will integrate reflection on their experiences in the community with people facing barriers to living well with philosophical argument and theory to refine and revise judgments about key ethical issues.  This course fulfills the College of Arts and Sciences Engaged Learning requirement.

title: Introduction to Moral Philosophy
description: Introduces students to the question of moral philosophy: how ought we to live?  Equips students with the philosophical vocabulary required to take a well-reasoned stand in ongoing debates about moral norms and their practical application.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Existentialism
description: An examination and evaluation of philosophical themes and methods in existentialist writings. Themes such as freedom, anxiety, despair, nothingness, alienation, death, God, the impotence of reason, the conflict between individuality and the dehumanizing tendencies of mass society, and the conflict between authentic self and inauthentic self are considered. Attention is focused upon the work of such thinkers as Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Jaspers, Heidegger, Marcel, Sartre, and Camus.

title: Philosophy in Literature
description: A critical engagement of the philosophical ideas represented in selected literary texts, such as science fiction, dystopian fiction, the inklings, or existentialist literature, indicated by course subtitle.  Examines a wide variety of philosophical topics with special attention to the role of imagination.

title: Philosophy and Religion
description: A writing-intensive introduction to philosophical issues arising from religious belief and practice.  Students develop the ability to read texts critically, and to write clear argumentative essays about such topics as faith and reason, the problem of evil, and the coherence of doctrines like atonement and incarnation.

title: Philosophical Texts
description: A study of central philosophical texts in their historical context and for their enduring philosophical contributions. Possible texts include Plato's Republic; Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics, Augustine's Confessions, St. Thomas's Summa, Descartes' Meditations, Kierkegaard's Fear and Trembling, and Nietzche's Thus Spoke Zarathustra. May be taken a maximum of two times if different topics, not to exceed six semester hours.

title: Philosophical Traditions
description: An introductory study of a central philosophical tradition in its historical context and for its enduring philosophical contribution. Possible traditions include existentialism, feminism, political liberalism, pragmatism, post-modernism, naturalism, positivism, and scholasticism. May be taken a maximum of two times if different topics, not to exceed six semester hours.

title: Law, Science and Society
description: A study of philosophical issues arising at the intersections of law, morality, science, and society. The course will consider such issues as the proper relation between morality and law, civil disobedience, racism, feminism, equal opportunity and justice, abortion, euthanasia, animal rights, punishment, pornography, creationism, and moral aspects of technological development.

title: Literary & Philosophical Perspectives on the Environment
description: Integrates literary and philosophical perspectives on the environment in order to address the ecologic crisis facing humanity in the twenty-first century. Equips students to read the literature from these different perspectives critically and write reflectively upon them. Students will develop creative writing skills, allowing them to express themselves through ecofiction/story/narrative and ecopoetry.

title: Business Ethics
description: An analysis of moral issues that arise within the economic sphere of society and specifically within profit and nonprofit organizations. The nature and justification of moral decision making will be examined. Topics may include moral issues involving the relationships between business and other social organizations, ecology, the social responsibility of entrepreneurs, and personnel and policy decisions.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Character and Computing
description: Equips students with the conceptual resources and moral vocabulary required to analyze and critically engage ethical issues encountered by professionals working with information technologies. Issues may include employee rights, privacy, whistle-blowing, conflicts of interest, environmental impacts, and the role of information technologies in society.

title: Moral Philosophy
description: A critical study of problems in moral judgment and evaluation, with analysis of presuppositions and justifications used in moral discourse. Problems such as freedom and determinism, relativism and absolutism, conflicts of duties and ends, grounds of moral obligation, and choices involving personal and social goals are also studied. This course will introduce students to a number of major primary sources in the history of moral philosophy.

title: British Philosophy and Culture
description: This course is designed for Baylor University's study-abroad program. (Note: see section in this catalog regarding foreign study.) While the specific course content will vary with the instructor, attention will be given to the way issues have been addressed by philosophers in the British Isles such as Thomas More, Francis Bacon, Hobbes, Locke, Hume, John Stuart Mill, Wittgenstein, Bertrand Russell, and Gilbert Ryle. The philosophical ideas of literary figures such as Jane Austen, Robert Browning, and William Wordsworth may also be considered. Discussions will be developed in the rich settings of cathedrals, theaters, universities, and museums.

title: History of Philosophy: Classical Philosophy
description: Historical context in which philosophy developed and how the original issues of philosophy continue to inform historical and contemporary philosophical debate. Emphasizes the reading of primary sources: Homer, Hesiod, the pre-Socratics, the Sophists, Plato, and Aristotle, and the study of Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Skepticism.

title: History of Philosophy: Modern European Philosophy
description: A study of the major developments in philosophy from the Renaissance through the first half of the nineteenth century. The demise of late Scholasticism, the rise of modern science, the philosophies of the Continental Rationalists and the British Empiricists, the critical philosophy of Kant, and German Idealism are considered. Philosophers studied include Descartes, Leibniz, Spinoza, Hobbes, Locke, Berkeley, Hume, Kant, Hegel, Marx, Schopenhauer, Kierkegaard, and Nietzsche.

title: Philosophy and Constitutional Issues
description: The examination of certain philosophical issues that are raised by the U.S. Constitution, and especially by the Bill of Rights and the 14th Amendment.

title: Philosophical Issues in Feminism
description: A critical study of philosophical issues in feminism including moral issues of equal rights and justice, sex role stereotypes, equal opportunity and reverse discrimination, equality between the sexes, abortion, and philosophers' theories of feminism. Topics may vary from semester to semester.

title: Philosophy and the Arts
description: A survey of the major contemporary sources in aesthetics. Problems discussed are concerned with the aesthetic experience, the interpretation of art (including the definition of art, the nature of metaphor, the relation of art to knowledge, meaning in art), and criticism in literature and other art forms.

title: Law and Religion
description: Relationships between government and religion, especially, United States Supreme Court decisions dealing with prayer and Bible reading in public schools, government aid to church-related schools, and religious liberty rights of individuals and churches. Philosophical debates about the nature of religious free exercise and establishment, their justification, and their relationship to different political theories. Note for undergraduate religion majors: This course will be accepted as three elective hours on a religion major, but will not be accepted for credit on a minor in religion or toward the six hour religion requirement by the University.

title: Normative Ethics & Agency
description: Surveys the philosophical questions in ethical theory concerning the nature of the good life, moral obligation, and human flourishing. Readings from classical and contemporary sources explore the meaning of life, the conditions of virtue and right action, and how agency, rationality, and personhood relate to moral obligation, value, and worth.

title: Moral Foundations and Metaethics
description: Surveys the philosophical commitments underlying ethical systems through classical and contemporary readings. Examines whether ethical truth is relative to individual or cultural preferences, transcendent of human practices, or dependent on God and considers how these views bear on the possibility of moral knowledge and the grounding of moral principles, values, and justifications.

title: Historiography and the Philosophy of History
description: An introduction to ancient, medieval, and modern historiography and the development of the philosophy of history. Critical consideration will be given to traditional thought about concepts fundamental to history, including the ideas of historical explanation, purpose, cause, and interpretation. Emphasis will be given to methods of historical research and writing.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: History of Medicine
description: A review of the origins and development of medicine and medical science from prehistoric times to the present. Consideration will be given to the impact of major diseases on civilization and the emergence of successful measures of control.

title: War and Morality
description: An analysis of the moral and philosophical issues arising from military operations around the world, whether formal or informal, historical and contemporary.

title: Philosophy of Mind
description: Critical examination of current problems in philosophy of mind, focusing on the relationship between mind and physical world. Central issues include the nature of consciousness, and the nature of mental content; secondary topics may include, reductionism, functionalism, non-reductive materialism, epiphenomenalism, panpsychism, and dualisms of various forms.

title: Philosophical Writing, Research and Oral Presentation
description: Enhancing philosophical writing skills, promoting proficiency with new computer research technologies, and refining oral communication skills.

title: Literary and Philosophical Perspectives on Medicine
description: Examination of literature dealing with illness, disease, pain, and death in order to understand better how societal perceptions and values of the care-giver affect the patient.

title: Philosophy and Medicine
description: Philosophical approaches to clinical medicine and contemporary health care, focusing on experience as a basis for knowledge.

title: Religious Ethics in a Liberal Democracy
description: Addresses both historical and contemporary arguments about the relationship between religious morality and liberal democracy. Pays particular attention to the debate about the role of religious forms of ethics/morality in public debate, public choices, and the decisions of political actors.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Finding Success and Community Engagement in Physics
description: New Student Experience (NSE) course that introduces students in physics, astronomy or astrophysics to the benefits and challenges of studying these subjects. Includes an introduction to research areas and strategies for problem solving in physics. Students will bring some of what they learn into area classrooms of secondary schools for community engagement.

title: Light, Vision and Optics
description: A conceptual course on the physics of light, optics, photography, vision, color, and other visual phenomena.

title: Great Ideas in Physics
description: Concepts and historical development of physics; selected topics as they apply to physics encountered in everyday life. This course is not intended for students pursuing a science major.  (Does not apply toward a major in physics.)

title: Sound and Acoustics
description: Physics of sound and sound production, including the nature of sources of sounds. Introduction to the primary instrumentation used in recording, reproduction, and analysis of the physical properties of sound, and a study of the principal physical properties of architectural acoustics.

title: General Physics for Natural and Behavioral Sciences I
description: The fundamentals of mechanics, heat, and sound, with emphasis on physical concepts, problem solving, notation, and units. ( PHY 1408 and 1409 or PHY 1420 and 1430 satisfy premedical, predental, and liberal arts requirements.)

title: General Physics for Natural and Behavioral Sciences II
description: Algebra-based introduction to electricity, magnetism, circuits, light, and modern physics with emphasis on physical concepts, problem solving, notation, and units.

title: General Physics I
description: Calculus-based introduction to motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotational dynamics, statics, fluids, oscillations, sound, and heat with emphasis on physical concepts, problem solving, notation, and units.

title: General Physics II
description: Calculus-based introduction to electricity, magnetism, circuits, light, and modern physics with emphasis on physical concepts, problem solving, notation, and units.

title: Descriptive Astronomy
description: A survey course on the fundamentals of astronomy including night-sky viewing, the history of astronomy, the solar system, stars and galaxies, and the Big Bang cosmology.

title: Independent Study in Physics
description: Independent study under the supervision of a faculty member in the department. May be repeated once for credit up to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Basic Electronics Laboratory
description: Principles and applications of electrical circuits and electronics for physical science majors.

title: Foundations of Astronomy Laboratory
description: Laboratory course that illustrates topics covered in PHY 2355 .

title: Introduction to Research in Physics
description: Preparation for undergraduate research. Includes an introduction to research techniques, identification of a research supervisor, and completion of a research prospectus.

title: Modern Physics
description: Fundamental concepts of quantum physics and relativity. Applications to atomic structure and spectra, black body radiation, solid state physics, and nuclei.

title: Foundations of Astronomy
description: Physical and mathematical foundations of modern astronomy emphasizing techniques, history, and development of our current picture of the universe including galaxies, stars, planets, planetoids, and comets.

title: Mathematical and Computational Physics
description: Mathematical methods for the physical sciences, including complex variables, linear algebra, partial differentiation, vector calculus, Fourier series, and differential equations. Emphasis on analytical techniques with computational implementations in Python and Mathematica to support visualization and solution of physical problems.

title: Foundations of Astronomy with Laboratory
description: Physical and mathematical foundations of modern astronomy emphasizing techniques, history, and development of our current picture of the universe including galaxies, stars, planets, planetoids, and comets. Includes laboratory to illustrate the topics covered in lecture.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Intermediate Physics Laboratory I
description: Perform foundational experiments in classical and modern physics (typically 3), with a focus on experimental planning and data and error analysis.  Experiments are selected from measurements of the electric charge (Millikan oil-drop), the gravitational constant (Cavendish), the speed of light, radioactive decay, Compton scattering, quantum encryption, and others.

title: Intermediate Physics Laboratory II
description: A more in-depth study of experimental planning, data analysis, and elementary error analysis with a larger number of experiments and reports than in PHY 3175 .

title: History of Invention and Technology
description: History of invention and technology including biographical study of scientists. Does not apply toward a major or minor in physics, astronomy or astrophysics.

title: Intermediate Classical Mechanics
description: Principles of classical mechanics in three dimensions. Topics include Newtonian mechanics, conservation laws, oscillations, central-force motion, nonlinear dynamics, and chaos, with an introduction to Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations. Emphasis on analytical problem-solving and mathematical modeling.

title: Intermediate Electricity and Magnetism
description: Topics normally include electrostatics, Laplace's equation, method of images, multipole expansion, magnetostatics, and Maxwell's electromagnetic field equations.

title: Topics in Astronomy
description: Topics in contemporary research in astronomy and astrophysics.

title: Introductory Quantum Mechanics I
description: Postulates of quantum mechanics, operators in Hilbert space, superposition principle and compatible observables, time development, conservation theorems, one-dimensional bound and unbound state problems, three-dimensional problems, identical particles.

title: Introductory Quantum Mechanics II
description: Time-independent and time-dependent perturbations, approximation methods including WKB, variation principles, and the adiabatic approximation, scattering theory, introduction to relativistic quantum mechanics, entanglement.

title: Observational Astronomy
description: Fundamentals of practical sky observing including visual, photographic, and photometric techniques using local telescopes and CCD cameras. Introduction to methods for finding and identifying celestial objects, making observations, and operating an observatory, telescopes, and related equipment.

title: Undergraduate Research in Physics
description: Research conducted under the supervision of a faculty member in the department. May be repeated up to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Exit Exam
description: An exit examination, designated by the department, is required of all physics majors. This examination will be chosen by the department from the GRE Subject Test or another similar examination.

title: Instructional Observing
description: Teaching astronomy techniques and assisting on observing nights for the PHY 3455 class and campus outreach events.

title: Dissemination of Research Results in Physics
description: Culmination of an undergraduate research project including presentation of research results.

title: Special Topics in Physics
description: Advanced topics in physics, astronomy, or astrophysics. May be repeated once for credit with a different topic up to a total of six semester hours.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Portuguese Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Portuguese Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Elementary Portuguese
description: Introduction to Portuguese language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).  Progress through novice proficiency levels

title: Elementary Portuguese
description: Introduction to Portuguese language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).  Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Portuguese for Spanish Speakers
description: For students who already speak Spanish. Integrates and compresses the content of POR 1301 and POR 1302 into a single semester. Develops proficiency in the three communicative modes—interpersonal, interpretive, and presentational—while engaging with cultural practices and perspectives from the Portuguese-speaking world, with particular emphasis on Brazil.

title: Intermediate Portuguese
description: Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).

title: Exploring the Portuguese-Speaking World
description: An exploration of the cultures of the Portuguese-speaking world with continued practice listening, speaking, reading, and writing POR at the intermediate level. Class conducted in Portuguese, with emphasis on developing conversational skills.  Topics vary by section but may include Pop Culture, Music, Film, Literature, Art, Social Media, Journalism of the Portuguese-speaking world.

title: Exploring Brazilian Pop Culture
description: Introduction to Brazilian pop culture through an examination of music, film, literature, sports, and other forms of expression. Preparation for further study in Portuguese language and Brazilian culture. Taught in English.

title: Special Topics in Portugese
description: Independent study of Portuguese language and/or culture.  With content changes, may be repeated for up to 6 credits.

title: Introduction to Citizenship and Community Service
description: Designed to introduce students to shared public challenges and to help them cultivate the knowledge, skills, commitments, partnerships, and imagination required to orient their emerging expertise toward human flourishing and the common good. Scholarship is enriched by learning outside the classroom through engagement activities such as research, service, and reflection. May be repeated a maximum of four times with a different topic each time.

title: Learning for the World
description: Focuses on pathways to excellence in scholarship and citizenship for students, enabling them to connect their transformational work in the classroom to opportunities for immersion, application, and engagement. Students will work with local nonprofits to learn how to address persistent community needs and challenges.

title: Community Law Enforcement
description: Examination of the criminal justice system, law enforcement, police, courts, and the corrections system through service-learning, as well as classroom instruction.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Community-Based Global Learning
description: Explore concepts of power and privilege relating to global citizenship, service, and community-driven efforts. Students will be assigned leadership roles related to Engaged Learning courses at Baylor and will experience ethical, critical, and decolonial community-based learning and research practices.

title: Leadership and Social Change
description: Focuses on theory and practice of leading groups toward positive social change locally and globally, emphasizing issues such as poverty, public education, and systemic inequality. Students will participate in community engagement projects and activities on best practices for leading change.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Social Innovation Lab
description: Designed to help students design and implement projects that address civic and social challenges in partnership with community stakeholders.

title: Community Education & Engagement
description: Students apply their discipline-specific expertise to create educational programming for the broader community. Through hands-on projects, students translate scholarly knowledge into accessible, meaningful learning experiences that promote public understanding and engagement.

title: Citizenship, Community, and Service Learning
description: Intensive integration of academic study with service learning opportunities in the community. Course emphasis will vary by semester. Seminar discussions, readings, and personal reflection will enrich the community volunteer experience. A minimum of three hours per week of community service is required.

title: Public Policy Innovation and the Common Good
description: The role of public and private actors in the making and implementation of public policies directed to address complex problems. Special focus on the collaborative efforts of government and nonprofit entities as they seek solutions that contribute to the public good.

title: Philanthropy and Fundraising
description: Provides perspective, application, and contextual reflection on the work of philanthropy, fundraising, and organizational development. This course qualifies for credit in the Engaged Learning distribution list.

title: Law, Justice and the Community
description: Introduction to legal practice. Contains community service component and required internship in legal offices.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Social & Economic Innovation
description: Develop theoretical and historical frameworks to understand how social and economic innovation can be employed to promote human flourishing. Students participate outside class in social and economic innovation in the community through partner-led projects and interactions.

title: Civic & Community Innovation
description: Develop understanding of how institutions across the public, private, and social sectors can collaborate and innovate to promote human flourishing.  Involves partnerships with the City of Waco in which students apply learning to promote community improvement initiatives.

title: Christian Social Innovation
description: Examines historical, theological, and theoretical frameworks, along with strategies that encourage Christians to build, renew, and transform institutions. Students spend time outside class working with institutions throughout the community to promote human flourishing.

title: Nonprofits, the Social Sector, & Philanthropic Innovation
description: Explores the role of nonprofit organizations and the social sector in a democratic society. Evaluates effective practices in consultation with nonprofit leaders and engages with nonprofit partners to explore new innovations for social impact.

title: Philanthropy, Civil Society, & the Public Good
description: The role of philanthropy and civil society in public problem solving with an introduction to ethics and importance of financial giving and community investments.  Students will spend significant time outside class working with foundations and nonprofits.

title: Philanthropy and Public Service Internship
description: Internship with a philanthropic or public-service oriented organization supplemented by research, professional development workshops, and seminars.

title: Advanced Study in Philanthropy and Social Innovation
description: Advanced study in philanthropy, the social sector, and social innovation, with emphasis on cross-sector collaboration and innovative approaches to pursuing the public good.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Professional Development
description: Resume and cover letter writing, interviewing, networking, and presenting professional self. Exploration of career options, including assessments, internships, job search process, and graduate school admission.

title: Professional Development Internship
description: Provides students, with assignments, exercises, and career development strategies to help them with their future professional pursuits. This is a 0-3 variable credit hour course that may be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours.

title: New Student Experience in Psychology and Neuroscience
description: An introduction to the PSY and NSC majors including the faculty and degree requirements of their major, techniques for academic success, and professional and career options.  Satisfies U1000 requirements, and is required of all first-year students with a declared major in Pre-PSY or Pre-NSC.

title: Psychological Science: Understanding Human Behavior
description: A survey course investigating the connections among mind, brain, and behavior.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: An introduction to historical and modern views of abnormal behavior and a survey of the field of psychological disorders.

title: Survey/Human Development
description: A survey of the field of developmental psychology. Consideration is given to the development of human behavior from conception to death.

title: Statistics
description: An introduction to descriptive and inferential statistics used in behavioral science research. Emphasis is placed on techniques of data analysis through the use of computers.

title: Research Methods in Psychology
description: Research methods used in psychology, including various research designs, appropriate statistical analyses for the research designs, and writing reports using APA style.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Peer Leadership and Mentoring
description: Instructor approval required. Application of leadership, mentoring, and pedagogical techniques, and the practice of student engagement in a classroom setting. May be repeated once for credit.

title: Laboratory in Cognition
description: Introduction to the structures and processes involved in human cognition, including thinking, problem solving, language, attention, and memory.

title: Laboratory in Learning and Behavior
description: See NSC 3120 for course information.

title: Laboratory in Sensation and Perception
description: See NSC 3123 for course information.

title: Theories of Psychotherapy and Counseling
description: The major schools and current trends in psychotherapy. Discussion also includes issues involving ethical practices and empirical aspects of psychotherapy.

title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
description: Students will learn about models of training in clinical psychology and the basic tools used by clinical psychologists in research, assessment/diagnosis, and treatment.

title: Social Psychology
description: A study of individual behavior as affected by cultural and social stimuli.

title: Cognition
description: Introduction to the structures and processes involved in human cognition, including thinking, problem solving, language, attention, and memory.

title: Health Psychology
description: Students explore how psychological factors influence health and well-being while working directly with community partners to apply theory, analyze real-world health challenges, and support meaningful health-promotion efforts.

title: Psychology and Culture
description: Adapts a cultural lens to evaluate psychological research and theories with the goal of having a more accurate understanding of the global human experience. Students will learn and apply their knowledge with community partners via a community outreach project.

title: Industrial/Organizational Psychology
description: A survey of personnel and organizational psychology. Topics include job satisfaction, leadership, formal organizational structures and issues relevant to the human relations field.

title: Psychology of Memory
description: An introduction to the study of human memory, including both laboratory studies and clinical data. This information will be related to major contemporary theories of memory, as well as to a range of phenomena including mnemonics, eyewitness testimony, retention of course content, and supranormal memory. Demonstrations and experiments will complement lecture material.

title: Learning and Behavior
description: An examination of human and animal learning and behavior within a nature-nurture framework.

title: Abnormal Psychology
