title: Methods of Social Research
description: Basic methods and techniques used in social research.

title: Social Theory
description: Social theory from its origins in philosophy to current efforts at theory construction. Particular schools are critically evaluated, and recent convergences in social anthropology, sociology, and social psychology are noted.

title: Sociology of Aging
description: See SWO 4393 for course information.

title: Aging and Mental Health
description: See SWO 4395 for course information.

title: Senior Colloquium
description: Capstone course providing theoretical and methodological overview of the discipline and professionalization.  Requires a full sociological research presentation and paper including scholarly literature review, research design and analysis, and interpretation of findings.

title: Special Topics in Sociology
description: Designed for students who wish to study with a professor in an area of sociology not covered by regular course offerings. Students will contract with professor regarding study and number of semester hours. May be repeated for credit up to a total of six semester hours, provided topic is different. For sociology majors with upper-level standing only.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Spanish Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Spanish Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Elementary Spanish
description: Introduction to SPA language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).  Progress through novice proficiency levels

title: Elementary Spanish
description: Introduction to Spanish language and cultures; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).  Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Elementary Spanish for Heritage Speakers
description: Intended for students who have prior exposure to Spanish but have not studied the language formally. Improvement of Spanish as used in the academic and professional world and development of historical consciousness about the Hispanic presence in the USA. Practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress through novice or novice mid-level proficiency levels.

title: Accelerated Elementary Spanish
description: An intensive course in first-year college Spanish especially for students with two-three years of high school Spanish. Covers the content of SPA 1301 and 1302 in only one semester.  As grammar is reviewed, emphasis is placed on the major skill areas (ACTFL Guidelines): reading, writing, listening, speaking, and cultural awareness.

title: Intermediate Spanish for Heritage Speakers
description: Designed for students who have prior exposure to Spanish but may not have studied it formally. Review of grammar, exploration of SPA-speaking cultures, and practice at intermediate-level proficiency to develop speaking and writing skills for professional contexts. May be substituted for SPA 2310 and SPA 2320 and used in fulfillment of the FL req.

title: Intermediate Spanish
description: Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading).

title: Intermediate Spanish for Teachers
description: Development of communication skills within a school (K-12) context with attention to vocabulary building, grammar review, conversation, composition, and cultural readings. Designed primarily for students in the School of Education, this course may be substituted for SPA 2310 to fulfill the foreign language requirement in Arts and Sciences.

title: Exploring the Spanish-Speaking World
description: An exploration of the cultures of the Spanish-speaking world with continued practice in listening, speaking, reading, and writing Spanish at the intermediate level. Class conducted in Spanish, with emphasis on developing conversational skills.  Topics vary by section but may include Pop Culture, Music, Film, Literature, Art, Social Media, Journalism of the Spanish-speaking world.

title: Intermediate Spanish for Health Professions
description: Development of intermediate level skills in comprehension, speaking, reading, writing, and cultural interpretation with an emphasis on medical and scientific issues for prospective medical and health-care professionals. May be substituted for SPA 2320 . Must be taken in residence at Baylor in order to count toward the Certificate in Spanish for Health Professions.

title: Spanish for Christian Ministry
description: Development of vocabulary, grammar, composition, and conversation skills in Spanish for students interested in Christian ministry, using the Bible and related religious materials. May be substituted for SPA 2320 .

title: Spanish for Business
description: Development of communication skills at the intermediate and low-advanced proficiency levels for reading, writing, speaking and listening.  Focus on using Spanish in Business contexts and examines the business cultures of the Spanish-speaking world.

title: Accelerated Intermediate Spanish
description: Intensive intermediate Spanish course geared toward strong language learners. Content of Spanish 2310 and 2320 in one semester.  Continued development of Spanish language skills, including listening, speaking, reading and writing.  Emphasis on cultural and literary readings.  May be substituted for SPA 2310 and SPA 2320 and used in fulfillment of the foreign language requirement.

title: Spanish for Health Professions Certificate Project
description: An individually driven project to be carried out in tandem with a Spanish course at the 3000 or 4000 level. Subject matter must be health-related and the project must be completed in Spanish. Approval of the Director of the Certificate in Spanish for Health Professions required.

title: Conversation and Composition: Spanish for Christian Ministry
description: Development of vocabulary, grammar, composition, and conversation skills in Spanish for students interested in Christian ministry, using the Bible and readings of a spiritual nature. This course includes an Engaged Learning Component.

title: Advanced Spanish for Heritage Speakers
description: Designed for students who have prior exposure to Spanish but may not have studied it formally. Readings and discussion about Spanish-speaking communities worldwide and practice at advanced-level proficiency to develop speaking and writing skills for professional contexts. May be substituted for SPA 3302 . This course includes an Engaged Learning Component.

title: Introduction to Hispanic Literature
description: Readings of representative authors and genres (poetry, prose fiction, drama, and essay) from around the Spanish-speaking world.  Includes an introduction to literary research in Spanish.

title: Introduction to Spanish Language and Linguistics
description: An introduction to the study of language and linguistics in general and of the sounds, structure, and meaning of Spanish in particular.

title: Spanish Culture and Civilization
description: Study of Spanish history and culture.

title: Latin American Culture and Civilization
description: Latin American history and culture.

title: Spanish Linguistics for Healthcare
description: Examines key linguistic devices for effective health care communication.

title: Spanish in the Workplace
description: Applies linguistic strategies to improve Spanish communication skills for professional environments such as business, healthcare, education, customer service, and public service.

title: Medieval and Early Modern Iberian Literature
description: Literary selections from the Iberian Middle Ages through the emergence of Spain as a nation and an imperial power in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

title: Modern and Contemporary Spanish Literature
description: Representative works of Spanish literature from the eighteenth century to the present.

title: Latin American Literature from Conquest through Independence
description: Representative works of Latin American literature from the Spanish conquest to the nineteenth century, tracing the course of the literary tradition through the colonial period and the struggle for independence to the emergence of Latin American modernity.

title: Modern Latin American Literature
description: Representative works of Latin American literature from 1880 to the present.

title: Research Writing: Hispanic Culture, Linguistics, and Literature
description: Focuses exclusively on research and writing in three areas of Hispanic studies: linguistics, literature, and culture. Students will develop a topic, create a bibliography and thesis statement, and write their findings in a professional level paper. Publication is the main objective.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: AVANT Spanish Language Proficiency Test
description: The AVANT Spanish Language Proficiency Test is a mandatory exit interview for Spanish majors and secondary majors before graduation. AVANT provides students with information about their Spanish proficiency level that may be leveraged on the job market. All costs of the test are covered by IMLC.

title: Spanish Sociolinguistics
description: Study of the close relationship between language and society and its implications in Spanish-speaking communities. Introduction to the methodologies and tools of sociolinguistics.

title: Methods of Foreign Language Teaching
description: Theoretical and historical bases with practical applications in communication methodology.  Introduction to professional activities and materials. Although this course cannot be counted toward the first or second teaching field, it will be counted toward a major in Spanish.

title: Introduction to Idiomatic Spanish
description: Study of the usage of frequent words and constructions in the Spanish language including idiomatic formulas and grammar points.

title: 21st Century Latin American Cinema
description: Students will learn how filmmaking has represented Latin America through close analysis of both feature-length and short films and by applying methodologies and theories from humanities and the arts. Discussion will be in Spanish.

title: Cultural Representations of the Spanish Civil War: Word, Image, and Memory
description: Examines the cultural production of and about the Spanish Civil War across a range of media, including works of visual art, photography, music, literature, and film.

title: Topics in Hispanic Language and Linguistics
description: A study of a topic related to the Spanish language or a field within Hispanic or Lusophone Linguistics. May be repeated for credit if topic is different.

title: Special Problems
description: Independent study in Spanish language and literature in consultation with a professor. Examination and term paper. With content changed, this course may be repeated once for credit.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Research Techniques I
description: The scientific process and common techniques and instrumentation used in biology, neuroscience and psychology research labs, including scientific literacy, data analysis and interpretation, and the breadth of scientific exploration.

title: Research Techniques II
description: Techniques and instrumentation for chemistry and biochemistry research labs; toxicology as it applies to all science disciplines.

title: Research Problems in Science
description: Research project conducted under the supervision of a science research faculty member. Techniques and overall goals of the research group, including the details of a particular research project. This course may be repeated for a maximum of 8 total hours if research continues.

title: Senior Research Seminar I
description: Lab work and data analysis in preparation for presentation and possible publication, supervised by a faculty member.

title: Senior Research Seminar II
description: Presentation of research in a formal oral presentation to faculty and SRF peers, supervised by a faculty member.

title: Senior Research Problems in Science
description: Independent research project as determined by the faculty research advisor. Students will collect enough scientific data for publication.  This course may be repeated for a maximum of 12 total hours if research continues.

title: Academic and Personal Success
description: This course guides students to develop tools for effective learning and productive self-management in the Baylor academic environment.  Topics focus on strategies for note taking, test preparation, test taking, and on methods for dealing with procrastination, stress, and test anxiety as well as for grade improvement, establishing academic priorities, and identification of an academic major and potential career.  Some sections of the course offer five-week workshops while other sections provide the opportunity for weekly mentoring by a trained graduate student or for an independent study on a relevant topic.  Sections will have varying titles descriptive of content. This Pass/Fail course may be attempted two times.

title: Academic and Personal Achievement
description: A graded, one-hour course facilitating development of essential skills for learning and self-management in the college academic environment.  Topics might include strategies for note taking, test preparation, grade improvement as well as tools for dealing with procrastination, stress, test anxiety, and goal-setting.  The focus of a course section might be linked with a particular profession, discipline or student population.

title: College Success: Freshmen
description: Designed to enhance the transition of freshmen students to Baylor University.  Provides direct exposure to specific campus support services, personal and professional development advice and practice, academic success strategies, and day-to-day tools to navigate the rigorous curricular and co-curricular environment at Baylor.

title: Transferring Your Success
description: Designed to enhance the transition of transfer students to Baylor and beyond.  Provides direct exposure to specific campus support services, personal and professional development advice and practice, academic success strategies, and day-to-day tools to navigate the rigorous curricular and co-curricular environment at Baylor.  Will be offered as a New Student Experience (NSE) course.

title: Academic and Personal Success for International Students
description: This course is for academically at-risk international students to develop effective learning and successful self-management strategies in the university. Topics focus on notetaking, study skills, and English language skills, along with methods for dealing with time management, stress, anxiety, and motivation. Students meet weekly with an instructor for individualized academic success planning and strategizing. This course may only be taken once.

title: Pathfinders Undergraduate Research Exploration Program Seminar
description: The Pathfinders course  provides undergraduate students in their first year at Baylor with the tools and resources necessary to identify research opportunities and apply for research and graduate preparation programs such as the McNair Scholars Program. The course provides content to support students in exploring research, professional opportunities, and applying to internships.

title: Strategic Learning for College Academics
description: (Course is intended for students with a term or cumulative GPA of 2.5 or less.)   Prepare students to exercise responsibility for their own learning by developing strategies for reading, understanding, analyzing, organizing, and applying information presented through lecture, textbooks, and other materials in the college classroom.  Skills for taking notes, completing assignments, preparing for tests, and taking tests as well as strategies for accessing resources, managing time, dealing with stress, and setting priorities will also be developed.

title: Strategic Learning
description: Prepare students to exercise responsibility for their own learning by developing strategies for reading, analyzing, organizing, and applying information presented through lecture, textbooks, and other materials in the college classroom.  Skills for taking notes, completing assignments, preparing for and taking tests, as well as strategies for accessing resources, managing time, dealing with stress, and setting priorities will be developed.

title: Academic Success Strategies for International Students
description: This graded course prepares international students to take responsibility for their own learning by developing strategies for comprehending and applying information presented in the college classroom. Students will also develop essential study skills like notetaking, time management, test preparation, dealing with stress, and setting priorities. Individualized meetings with the instructor are also incorporated into the course. This course may only be taken once.

title: Statistical Reasoning: A Guide to the Unknown
description: Philosophical, ethical, and sociological issues related to statistical uncertainty and randomness.

title: Elementary Statistics
description: Introduction to traditional statistical concepts including descriptive statistics, binomial and normal probability models, tests of hypotheses, linear correlation and regression, two-way contingency tables, and one-way analysis of variance. Credit may not be obtained after receiving credit in STA 2381 or 3381 .

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to Data Science
description: Principles of data science, including problem workflow, variable types, visualization, modeling, programming, data management and cleaning, reproducibility, and big data.

title: Data Communication with AI
description: Prepares students to transform data into compelling stories and visualizations while learning to use generative AI responsibly. Topics include cleaning, summarizing, and graphing real data sets, using AI tools to draft and critique narratives, and building reproducible workflows.

title: Introductory Statistical Methods
description: Parametric statistical methods. Topics range from descriptive statistics through regression and one-way analysis of variance. Applications are typically from biology and medicine. Computer data analysis is required.

title: Introduction to Computing for the Mathematical and Statistical Sciences
description: Computer programming for mathematical scientists with emphasis on designing algorithms, problem solving, and coding practices. Topics include development of programs from specifications; appropriate use of data types;  functions; modular program organization; documentation and style; and version control and collaborative programming.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Professional Development for Statistics Majors
description: A one-hour course that prepares junior-level students for professional pathways including actuarial science, graduate school, sports analytics, and data science. Students will develop professional materials, learn career relevant communication skills, engage with industry and academic speakers, and create a polished GitHub portfolio project.

title: History of Statistics: English Origins
description: Examines historical beginnings of the statistics discipline in England. Considers philosophical tenets from the Bayesian and frequentist perspectives as well as the debates between Fisher and Pearson. Emphasizes significance of such sites as Rothamsted Research Station, Bletchley Park, and the University of Cambridge, all linked to the birthplace of modern statistics.

title: Sports Analytics I
description: Combines classical statistical methods with cutting-edge data science tools to communicate findings and wield influence over decisions within sports organizations.  Fosters critical thinking, equipping students with statistical techniques for data analytics, and mastering data visualization to facilitate data-driven choices in sports.

title: Sports Analytics II
description: Delves deeper into sports analytics, emphasizing sophisticated statistical models and data manipulation techniques to refine predictions and strategies in sports settings.

title: Technologies for Sports Analytics
description: Concepts in big data analytics primarily applied to topics in sports focusing on graphical methods through dashboards and inferential methods.

title: Probability and Statistics
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of probability, random variables, discrete and continuous probability distributions, expectations, sampling distributions, topics of statistical inference such as confidence intervals, tests of hypotheses, and regression.

title: Undergraduate Research in Statistics
description: Independent study or research in topics not available in other courses. Maximum of four hours will count toward the degree.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Introduction to Experimental Design
description: Examines a variety of complex experimental designs that are available to researchers including split-plot factorial designs, confounded factorial designs, fractional factorial designs, incomplete block designs, and analysis of covariance. The designs are examined within the framework of the general linear model. Extensive use is made of computer software.

title: Financial Mathematics for Actuaries
description: Examines the theory of interest and its applications in actuarial science. Topics include present and accumulated value, annuities, loans, bonds, and related financial instruments. Emphasis on problem-solving techniques used in actuarial practice.

title: SAS Programming for Statistical Science
description: Concepts in SAS programming including methods to establish and transform SAS data sets, perform statistical analyses, and create general customized reports.  Methods from both BASE SAS and SAS SQL will be considered.

title: Statistical Machine Learning
description: Learning algorithms including classification methods, support vector machines, decision trees, neural networks, and deep learning are included.

title: Bayesian Data Analysis
description: An introduction to Bayesian inference emphasizing prior and posterior distributions, estimation, prediction, hierarchical Bayesian analysis, and applications with computer implemented data analysis. An introduction to Bayesian inference emphasizing prior and posterior distributions, estimation, prediction, hierarchical Bayesian analysis, and applications with computer implemented data analysis.

title: Applied Time Series Analysis
description: Statistical methods of analyzing time series. Model identification, estimation, forecasting, and spectral analysis will be discussed.   Applications in a variety of areas including economics and environmental science will be considered.

title: Sampling Techniques
description: Planning, execution, and analysis of sampling from finite populations. Simple random, stratified random, ratio, systematic, cluster, sub sampling, regression estimates, and multi-frame techniques are covered.

title: Data Management and Mining
description: Terminology, techniques, and management of Data Mining for biostatisticians.

title: Introduction to Biostatistics
description: Data Analysis for biostatisticians in the biomedical and pharmaceutical fields.

title: Computational Methods in Statistics
description: Computational methods using statistical packages and programming.

title: Applied Multivariate Methods
description: Numerical and graphical descriptive statistics for multivariate data,  principal components and factor analysis, canonical correlation, discriminant analysis, multivariate analysis of variance, multidimensional contingency tables, and cluster analysis.

title: Introduction to Probability Models
description: Applications of probability theory to the study of phenomena in such fields as engineering, management science, social and physical sciences, and operations research. Topics include Markov chains, branching processes, Poisson processes, exponential models, and continuous-time Markov chains with applications to queuing systems. Other topics introduced are renewal theory and estimation procedures.

title: Capstone in Sports Analytics
description: Applying statistics data science methodology to research problems in sports analytics.

title: Capstone Statistics Course
description: Statistical concepts applied to written and oral reports for consulting. For students majoring in statistics.

title: Special Topics in Statistics
description: Topics in probability and/or statistics not covered in other courses. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 hours if the content is different.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Swahili Language Workshop
description: For music students,  supplemental language training to accompany regular 1301 language sections.

title: Swahili Language Workshop
description: For music students, supplemental language training to accompany regular 1302 language sections.

title: Elementary Swahili
description: Introduction to Swahili language and cultures of East/Central Africa; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress through novice proficiency level

title: Elementary Swahili
description: Introduction to Swahili language and cultures of East/Central Africa; practice in three communicative modes: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading). Progress from novice toward intermediate-level proficiency

title: Intermediate Swahili I
description: Practice in three communicative modes at intermediate-level proficiency: interpersonal, presentational (speaking, writing), and interpretive (listening, reading)

title: Intermediate Swahili II
description: Continuation of SWA 2310 .

title: Syriac
description: See REL 4304 for course information.

title: Introduction to Mental Health Professions
description: New student experience course designed to help students acclimate to college life with a focus on learning about mental health and professional paths that serve this aspect of holistic wellness.

title: Social Work and New Beginnings at Baylor
description: New student experience course designed to help students acclimate to college life with a specific focus on vocational calling as defined within the social work profession.

title: Human Flourishing & Social Issues
description: Through this course, students will consider barriers to human flourishing in contemporary society through experiential learning opportunities in both residential and community contexts. Students will draw on the connections between leadership, social change, and human flourishing; seeking to develop a deeper understanding of self and their vocation interests in a global context.

title: Intro to Mental Health Professions
description: This course focuses on learning about mental health and professional paths that serve this aspect of holistic wellness.

title: Human Diversity and Leadership
description: This course creates opportunities to increase understanding of diversity and difference, power and privilege, and oppression—as well as an understanding of oneself.  Students will look at diversities within religion, ability, age, sexual orientation in addition to race/ethnicity, class, gender. This is an essential foundation for culturally competent leadership in any professional or volunteer setting.

title: Introduction to Social Work
description: An introduction to the profession of social work and the institution of social welfare. Emphasis is on common human needs and problems, and the development and functioning of social welfare as an institution of society. Course content includes history, knowledge base, values, and skills of professional social work as well as contexts for practice and career opportunities.

title: Special Topics
description: Provides instruction in areas of social work knowledge, values, and/or skills that are not available in the standard social work baccalaureate or graduate curricula. Course may be repeated up to six times with different topics of study, not to exceed a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Introduction to Gerontology
description: See SOC 3305 for course information.

title: Human Behavior and the Social Environment I
description: Human development across the life course with emphasis on the effects of the social and physical environment on maturation. Special attention given to gender issues, experiences of racial and ethnic minorities, and other special populations.

title: Human Behavior and the Social Environment II
description: The environmental contexts for human behavior with a special emphasis on groups, organizations and communities. Issues of human diversity, social and economic justice, and environments of populations at risk are emphasized.

title: Generalist Practice with Individuals and Families
description: In this course, students practice skill development by applying the steps of the generalist intervention model (engagement, assessment, planning, intervention, evaluation, and termination/transition) to create diversity sensitive care management plans for individuals and families.

title: Social Work Research
description: The basic purpose, methods and techniques of social research, evaluation of professional social work practice, and social program evaluation.

title: Data Analysis for Social Work
description: Apply basic statistical tools to describe, analyze, and evaluate research issues in professional social work practice.

title: Organizational Leadership & Management
description: This course prepares students to participate in managerial functions in public, nonprofit, and faith-based human service organizations. Specific topics include leadership, Human Resources, fundraising, grant writing, governance, and efforts to link human service organizations in an integrated community-wide service delivery system.

title: Interfaith & Civic Engagement
description: Interfaith & Civic Engagement is a course designed to explore theoretical and practical issues related to engagement across and inclusive of different faiths. Students will discuss various civic and interfaith practices related to leadership, policy, and religious identity. Students will engage cultural and religious humility while considering their own sense of formation and worldview through reading, writing, and class discussion, to cultivate personal growth, civic capacity, and intercultural skills.

title: Social Policy for Social Work Practice
description: Historical and current patterns of provision of social welfare services, the effect of social policy on people gaining optimal health and well-being, and the effect of social policy on social work practice.

title: Advanced Policy Frameworks: Poverty and Economic Inequality
description: This course explores social policy related to poverty and economic inequality. Utilizing social justice and anti-oppressive frameworks and building on content from SWO 4322 the course emphasizes economic policies and financial concepts as related to individuals and families across the life cycle, as well as communities, particularly those on the margins of society and experiencing poverty and near-poverty. The course examines and integrates research, social policy, policy advocacy, and policy change efforts related to poverty.

title: Generalist Practice with Groups
description: Knowledge and skills appropriate to social work intervention with small treatment and task groups representing different backgrounds, building on the skills learned in SWO 3371 with individuals and families.

title: Generalist Practice with Organizations and Communities
description: Knowledge, skills and strategies appropriate to work with organizations, neighborhoods, and communities to foster social and economic justice.

title: Statistics for Social Workers
description: Apply basic statistical tools to describe, analyze, and evaluate research issues in professional social work practice.

title: Social Work Internship I
description: First semester of Field internship requiring 240 hours of Social Work practice with supervision by a professional social worker within a local setting and a weekly integrative seminar. Requires integration of liberal arts background and social work knowledge, skills, values, and ethics within the structure of the agency, demonstrating beginning competence around each of the program's educational objectives and competencies.

title: Social Work Internship II
description: Second semester of Practicum internship requiring integration of liberal arts background and Social Work knowledge, skills, values, and ethics through two hundred forty hours of supervised practice in a community agency and a weekly integrative seminar. Student must demonstrate competence around each of the program's educational objectives and competencies.

title: Special Topics in Social Work
description: Course for students who wish to study an area of social work not covered by a formal course. May be repeated for up to a total of six hours credit when the subject matter varies.

title: Social Work Research Experience
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Instructional Technology Lab
description: The TED technology lab prepares teacher candidates for effective personal and classroom use of technology resources. Students will meet and exceed Texas State Board for Educator Certification (SBEC) requirements.

title: Introduction to Education I
description: This course provides exposure to and experiences within the variety of career opportunities available to professional educators. Students will explore education through the lenses of informal education, Christian and private education, teacher education, and leadership in education.

title: Instructional Technology Lab II
description: The TED 2112 technology lab extends and builds on TED 1112 in preparing teacher candidates for effective personal and professional use of technology resources. At the conclusion of the Baylor teacher education program, candidates will meet and exceed Texas State Board for Educator Certification (SBEC) requirements as outlined in the Technology Applications Standards for All Beginning Teachers.

title: Educational Technology in Learning Environments
description: This course introduces the fundamentals of educational technology in both traditional and non-traditional learning spaces. Students will explore a range of digital tools and emerging technologies, evaluating their effectiveness and determining how they can be meaningfully integrated into various learning environments. Emphasis is placed on critical evaluation, instructional planning, and the strategic use of technology to support diverse learners. Through a variety of course activities the students will develop the skills necessary to design and implement technology-enhanced educational experiences that align with instructional goals and learner needs.

title: Teaching in the Elementary School
description: An introduction to the role of the teacher in the elementary school. A field experience is included.

title: Teaching in the Middle Grades
description: An introduction to the role of the teacher in the middle school. A field experience is included.

title: Teaching in Secondary Schools
description: An introduction to the role of the teacher in the secondary school. A field experience is included.

title: Teaching Special Education
description: This course provides initial preparation in the role of a special education teacher. Students will learn teaching strategies for students with disabilities that are then applied in a one-to-one teaching experience.

title: Teaching Physical Education
description: This course provides teacher candidates with an introduction to the role of the all-level physical education instructor. Course includes a field experience in grades K - eight.

title: Introduction to Education II
description: This course will continue the exploration of the field of education to include the history of Western educational thought from the ancient Greek ideas of Plato and Aristotle to modern naturalistic theorists.

title: American Educational Thought
description: This course will address the history of American educational thought from the founding of the Republic to the present day. Individuals to be considered will include Thomas Jefferson, Horace Mann, William James, and John Dewey.

title: Social Issues in Education Lab
description: Varied action-oriented field experiences that allow students to develop a depth of knowledge and experience in the topics explored in TED 3380 . Through field based experiences in local organizations and schools, students will have the opportunity to examine how social issues play out in real-life settings.

title: Early Literacy Learning
description: Preparation of pre-service teachers to plan, organize, and implement an early literacy program that ensures that young children will be reading beginning materials independently by the end of the first grade.

title: Literacy Instruction in the Elementary School
description: An examination of methods of teaching reading in the elementary school. Includes atypical and typical language and literacy development in children in the elementary school.

title: Methods in Middle Grades English Language Arts
description: Introductions to typical and atypical literacy instruction in middle grades and an overview of cognitive, emotional, social, and physical development of adolescents. Includes reading, writing, oral language, and an introduction to literature for adolescents.

title: Methods in Middle Grades Science
description: This course will prepare candidates to plan and implement inquiry-based science instruction to meet the needs of diverse learners and increase their pedagogical repertoires.

title: Teaching Associate Secondary Part I
description: Practicum of one hundred hours in a local secondary classroom matching the selected content certification area. Course includes weekly seminars.

title: Teaching Associate Secondary Part II
description: Continuation of Teaching Associate Secondary Part I. Practicum of one hundred hours in a local secondary classroom matching the selected content certification area. Course includes weekly seminars.

title: School and Classroom Environment
description: Prepare the secondary education teacher candidate with research-based instructional and classroom management strategies. Provide opportunities for application of the strategies with students and opportunities for interaction with teachers at local secondary schools.

title: Foundations of Secondary Reading and Writing
description: A study of the typical and atypical developmental stages of reading and writing for secondary students. Focuses on instructional strategies in reading and writing to meet the needs of all students.

title: Methods in Middle Grades Social Studies
description: This course will expose middle school social studies candidates to the philosophies, methods and culturally relevant practices that nuance the field’s expressed disciplinary aims. Candidates will be asked to rethink traditional social studies instruction and develop transformational postures that will increase and diversify their pedagogical repertories. Concurrent enrollment in TED 3370 required.

title: Data Analysis and Instruction
description: An analysis of standardized assessments given at national, state, district, campus, and individual student levels. Planning and implementing instructional activities based on areas of need identified in the analysis. Field experiences are a critical element of the course.

title: Social Issues in Education
description: This course will explore cultural and social issues that influence education.  Concurrent enrollment in one hour field-based lab required.

title: Methods in Secondary Math
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in mathematics for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary Mathematics Practicum II
description: Continuation of Secondary Mathematics Practicum I. Course combines a practicum in a local secondary mathematics classroom with lecture and discussion about adapting instruction to meet the needs of diverse students, strategies for large and small groups, NCTM Standards, and teaching resources.

title: Methods in Secondary Social Studies
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in social studies for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary Social Studies Practicum II
description: Continuation of Secondary Social Studies Practicum I. Course combines a practicum in a local school with in-depth discussion of instructional design, technology in the classroom, and assessment. The course also includes overview of content included in secondary history, geography, economics and civics education courses.

title: Methods in Secondary Science
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in science for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary Science Practicum II
description: Course combines a practicum in a local school with in-depth discussion of instructional design, technology in the classroom, and assessment. The course also includes an overview of content of secondary science courses.

title: Secondary Foreign Language Practicum I
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in foreign language for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary Foreign Language Practicum II
description: Course combines a practicum in a local school with lecture and in-depth discussion of instructional design, technology in the classroom, and assessment and action research in the classroom.

title: Secondary Art Practicum I
description: Course combines a practicum in a local school with lecture and in-depth discussion of instructional design, components of a secondary art program, technology in the art classroom, assessment and action research in the classroom.

title: Secondary Art Practicum II
description: Continuation of Secondary Art Practicum I. Course combines a practicum in a local school with in-depth discussion of components of the art program, instructional design in art, technology in the classroom, and assessment in art classes.

title: Methods in Secondary English Language Arts
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in English/Language Arts for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary English Language Arts Practicum II
description: Continuation of Secondary English Language Arts Practicum I. Course combines practicum in a local secondary English classroom with lecture and discussion about adapting instruction in English to meet the needs of diverse students, strategies for building vocabulary, developing written and oral language, and an overview of the history of English.

title: Secondary Business Practicum I
description: This course prepares teacher candidates to adapt instruction in business courses for meeting the needs of diverse students, strategy implementation for large and small groups, application of state and national standards, and development of instructional resources.

title: Secondary Business Practicum II
description: A continuation of TED 3398 Secondary Business Practicum I. Course combines a practicum in a local school with lecture and in-depth discussion of the economic system, components of a secondary business program, technology in the classroom, leadership and career development, assessment of student learning, and action research in the business classroom.

title: Teaching Associate Elementary School Part I
description: Practicum of one hundred hours in the elementary school in a local classroom.  Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups in instruction in reading and social studies.  Course includes daily seminars.

title: Teaching Associate Elementary School Part II
description: Practicum of one hundred hours in the elementary school in a local classroom.  Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups in instruction in mathematics and science.  Course includes daily seminars.

title: Teaching Associate Middle Grades Part I
description: Practicum in a local middle grade classroom matching the selected content certification area. Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups of students. Course includes a weekly seminar.

title: Teaching Associate Middle Grades Part II
description: Continuation of TED 3630 . Practicum of one hundred hours in a local middle grade classroom matching the selected content certification areas. Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups of students. Course includes a weekly seminar.

title: Tchng Assoc Secondary Part I
description: Practicum of one hundred hours in a local secondary classroom matching the selected content certification area. Course includes weekly seminars.

title: Teaching Associate Secondary
description: Practicum in a local secondary classroom matching the selected content certification area. Course includes weekly seminars.

title: Teaching Associate EC-6 with Special Education and Gifted Education
description: In this course, teacher candidates teach small groups of early childhood through grade 6 general education with special education or gifted education students within the specific disciplinary areas that match their certificate areas.  The practicum consists of 97.5 hours in a professional development or partner school (7.5 hours per week). Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups of students.  Course includes seminars.

title: Teaching Associate Physical Education Part I
description: Practicum of one hundred hours teaching physical education in a local school. Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups of students. Course includes a weekly seminar.

title: Teaching Associate Physical Education Part II
description: Continuation of TED 3670 . Practicum of one hundred hours teaching physical education in a local school. Teacher candidates will work with small and large groups of students. Course includes a weekly seminar.

title: Elementary Reading - Integrated Language Arts
description: Students will develop strategies for facilitating communication skills and integrating the language arts across the curriculum.

title: Comprehension of Expository Texts
description: Preservice teachers will develop understanding of pedagogies that help elementary students develop proficiency in comprehending expository texts.

title: Science in the Elementary School
description: A study of the content of elementary science. Design and presentation of laboratory activities appropriate to various grade levels.

title: Social Studies in the Elementary School
description: The types, classification, and organization of social studies content. Emphasis on the integration of the historical, geographical, and civic content of the social studies.

title: Methods of Teaching English as a Second Language
description: Strategies for teaching English to non-English speaking students at the elementary and secondary levels. Emphasis will be placed upon developing specific instructional approaches appropriate for the individual's language background.

title: Bilingual Language Acquisition and Education
description: Examines childhood bilingual language acquisition and education and highlights how this understanding is critical to examining issues in bilingual education and promoting multilingual communities in an increasingly diverse U.S. society.

title: English Curriculum
description: Instructional methods and teaching aids and materials for the teaching of English in middle and secondary grades. Contemporary concerns relevant to the curriculum development of the English language arts in the components of language, composition, and literature will be stressed.

title: Science Curriculum
description: A survey of contemporary school science programs (all sciences) emphasizing their philosophies, designs, and unique methods and strategies for teaching in middle and secondary grades.

title: Mathematics in the Elementary Grades
description: Mathematics content, materials, and conceptual activities for the elementary school. The emphasis of the course is to prepare the elementary school teacher to implement a constructivist approach to the teaching of mathematics based on an understanding of the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM) standards. Includes laboratory experiences in teaching mathematics in the elementary school.

title: Mathematics in the Upper Elementary Grades
description: The student will be exposed to a wide range of issues and theories in mathematics curriculum and encouraged to relate these to his or her own teaching practices.  Opportunities for teaching and observation of teaching will be provided in order to analyze and reflect on teaching practices appropriate for upper elementary grade mathematics.

title: Methods in Middle Grades Math
description: Teaching strategies will be analyzed with developmental activities that can be used in middle grades mathematics. Candidates will reflect on what it means to teach mathematics for conceptual understanding and explore the factors that influence instruction. The NCTM Standards for grades 4-8 will be emphasized. Includes laboratory experiences in teaching mathematics in middle grades.

title: Social Studies Curriculum
description: Organization, content, research practices, and technology in the social studies curriculum in middle and secondary grades. Includes a discussion of current issues and standards in social studies teaching.

title: Secondary Mathematics Curriculum
description: Methods and activities used to actively engage secondary school students in the construction of mathematical concepts. This course includes development of instructional methods, teaching aids, and materials for the teaching of mathematics in the secondary classroom. Contemporary concerns relevant to the development in mathematics curriculum is included.

title: Critical Issues in Mathematics Education
description: Teacher candidates will investigate critical issues in the nature of knowledge and inquiry in middle grade and secondary school mathematics.  Candidates will explore current issues related to teaching practices and experiences. The NCTM Standards along with relevant research will provide a foundation.

title: Teaching Economic Geography in Social Studies
description: The focus of this course is to provide EC-4, 4-8, and 7-12 certificate level teacher education students an in-depth exploration of the curriculum and pedagogy of geography education in the social studies with special attention to the intersections of economics and geography. This emphasis guides the course themes which attend to critical geographic and economic approaches to understanding the world and taking informed action to change it.

title: Curriculum and Teaching in Informal Education Environments
description: This course is designed to engage students in educational program development and teaching experience in an informal setting. Students will examine the attributes of successful informal education (such as museums and zoos) and school partnerships and develop, create, and facilitate a curriculum or professional development project for an informal education site.

title: Capstone in Informal Education with Practicum
description: This course is designed as the final experience for students finishing their minor in informal education and preparing to enter the job market after graduation. Students engage in an immersive practicum experience in the field and design/develop and complete a professional project.

title: Student Teaching in Private Schools
description: A course designed to extend student teaching to a private school. Students will be involved in full-time student teaching for a minimum of four weeks.

title: International Internship Comparative Education
description: This program is designed to extend the teaching experience. For teaching candidates in the School of Education at Baylor University.  Teacher candidates will engage in cultural exchange, travel and education programs including three weeks of teaching and a comparative study of educational systems and instructional strategies.

title: Internship Elementary Part I
description: Full-time teaching experience in a professional development school.  A mentor teacher and resident faculty will support teacher candidates as they gradually assume complete responsibilities for teaching.  Course includes two eighty-minute seminars per week.

title: Internship Elementary Part II
description: Planning and preparing lessons and materials for full-time teaching. Includes completion of content modules, conferencing with mentor teacher and university instructor, written lesson reflections, observation of lessons taught by master teachers, and preparation of web-based portfolio.

title: Internship Elementary Part III
description: Continuation of TED 4620 and TED 4621 .

title: Internship Elementary Part IV
description: Continuation of TED 4620 and TED 4621 .

title: Internship Middle Grades Part I
description: Full-time teaching experience in a professional development or partner school. A mentor teacher and intern-supervisor will support teacher candidates as they gradually assume complete responsibilities for teaching. Course includes a 2-hour weekly seminar

title: Internship Middle Grades Part II
description: Planning and preparing lessons and materials for full-time teaching. Includes completion of content modules, conferencing with mentor teacher and intern-supervisor, written lesson reflections, observation of lessons taught, and experiences aligned with the teacher education standards.

title: Internship Middle Grades Part III
description: Continuation of TED 4630 and TED 4631 .

title: Internship Middle Grades Part IV
description: Continuation of TED 4630 and TED 4631 .

title: Internship Secondary Part I
description: Full-time teaching experience in a professional development school. A mentor teacher and resident faculty will support teacher candidates as they gradually assume complete responsibilities for teaching. Course includes one two hour seminar per week.

title: Internship Secondary Part II
description: Secondary Content Practicum.  Planning and preparing lessons and materials for full-time teaching.  Includes completion of content modules, conferencing with mentor teacher and university instructor, written lesson reflections, observation of lessons taught by master teachers, and preparation of electronic portfolio.

title: Internship Secondary Part III
description: Continuation of TED 4640 and TED 4641 .

title: Internship Gifted and Talented Part III
description: Continuation of EDP 4650 and EDP 4651 .

title: Internship Gifted and Talented Part IV
description: Continuation of EDP 4650 and EDP 4651 .

title: Internship Physical Education Part I
description: Full-time experience in professional development.  A mentor teacher and resident faculty will support teacher candidates as they gradually assume complete responsibilities for teaching.  Course includes two eighty-minute seminars per week.

title: Internship Physical Education Part II
description: Planning and preparing lessons and materials for full-time teaching.  Includes completion of content modules, conferencing with mentor teacher and university instructor, written lesson reflections, observation of lessons taught by master teachers, and preparation of web-based portfolio.

title: Internship Physical Education Part III
description: Continuation of TED 4670 and TED 4671 .

title: Internship Physical Education Part IV
description: Continuation of TED 4670 and TED 4671 .

title: Secondary School Teaching
description: A full-time daily supervised teaching experience in a local public school. Students collaborate with classroom teacher to provide instruction under the direction of a university supervisor. Includes weekly seminar with supervisor.

title: Special Topics in Teacher Education
description: Examination of special topics in teacher education focusing on pedagogy, curriculum, and PK-12 field experience professional development. May be repeated once under a different topic not to exceed six semester hours.

title: Beginning Science
description: Beginning science for summer program students.

title: Women's and Gender Studies: An Introduction
description: An interdisciplinary exploration of critical questions about the meaning of gender in society. Includes contemporary debates about appropriate gender roles, body image, violence against women, access to education and occupations, and women’s healthcare.

title: Independent Study in Women's and Gender Studies
description: Students collaborate with a faculty mentor of their choice to explore complex issues from multiple perspectives and to dive deeper into specific areas like gender equality, identity, and intersectionality. Fosters critical thinking and reflection through in-depth exploration of course material. May include experiential learning.

title: University Scholars Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: University Scholars Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Exit Interview HNR
description: Discussion of independent reading list and proposed thesis topic with a selected faculty committee.

title: University Scholars Capstone HNR
description: Special topics of study in modern and post-modern intellectual thought. Bridging genres and disciplines, the course encourages students to make connections with their previous study of Great Texts within the broad context of their University Scholars experience.

title: University Scholars Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Senior Thesis HNR
description: Submission of an introductory chapter and bibliography for thesis.

title: Senior Thesis HNR
description: Completion of thesis and approval by assigned committee.

title: University Scholars Independent Study HNR
description: Flexible credit optional. University Scholars Program for in-depth study of interdisciplinary topic not regularly offered in other traditional departments.  Topic of study must be approved by program director and instructor and may not relate to the University scholar's proposed thesis. May be repeated only with permission of director and instructor.

title: University Scholars Special Topics
description: This course designed to allow University Scholars to pursue lines of inquiry not otherwise available in the curriculum. Topics of the course determined by instructor in coordination with the University Scholars Director. Course may be repeated.

title: University Scholars Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken under the supervision of a faculty member. May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Fundamentals of Voice and Movement I
description: An introduction to the fundamentals of voice and movement for the actor with emphasis on freeing the natural voice and developing awareness of the voice and body as creative, expressive instruments. This course supports the work done in acting core courses.

title: Fundamentals of Voice and Movement II
description: A continuation of THEA 1101 with emphasis on the fundamentals of voice and movement for the actor, freeing the natural voice, and developing awareness of the voice and body as creative and expressive instruments.

title: Introduction to Musical Theatre Voice 1
description: Introduction to four of the five parts of healthy functional vocal production in Musical Theatre styles: Phonation, Relaxation/Alignment, Respiration and Articulation.

title: Introduction to Musical Theatre Voice 2
description: Introduction to Resonance (one of the five parts of healthy functional vocal production) in Musical Theatre styles with continued work on: Phonation, Relaxation/Alignment, Respiration and Articulation

title: The Fine Arts: An Experiential Survey of Music, Art, Theatre, and Film
description: An exploration of four Fine Arts disciplines (film, art, theatre, and music) from an interdisciplinary perspective, with emphasis on specific performances and exhibitions, and a small group focus on new student experiences.

title: Acting I: Realism
description: Introduction to the basics of acting, including theatrical terminology, improvisation, scene study, and character analysis. Emphasis will be placed on an acting method based on the works of Constantin Stanislavski.

title: Acting Realism: Scene Study
description: An introduction to basic acting technique. Students employ the character analysis, basic object exercises, and other acting tools toward producing a character study based on a modern American play.

title: Introduction to Theatre
description: Develops an engaged understanding of live theatre and performance in contemporary society through an overview of the various artists, skills, crafts, traditions, and conventions that inform the creation of a theatrical event. Students with credit in Thea 1306 may not earn credit for Thea 1206.

title: Scenic Elements
description: Basic course in stagecraft. Technical crew lab included.

title: Costume and Stage Makeup Elements
description: Study and application of the theories and techniques of theatrical costuming, construction of theatrical clothing, and theatrical makeup.  Technical crew lab included.

title: Script Analysis
description: Introduction to the process of script analysis, focusing on play structure, environmental research and character development, and culminating in a written play analysis.

title: Theatre Aesthetics: Visual Storytelling
description: An introduction to basic theatre aesthetics.  Emphasis placed on elements and principles of design for visual storytelling.

title: Theatrical Drafting
description: Introduction to theatrical hand drafting techniques, tools and standards, including fundamentals of graphic problem solving by visualization of elements in three dimensional space.

title: Applied Voice and Movement I
description: Continuation of the work begun in THEA 1101 and 1102 with emphasis on movement for the stage as it applies to realistic acting, as well as interpretive, post-modern theatre making.

title: Applied Voice and Movement II
description: Continuation of the work begun in THEA 2101 with emphasis on movement for the stage as it applies to realistic acting, as well as interpretive, post-modern theatre making.

title: Musical Theatre Voice
description: Beginning work on vocal technique in various musical theatre styles. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Sophomore Voice for Contemporary Musical Theatre
description: Acting technique and character development, in conjunction with vocal technique, as they relate to traditional musical theatre styles, in small group/ensemble roles with repertoire before 1965. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Singing for the Actor
description: Preparing the actor to participate in singing roles including ensemble work, straight plays with music, and Shakespeare sonnets.  The course will also cover vocal technique, sight singing, and harmony/group singing. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 hours.

title: Workshop in Traditional Musical Theatre I
description: Application of techniques and skills learned in the voice class in a performance setting using materials from traditional musical theatre styles pre-1965.

title: Workshop in Contemporary Musical Theatre I
description: Application of techniques and skills learned in the voice class in a performance setting using materials from contemporary musical theatre styles from 1965 to the present.

title: Ballet I
description: Ballet with emphasis on technique and vocabulary. Theatre Arts majors may substitute this class for one semester of Human Performance.

title: Jazz I
description: Jazz techniques, vocabulary, and styles relevant to character/theatre dance.  Theatre Arts majors may substitute this class for one semester of Human Performance.

title: Acting: Physical Methods
description: An Introduction to the physical methods of Acting, focusing on Viewpoints.

title: Acting: Shakespeare
description: Exploration of the acting challenges of Shakespeare's plays, through studies of text sense and structure, and exercises for developing breath, relaxation, vocal energy, and creative acting choices.

title: Stage Makeup
description: Study and application of theories and techniques of theatrical makeup. Technical crew laboratory included.

title: Technology Fundamentals for Theatre
description: Introduction to essential software and technologies utilized in the creation of a live theatrical production.

title: Lighting and Sound Elements
description: General principles and practices of stage lighting and theatrical sound technology.  Technical crew laboratory included.

title: Theater History I
description: Historical investigation of theatre practice, performance, and dramatic literature from ancient times through the Renaissance.

title: Theater History II
description: Historical investigation of theatre practice, performance, and dramatic literature from the Renaissance through the nineteenth century. Emphasis on Europe and North America.

title: Theatrical Drawing and Rendering
description: Principles and practices of drawing, rendering, and modeling techniques used for the theater.

title: Stage Management
description: The art and craft of stage management, including the role and responsibilities of the stage manager.

title: Production Lab
description: Evaluation of acting and crew work for all major productions, thesis production, qualifying productions, and weekly workshops. May be taken a maximum of 3 times.

title: Studio for Applied Musical Theatre Voice
description: Studio is a co-requisite for applied musical theatre voice. In studio class, songs are workshopped in order to gain confidence in acting technique, movement, and character development, in conjunction with vocal technique in various musical theatre styles. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 2103 , 2104 , 3103 , 3104 , 4103 , or 4104.

title: Advanced Musical Theatre Voice
description: Develops musical theatre vocal technique in various musical theatre styles, employing acting techniques, movement, musicality, and character development. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Junior Voice for Contemporary Musical Theatre
description: Acting technique and character development, in conjunction with vocal technique, as they relate to traditional musical theatre styles, in small group/ensemble roles with repertoire before 1965. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Production Lab II
description: Continuation of THEA 3105.

title: Workshop in Traditional Musical Theatre II
description: A continuation of THEA 2113 .

title: Workshop in Contemporary Musical Theatre II
description: A continuation of THEA 2114 .

title: Tap I
description: Tap with an emphasis on the basic steps, rhythms, and music theory related to the dance.

title: Ballet II
description: A continuation of Ballet I, refining technique and developing broader ranges of movement.

title: Acting: Advanced Realism
description: Correlating advanced acting techniques and the performance requirements of important realistic playwrights. Students will analyze, rehearse and perform scenes from playwrights of the early independent theater movement and from a range of contemporary playwrights.

title: Theatre for Young Audiences
description: An applied study of the dramatic literature and production techniques of theater intended for young audiences. Emphasis will be on techniques of acting and the creation of new material.

title: Acting: Playwright Study
description: The application of advanced acting techniques to the unique style and period conventions of an individual playwright. May be repeated once for credit with study of a different playwright(s).

title: Introduction to Standardized Patient Methodology
description: See HP 3312 for course information.

title: Costume Draping and Patterning
description: Technical skills with emphasis on interpreting two-dimensional drawings into three-dimensional costumes by manipulating fabric through draping, patterning, and construction.

title: Costume Crafts
description: Technical skills with an emphasis on 2D and 3D techniques in creating and modifying specialty costume pieces.

title: Directing I/Script Analysis
description: A study of play analysis for beginning directors. Workshop laboratory required.

title: Directing II
description: Continuation of THEA 3324 . A study of techniques of play direction. Fundamental consideration of blocking and director-actor communication. Workshop laboratory required.

title: Costume Design
description: Development and execution of costume designs for a variety of theatrical situations.  Lectures, design projects, critiques, and discussions.

title: Advanced Makeup Techniques
description: This course builds on introductory skills learned in THEA 1316 (Costume and Makeup Elements).  Students will study advanced application skills and tools for character transformation with makeup, masks, and 3D elements.

title: Theatre in Cultural Context
description: An exploration of theatre production and theatrical history particular to a specific region of the world, with references to regional historical, political, social, cultural, artistic, and architectural influences. Course may be repeated for a maximum of six semester hours if topic varies.

title: Master Works in Drama
description: See GTX 3341 for course information.

title: Japanese Theatre and Culture
description: See JPN 3352 for course information.

title: Metal-Working for the Theatre
description: Prepares students for various aspects of metal-working for the theatre, including safety, welding, cutting, and shaping.

title: Scene Painting
description: Introduction to large scale painting. Students execute texture techniques.

title: Short-form Playwriting
description: Development of techniques and skills of dramatic writing through practice in creating short-form original dramatic work.

title: Sound Design
description: The creation and implementation of sound design for theatre; also applicable to film, music production, video games, and live events.  Includes the artistic process of design and the development of technical abilities and conceptual approaches to sound manipulation.

title: Computer Aided Drafting and Modeling
description: Computer aided drafting to incorporate many of the principals covered in THEA 1383 (hand-drafting for the theater). Utilize both the vector based Vector works software and the floating point algorithm based AutoCAD software.

title: Marketing for the Performing Arts
description: Audience development principles and marketing techniques for both commercial and non-profit arts organizations. Emphasis on live theatre. Technical crew lab included.

title: Theatrical Props: Construction and Management
description: Practical study of the design, construction, organization, and implementation of theatrical properties in production.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Theatre in the Professional World
description: Prepares majors for the transition from academia to the professional arena.

title: Senior Voice for Traditional Musical Theatre
description: Acting technique and character development, in conjunction with vocal technique, as they relate to traditional musical theatre styles, in small group/ensemble roles with repertoire before 1965. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Senior Voice for Contemporary Musical Theatre
description: Acting technique and character development, in conjunction with vocal technique, as they relate to traditional musical theatre styles, in small group/ensemble roles with repertoire before 1965. This course must be taken concurrently with THEA 3003 .

title: Production Lab III
description: Evaluation of acting and crew work for all major productions, thesis production, qualifying productions, and weekly workshops.

title: Production Lab IV
description: A continuation of production methods learned in THEA 3106 .

title: Advanced Professional Development
description: Development of audition and interview materials required for Showcases, auditions, and employment in the Theatre. This course is reserved for Students attending the Senior Showcase in New York City.

title: Workshop in Traditional Musical Theatre III
description: A continuation of THEA 3113 .

title: Workshop in Contemporary Musical Theatre III
description: A continuation of THEA 3114 .

title: Portfolio Development
description: Development of design portfolios and presentation skills needed for professional employment in theatrical design.

title: Art as Civic Practice:  Engagement, Collaboration, and Inclusivity
description: Engagement, collaboration, and inclusivity through the lens of arts-based practices as they are related to three key groups: ourselves, organizations, and communities, including community-based artistic programming across fine arts entities.

title: Jazz II
description: Continuation of Jazz I, refining technique and exploring different styles of jazz and character dance.

title: Tap II
description: Continuation of Tap I, refining technique and exploring advanced rhythms and steps.

title: Musical Theatre Dance
description: Application of dance techniques and skills learned in Theatre Dance classes, focusing on the dance audition and the expectations for Theatrical dance practitioners.

title: Acting for the Camera
description: Preparation for professional performance in television and film.

title: Research Methods in Sustainable Design
description: Students will explore research modalities engaged by designers in performance, including movements in sustainable design, integrated scenography, and community engagement. They will also participate in engaged learning components to investigate real-world questions in diverse community contexts.

title: Advanced Theatre Theory
description: Great texts of dramatic theory ranging from Aristotle and Zeami through to Emile Zola and Bertolt Brecht.  Theoretical ideas on the aesthetics, efficacy and morality of theatre have interacted with plays at various time periods.

title: Automated Lighting
description: Design techniques and the programming of automated lighting fixtures on various lighting consoles.

title: Advanced Scene Design
description: Development and execution of scenic designs for a variety of theatrical situations. Lectures, design projects, critiques and discussions.

title: Advanced Lighting Design
description: Developing and executing lighting designs for a variety of situations. Lectures, design projects, cue composition, and critique and discussion.

title: Playwriting
description: A study of the art and craft of playwriting with an emphasis on play structure, development of the personal voice, use of dramatic irony, and character development.

title: Video Design for Live Performance
description: Explores the design and integration of digital media into live performance, specifically as it relates to theater.

title: Advanced Theatrical Props
description: Design, construction, organization, and implementation of theatrical properties in production; includes specialized training in additional tools and techniques.

title: Stage Combat
description: Actor movement in stage combat (armed and unarmed) with emphasis on proper technique, safety, and theatricality.  May be repeated once for credit with different contents.

title: Music Directing for Musical Theatre
description: A practical approach to teaching music, conducting ensembles, collaborating with directors, evaluating voices, and improving vocal technique.  Designed for students interested in learning how to Music Direct Musicals and Theatre performances. The students will audition, cast and direct members of the community of all ages in mini-cabarets.

title: Professional Internship
description: Internship in Theatre and/or the Entertainment industry fulfilled in cooperation with an approved organization.  The student will participate in formal training and directed activities in Theatre, Television, Film, Music or other Industry related fields.

title: Special Studies in Theater Arts for Majors
description: Advanced studies in special theater topics. May be repeated for up to six hours credit.

title: Research
description: Undergraduate research undertaken with the supervision of a faculty member.  May be taken for a maximum of 6 hours.

title: Production Capstone
description: Advanced practicum in production performance design technical theater or management. May be repeated with different topic not to exceed six credit hours.
